title: Project for the Ruin of the Turkish Pirates of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salé
description: A printed Dutch petition and project, addressed to the States General ("Uw Hoog Mogende"), proposing the chartering of a privately funded joint-stock shipping venture ("Rederye") to destroy the Barbary corsairs ("Turkze Rovers") of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli, and Salé, to free enslaved captives, and to secure Dutch navigation and commerce. Merchants who have suffered some thirty years of losses request an octroi to fit out eight frigates to cruise the Strait of Gibraltar and Mediterranean. The scheme is capitalised at one million guilders in ten thousand transferable shares of five hundred guilders each, governed by directors and principal shareholders, with prize money, pensions, and Exchange Bank payments. The document cites a placard of 1705, after which it must have been printed; it was later bound into a Bubble-era compilation around 1720.

pages.description: Title page, set within ruled borders. The heading "PROJECT tot het ruïneeren der TURKZE ROVERS van Algiers, Tunis, Tripoly en Zalée" announces a plan to suppress the corsairs, advance navigation and commerce, free enslaved "ongelukkige en onmachtige Menschen," and recoup damages to the Republic. Page 1 of 8.

Opening of the petition, marked "(2)," a single justified text block. Merchants trading to these lands address the States General, recalling thirty consecutive years of injury from the Barbary corsairs and seeking an octroi to establish a Rederye and equip ships. Page 2 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(3)." Details the fitting-out of armed ships through the Admiralty, their cannon, ballast, and crews, the division of prizes taken from corsairs, the ransoming and freeing of slaves, pay for officers and sailors, and convoy protection. Page 3 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(4)." Describes benefits to the State and to poor seamen: pensions for the aged and disabled, provision for sailors dying intestate at sea, and contributions from ships sailing past Cape Finisterre into the Bay of Biscay, Spain, Portugal, and the Mediterranean, against a fixed premium of five thousand guilders. Page 4 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(5)." Sets the premium for protected ships, terms for officers and crew, and provisions should war with a Christian prince void the cited placard of 1705. States that the venture will form a first fund and raise a capital of one million. Page 5 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(6)." Specifies the capital: ten thousand portions of five hundred guilders each, called in twenty-percent instalments, transferable like Zeeland obligations without transport. Eight equipped frigates are to cruise the Strait and Mediterranean toward Port Mahon, freeing ships and guarding the Portugal and France routes. Page 6 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(7)." Outlines governance: the octroi without monopoly, election of directors by principal shareholders each holding thirty portions, payment of shares through the Exchange Bank (Wisselbank), and the requirement of two directors' signatures. Page 7 of 8.

Closing text, page "(8)," with administration and dividend provisions and an appeal to the States General to grant the project. Signed: "Ondertekent ... Was geteekent, C. Schryver, A. v. Waeyen, Isaac Clifford & Carel C. vande Putten, Jean Fizeaux." Page 8 of 8.

creator: Anonymous
notes: From Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720) or related collection. 8 pages total (goetzmann0020-0027).
transcription:
# Project for the Ruin of the Turkish Pirates of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salé — Transcription

## Original:

(Title page of the pamphlet. Text centered; long-s of the original rendered here as modern "s". Faint bleed-through from adjacent leaves is visible but is not part of this page and is not transcribed. Folio number "53" appears in the upper right corner.)

**PROJECT**

tot het ruineeren der

**TURKZE ROVERS**

van

*Algiers, Tunis, Tripoly en Zalée,*

niet alleen tot het beveyligen, maar het doen aangroejen van de
Navigatie en Commercie deeser Landen;

verder gemelde Mooren in korten tijdt met een weinigh onkosten en
assistentie van den Staat, door de Wapenen tot een bestendige
vreede te dwingen, tot glorie van de Republicq, en gene-
raale welstandt van de Ingezetenen

*Als mede,*

alle ongelukkige en onmachtige Menschen in slavernye verval-
len, en nogh te geraaken, daar uyt te verlossen,

en voor haar veel en achtereen volgende jaaren gedaane diensten in de
Republicq, by ouderdom en onbequaamheit, onderhoudt te doen
hebben, om daar door abondantie van Zeevarende Manschap
in de Republicq te trekken, daar voor 't tegenwoor-
digh gebreck aan is.

---

## English Translation:

**PROJECT**

for the ruin of the

**TURKISH PIRATES**

of

*Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salé,*

not only for the safeguarding, but for the promoting of the growth of the
Navigation and Commerce of these Lands;

further, to force the aforesaid Moors in a short time, with little expense and
assistance from the State, by force of Arms to a lasting
peace, to the glory of the Republic and the general
welfare of its Inhabitants;

*As also,*

to deliver all the unfortunate and helpless People who have fallen into
slavery, and who may yet fall into it, out of that condition;

and, for the many years of services they have successively rendered to the
Republic, to provide maintenance for them in their old age and incapacity,
so as thereby to draw an abundance of Seafaring Men
into the Republic, of which there is at present
a shortage.

title: John Law and South Sea Bubble Satirical Playing Cards (Deck of 51), 1720
description: A near-complete pack of fifty-one satirical playing cards, each engraving lampooning a different scheme from the speculative manias of 1720 — the South Sea Bubble in England and John Law's Mississippi system in France — with a mocking verse beneath. Far from neutral game pieces, the cards cast speculative ventures as ruinous follies, picturing the human wreckage of the crash: bankruptcy, broken families, and want. Published in London at the height of the bubble year, the deck turns a household object into a vehicle of financial satire and moral warning. It captures an early and enduring tension in commercial society — the new machinery of joint-stock finance and public credit promised growth, yet visited upheaval and ruin on ordinary investors — a disquiet that Londoners voiced through pamphlets, prints, and, here, a deck of cards.

pages.description: The "Temple Mills" card (King of Diamonds, inset upper-left): workers at a water mill with figures hauling sacks, beneath the verse "By these Old Mills, Strange Wonders have been done / Numbers have Suffer'd, yet they Still Work on; / Then tell us which have done the Greater Ills, / The Temple Lawyers, or the Temple Mills." — a sardonic equation of the lawyers of London's Temple with a fraudulent scheme promoting the Temple Mills site as an investment. Page 1 of 51.

Nine of Spades, titled "Puckle's Machine." Two inset diagrams show "Round Bullets against Christians" and "Square Bullets against Turks." The engraving depicts a tripod-mounted revolving cannon firing on fleeing, falling men. The verse mocks it as "A rare invention to Destroy the Crowd / Of Fools at Home instead of Foes Abroad," satirising Puckle's gun as a fraudulent investment scheme of the South Sea Bubble era.

King of Diamonds (crowned, sword-bearing figure in the upper-left inset). Engraved street scene titled "An inoffensive way of emptying Houses of Office": labourers cart off barrels of night-soil as onlookers watch from windows. The verse mocks a project to deodorize human waste: "Our fragrant Bubble, would the World believe it, / Is to make Humane Dung, smell sweet as Civet." Satirises a South Sea-era bubble company promising to turn excrement into rich perfume.

Queen of Spades (queen pip, spade in top-left inset). Titled "Bastard Children," the engraving shows a man at an arched doorway handing over a swaddled infant to a nurse while women with babies and baskets crowd a city street. The verse jeers: "A Child is safer than venereal Flames... We'll keep your Bastards at a small expence," satirising a foundling-hospital subscription scheme for unwanted children.

King of Spades, titled "Raddish Oil." The engraving shows labourers harvesting and pressing radishes in a field beside a press-house, with a man wheeling a barrow in the foreground and a town beyond. The verse mocks a fraudulent stock scheme: "Our Oily project, with the Gaping Town, / Will surely for a time go smoothly down... / To Bite Change Alley is not Fraud but Wit." Satirises a bubble-era radish-oil joint-stock project of the 1720 South Sea speculation.

King of Hearts, titled "Whale Fishery." The engraving shows a ship and rowing boats hunting a whale amid icebergs in arctic seas. The verse mocks how "Whale Fishing, which was once a gainfull Trade, Is now by cunning Heads, a Bubble made," satirising a fraudulent whale-fishery share scheme floated during the 1720 South Sea Bubble.

King of Hearts. Top inset titled "Cureing Tobacco for Snuff." The engraved scene shows an overseer directing two enslaved labourers sifting tobacco through a riddle (sieve) before a hill and distant town. Verse: "Here Slaves for Snuff, are Sifting Indian Weed... To shew their Blindness that Espouse the Bubble." Satirises a tobacco/snuff stock-jobbing bubble of the 1720 South Sea era.

Knave of Hearts (red heart pip, male court figure in the inset). Titled "Holy Island - Salt", the engraving shows labourers by mounds of mud, one scattering salt, another digging; verse reads "Here by mixt Elements of Earth and Water, They make a Mud, that turns to Salt hereafter" and mocks "Change Dealers" and "Salt Sellers". Satirises a Holy Island salt-making speculative scheme.

Queen of Diamonds (red diamond pip and crowned queen in upper-left inset). Titled "Furnishing of Funerals to all parts of Great Britain," the engraving shows a black-clad funeral procession bearing a coffin toward a country church. The verse jeers, "Come all ye Sickly Mortals Die apace," mocking a speculative funeral-furnishing company among the bubble schemes of 1720.

Playing card, Jack (Knave) of Diamonds, shown in the upper-left inset. Titled "Corral Fishery," the engraving depicts a moored ship and figures gathering coral along a rocky shore. The verse reads, "Corral that Beautious product only found / Beneath the Water, and above the Ground... A Neptunes Pallace for a British King." Satirises a speculative coral-fishery bubble company of 1720.

Playing card, King of Clubs (crowned king inset top-left). Engraving titled "Irish Sail Cloath": workmen stretch and measure cloth before a large loom in a workshop with tall windows. Verse: "If Good St. Patricks Friends should raise a Stock... No longer be a Bubble, but a Bull." Satirises the South Sea Bubble-era Irish sailcloth (linen) manufacturing project.

Queen of Clubs (pip and crowned queen in upper-left inset). Engraved harbour scene: men loading casks onto a moored ship while two figures sit at a table counting coins by a quayside house. Titled "Lending Money upon Bottom-Ree." Verse: "Some lend their Money for the sake of More... Both these do oft Engage in Bottom Ree." Satirises bottomry, lending money on a ship's hull as maritime loan speculation.

Playing card, King of Clubs (inset top-left). Titled "The Freeholder." An engraved interior shows a line of well-dressed gentlemen queuing at a counter where a seated clerk receives money. The verse begins "Come all ye Spendthrift Prodigals, that hold Free Land and want to turn the Same to Gold" and ends "To Drown your Purchase Money in South Sea." Satirises landowners selling estates to gamble in the South Sea Bubble.

Playing card, suit of spades (pip top-left, rank [illegible]). Titled "River Douglas." Engraved scene shows labourers digging and cutting through rocky ground to make a waterway navigable, with a town beyond. Verse: "Since Bubbles came in vogue, new Arts are found, / To cut thro' Rocks, and level rising Ground." Satirises the River Douglas navigation bubble scheme.

Two of Spades (two pip insets, upper left). Titled "Grand Fishery," the engraving shows a three-masted ship at anchor in a harbour with small rowing boats and figures hauling nets in the foreground, a fort on the shore at left. The verse mocks "Well might this Bubble claim the Style of Grand" and "They've nothing else to cry but Stinking Fish," satirising a fishery joint-stock bubble company of the 1720 South Sea bubble era.

Playing card, three of spades (three pips in the upper-left inset), titled "Cleaning the Streets." The engraving shows a street-sweeping cart with a man astride it, a woman watching from a window, and a child and gentlemen with bags by a cobbled street. The verse mocks the scheme: "A Cleanly project, well approv'd, no doubt... This Bubble well deserves the Name of best, Because the Cleanest Bite of all the rest." It satirises a bubble company floated to clean London's streets.

Six of Spades, titled "Fish Pool." The engraving shows a large sailing ship beside a great whale, with men in a small boat and others hauling nets in the sea. The verse mocks a fishing-pool stock scheme: "now we're bubbl'd by his Fishing Pools, / And as the Men catch Fish the Fish catch Fools." Satirises the Fish-Pool bubble company of the 1720 South Sea mania.

Five of spades, titled "York Buildings." The engraving shows a riverside waterworks with a large waterwheel and pump-tower beside the Thames, with collapsed timber and ruined buildings; townsfolk gesture in dismay. The verse jeers, "You that are blest with Wealth... And want to drown your Money in Thames Water, / Buy but York Buildings... Will sink more Pence, than any Fool can spare." Satirises the York Buildings Company waterworks share scheme of the 1720 Bubble.

Playing card, six of spades (six spade pips in the upper-left inset). Titled "Insurance on Lives," the engraving shows clients and a clerk at a desk inside a busy office where a couple petitions an agent. The verse runs: "Come all ye Gen'rous Husbands, with your Wives, / Insure round Sums, on your precarious Lives... Your Widows may be Rich when you're forgotten." Satirises the speculative life-insurance schemes of the South Sea Bubble era.

Playing card, seven of spades (seven leaf-shaped pips in the corner inset). Titled "Stockings." Engraved scene of women spinning at a wheel and knitting stockings outside a cottage while a child reaches up. The verse warns, "Deal not in Stockin' Shares, because I doubt / Those that buy most, e'erlong will go without," satirising speculation in stocking-knitting (hosiery) joint-stock shares during the South Sea bubble era.

Seven of Spades, titled "Welch Copper." Engraved scene of men gathered on a hill, a goat being led, and a rider on horseback bearing a banner reading "Scire Facias." Verse: "This Bubble for a time may currant pass, / Coppers the title but 'twill end in Brass." Satirises a fraudulent Welsh copper-mining stock scheme of the 1720 Bubble era.

Ten of Spades (ten pips in the upper-left inset). Titled "Providing for and employing all the Poor in Gr. Britain," the engraving shows labourers at a workhouse: people spinning at a wheel, carrying baskets, and being put to industry beside a building. The verse runs "The Poor when manag'd, and employ'd in Trade, / Are to the publick Welfare, usefull made," warning idleness breeds "Whores for the Stews, and Soldiers for the King." Satirises a projected scheme to employ Britain's poor.

Playing card, Heart (rank pip not numbered in inset). Titled "Hemp & Flax", the engraving shows two countrymen sowing seed across a furrowed field, with cottages, a hill, and birds beyond. The verse reads: "Here Hemp is Sow'd for Stubborn Rogues to Die in... The Knaves may prosper but the Fools are cheated." A satire on a hemp-and-flax cultivation project floated during the South Sea Bubble era.

Two of Hearts. Titled "Manuring of Land," the engraving shows two labourers in a field by a large tree—one swinging a mattock, the other spreading dung—with grazing horses behind. The verse mocks a "Tricking Bubble": "A Noble Undertaking but abus'd... They'll never make Corn Cheap or Horse-Dung Dear." Satirises a South Sea Bubble-era land-improvement (manuring) stock scheme.

Three of Hearts (three red heart pips in the inset). Titled "Coal Trade from Newcastle," the engraving shows a horse-driven coal whim with labourers turning a capstan, a basket on a rope, and laden ships at sea. The verse jests "Some deal in Water, some in Wind like Fools... but we alone in Coals," warning the declining nation may "fear a fatal inflamation" — satirising the speculative bubble projects of 1720, here the coal-trade scheme.

Four of hearts. Titled "Water Engine," the engraving shows a labourer working a pump beside a flooded, fenced field with a village and hills behind. The verse hawks shares: "Come all ye Culls, my Water Engine buy, To Pump your flooded Mines, and Cole Pits dry; Some Projects are all Wind, but ours is Water." Satirises a South Sea Bubble-era water-engine mining-drainage stock scheme.

Six of hearts (six red pips, upper-left inset). Titled "Royal Fishery of Great Britain." Engraving shows a shore fishing scene: men hauling nets and baskets beside beached and rigged sailing vessels, ships at sea, hills behind. Verse mocks the venture: "with the Stile of Royal Gild their Baits... Is to catch Fools, the only Chubs they Fish for." Satirises the Royal Fishery joint-stock bubble scheme of 1720.

Seven of Hearts. Engraving titled "Erecting Houses of Office in N. Britain for Strangers & Travellors," showing privies (outhouses) along a road, a man at a doorway, and figures relieving themselves. Verse: "A usefull Project merrily advanc'd... Design'd to Sweeten the North British Nation, And put Close Stools, and Bed Pans, out of fashion." Satirises a frivolous bubble-era joint-stock scheme for building public toilets in Scotland.

Seven of hearts. Titled "Building Ships to let to Freight," the engraving shows a dockyard with vessels under construction along a harbour wall, workmen and merchants among barrels in the foreground. The verse mocks speculative folly: "Who but a Nest of Block-heads to their cost, / Would build new Ships for Freight, when Trade is lost." Satirises the South Sea Bubble era's fraudulent shipping/freight ventures.

Seven of Hearts. Titled "Drying Malt by the Air," the engraving shows a man leaning from an upper window of a brick malthouse, pointing toward a walled yard and field. The verse jeers, "Of all the Windy Projects now in vogue... The Malting Bubble, seems to be most fair." It satirises a South Sea-era malt-drying joint-stock scheme as a worthless speculative bubble.

Nine of hearts (nine red pips in the upper-left inset). Titled "English Copper & Brass," the engraving shows workmen at a smelting furnace and forge, one blowing the fire, another working a screw-press; a scroll on the floor reads "Scire Facias." The verse mocks the speculator: "The Headlong Fool that wants to be a Swopper, / Of Gold and Silver Coin for English Copper... / And give Rich Mettle, for Adult'rate Brass." Satirises South Sea era copper/brass-manufacturing stock schemes.

Ten of Hearts. Titled "Exporting Timber from Germany," the engraving shows a harbour where workmen labour beside stacked timber and a crane, with a moored ship and town beyond. The verse warns: "You that are Rich, and hasty to be Poor, / Buy Timber Export from the German Shoar; / For Gallouses, built up of Forreign Wood, / If rightly us'd, may do Change Alley good." It satirises the South Sea Bubble speculators of Exchange (Change) Alley, jesting that imported timber would furnish gallows for the ruined.

Diamonds (red pip, top-left); rank not separately marked. Titled "Sr Jn L——ts Improvement of Land," the engraving shows men holding a banner reading "an Act against Bubbles" above robed figures laying bricks and digging on a walled plot. Verse: "The Famous Knight that is the Sole Projector / Of this New Bubble is a South Director." Satirises a land-improvement bubble scheme tied to a South Sea director amid the 1720 bubble mania.

Two of diamonds, titled "Greenland Trade." The engraving shows men beside a large try-pot rendering oil from a beached whale, with a sailing vessel offshore. The verse reads: "This Project was to Catch to Cut and Boil / Huge Whales... A Stinking Bubble, tho' of late so Dear, / Yet now the Greatest Sharers stink for Fear." Satirises the South Sea-era Greenland whale-fishery stock bubble.

Five of diamonds (five red pips in upper-left inset). Titled "Insurance on Horses," the engraving shows men gathered around a dead horse lying on the ground while another holds a live mount. The verse runs: "You that keep Horses to preserve your Ease... Insure their Lives, and if they Die we'll make Full Satisfaction, or be bound to Break." Satirises the speculative insurance schemes of the South Sea Bubble era.

Seven of diamonds, titled "Bahama Islands." The engraving shows a crowd of emigrants on a shore boarding a ship's boat, helped aboard by sailors. The verse mocks the scheme: "Rare Fruitfull Isles, where not an Ass can find / A verdent Tuft, or Thistle, to his Mind!"—satirising the South Sea Bubble-era subscription to colonise the barren Bahama Islands.

Seven of Diamonds (red pips, upper-left inset). Titled "Insurance on Ships &c." An engraved three-masted sailing ship rides choppy seas with figures aboard and a crowded longboat alongside. The verse mocks marine-insurance schemes: "In vain are all Insurances, for still / The Raging Winds must answer Heaven's Will," since "some must Loose what e'er the Sea Devour."

Seven of Diamonds, titled "Rock Salt." Workers hack at jagged rock formations, fill barrels and a sieve-like trough, and kneel sifting salt into baskets. The verse urges: "You that are willing to Preserve your Meat... Encourage this Salt Project, and your Coin / Will turn to some Account, at least to Brine." Satirises a speculative rock-salt mining venture.

Eight of Diamonds (eight red diamond pips inset top-left). Titled "Settling Collonies in Accadia North America," the engraving shows speculators on a riverbank handling papers and money, a tilled field with figures and natives behind. The verse mocks subscribers: "He that is Rich, and wants to Fool away / A Sporting Sum, in North America... And Asses Ears, shall Honour him or Bearer." Satirises colonial land/colony-settling investment schemes of the South Sea Bubble era.

Nine of Diamonds, titled "Pensilvania Company." Engraved interior shows men queuing at a counter where a seated clerk and a tall Quaker in a pointed hat transact business. The verse jeers: "Come all ye Saints that would for little Buy, Great Tracts of Land... Deal with your Quaking Friends, they're Men of Light, The Spirit hates Deceit and Scorns to Bite." Satirises the Pennsylvania land-company speculation of the 1720 bubble era, mocking Quaker promoters.

Ten of diamonds (ten red pips, upper left). Engraving titled "Purchaseing of Estates illegally detain'd": seated clerks at a chest paying out coins to a man, while a crowd of gentlemen queues at right. Verse: "You that have Dormant Titles to Estates... Sell them to us we'll gratify your Spite, And Plague the Rogues that Rob you of your Right." Satirises a speculative scheme trafficking in disputed estate titles and dormant claims.

Satirical playing card of the club suit (single club pip inset, top-left). Titled "Lute-String," it engraves a warehouse interior where crafty managers in coats handle stacked boxes and bales of lutestring silk amid a crowd. The verse runs: "These Crafty Managers have play'd for Years... They first made Lutestrings, but they now make Fools," satirising the fraudulent Royal Lustring (lutestring) silk-weaving company and its share-bubble managers.

Two of Clubs (two club pips, upper-left inset). Engraving titled "Past-board Manufactory": two workmen at benches handling large sheets of pasteboard in a workshop. The verse reads, "As Empty Sayings flow from Windy Fools, / So Pastboard Bubbles rise from Paper Skulls" — satirising the worthless paper bubble schemes of the 1720 South Sea financial mania.

Playing card, four of clubs (four club pips inset top-left). Titled "Trade to Harborough," the engraving shows a three-masted merchant ship at a quayside warehouse, with figures hauling and weighing bales of cloth. The verse mocks consumers of imported textiles: "You that delight to take up foreign Linnen, At Harb'rough made, a little Town in Bremen... And like Kind Fools, neglect your own at home." Satirises a fraudulent foreign-linen trading scheme.

Five of clubs (pips inset top-left), titled "Salt-Petre." Engraved scene of men digging and gathering saltpetre near a large cask and cannon-wheeled cart, a cottage behind. The verse beneath reads: "Come all ye Black Infernal Powder Makers... Buy Petre Stock... 'twill make you (tho' not Richer) much y[e] Wiser." Satirizes a bubble-era saltpetre / gunpowder stock scheme.

Seven of clubs (seven pips, top-left inset), titled "Sugar." Engraving of a sugar refinery: a near-naked worker stirs a boiling vat over a furnace while another tends conical sugar moulds along the wall. Verse: "Fair Tattling Gossips, you that Love to see / Fine Sugar blended, with Expensive Tea... Buy Sugar-Shares, and you'll be Sweetly Bit." Satirises speculation in sugar-company shares during the South Sea bubble.

Eight of Clubs (eight pip insets, upper left). Titled "Fatning of Hoggs", the engraving shows pigs feeding at a sty trough beside a barn, with men slaughtering a hog in the foreground. The verse begins "Come all ye Bacon making Greasy Rogues, That want Good Nurses for your Meagre Hoggs," promising to fatten swine "with Offal, Blood, and Grains." Satirises a hog-fattening projecting scheme.

Eight of Clubs. Titled "Rose Insurance from Fire," the engraving shows firefighters playing a hose on a burning building while figures fan flames below, mocking the fire-insurance bubble company. The verse reads, "Projecting sure must be a Gainfull Trade, / Since all the Elements are Bubbles made," satirising speculative insurance schemes of the 1720 bubble year.

Nine of clubs, titled "Buying Seamen's Tickets." The engraved scene shows clerks at a counter buying up sailors' pay-tickets while men crowd around; one figure gestures with a cane. The verse runs: "As the Case stands the Wapping Ale Wives buy / The Seamen's-Tickets for a small Supply," mocking the discounted trade in seamen's wage-tickets and the cheating of spendthrift sailors.

Ten of clubs. Engraved view of Liverpool titled "Liverpool Fresh-water": water-carriers fill buckets at a spring while horse-drawn carts haul casks toward a church-topped town. Verse: "This Town does to our Western Islands deal, And serves 'em with Malt Liquors, & with Meal... Can People Brew Fine Drink ye want Fresh Water." Depicts Liverpool's trade and water supply.

Eight of clubs (pip inset, top left), titled "Bleeching of Hair". Engraving shows workers tending a steaming vat by a shed while a man at right holds up bleached hairpieces, more hung on a rail behind. Verse: "Here Dirty Brown, Dark, Red, and Yellow Hair / Are Bleach'd to Colours that are Fine and Fair... half the Whores in Town / Contribute to adorn one Addl'd Crown." Satirises the trade in bleached hair and wigmaking vanity.

# Transcription

**Title (top of card):**
Temple Mills

**Caption (bottom of card):**
By these Old Mills, Strange Wonders have been done
Numbers have Suffer'd, yet they still Work on;
Then tell us which have done the Greater Ill,
the Temple Lawyers, or the Temple Mill.

**Notable markings:**
- Portrait of a playing card figure (King of Diamonds) in upper left corner, wearing ornate period clothing with a red diamond symbol
- Engraved illustration showing Temple Mills with brick buildings, a water wheel on the right, figures in 18th-century dress in the foreground, and architectural structures in the background
- This is identified as part of a set of South Sea Bubble satirical playing cards from Great Britain

# Transcription

**Title (top):** Puckle's Machine

**Text on wheels (top right):**
- Left wheel: Round Bullets [illegible] [illegible]
- Right wheel: Square Bullets [illegible] [illegible]

**Main caption (bottom):**
A rare invention to Destroy the Crowd,
Of Fools at Home instead of Foes Abroad.
Fear not my Friend, this terrible Machine,
They're only Wounded that have Share therein.

**Card designation (top left):** Eight of Diamonds (spade symbols visible)

**Notable markings:**
- Playing card suit symbols (spades/diamonds) in top left corner

- Building structure visible in background
- This is part of a South Sea Bubble satirical playing card series

# Transcription

**Title (top of image):**
An inoffensive way of emptying Houses of Office

**Caption (bottom of image):**
Our fragrant Bubble, would the World believe it,
Is to make Humane Dung, Smell Sweet as Violet:
None Sure before us, ever durst prefume,
to turn a't—d, into a Rich Perfume.

**Notable markings:**
- Decorative border frame around entire image
- Small spade symbol (♠) in top left corner near crowned royal figure
- Engraved illustration in woodcut/etching style
- Period spelling conventions preserved (e.g., "prefume" for "perfume," "durst" for "dared," "a't—d" for censored word)

# Transcription

**Title:** Baftard Children

**Text at bottom:**

Love on ye jolly Rakers, and burcome Dames, —
A Child is safer than venereal flames ;
Indulge your Senses with the Sweet offence, —
We'll keep your Baftard at a Small expence .

**Notable markings:**

- Suit symbol (Spade) visible in top left corner
- Decorative illustration showing a woman in a doorway (top left) and multiple figures in period dress in an urban courtyard setting (center/bottom)
- Brick buildings depicted in background

# Transcription

**Title:** Raddith Oil

**Text at bottom:**

Our Oily project with the Caping Town,
Will Surely for a time go Smoothly down,
We Sonr and Presf. to carry on the Cheat,
to Bite Change Alley is not Fraud but Wit.

**Notable markings:**

# Transcription

**Title:** Whale Filthery

Whale Fishing, which was once a gainfull Trade,
Is now by cunning Heads, a Bubble made;
For round the Change they only Spread their Sails,
And to catch Gudgeons, bait their Hooks with Whales.

# Transcription

Cureing Tobacco for Snuff

Here Slaves for Snuff, are sitting Indian Weed,
Whilst their O'erseer, does the Riddle feed.
The Dust arising, gives their Eyes much trouble,
to shew their Blindness that Espouse the Bubble.

Here by mixt Elements of Earth and Water,
They make a Mud, that turns to Salt hereafter;
To help the Project on among Change Dealers!
May all bad Wives like Lot's become Salt Pillars!
Since crowds of Fools delight to be Salt Sellers.

title: Pasquin's Windkaart: Dutch Satirical Playing Cards on the 1720 Windhandel (Deck of 55)
description: A set of fifty-five satirical playing cards — Pasquin's Windkaart ("Pasquin's Wind-Card"), from the Dutch compilation Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid — mocking the windhandel, the speculative "wind trade" in company shares that gripped the Dutch Republic in 1720. Each card pairs an engraved figure with a Dutch verse, caricaturing the speculators and projectors of the bubble: the King of Spades, for instance, shows a periwigged gentleman holding a balance in which a spade and a sheet of paper outweigh a purse of gold — paper wealth absurdly heavier than real coin. As an object of communal play, the deck shows that financial satire was not confined to the elite forms of pamphlet and essay; it travelled through cheap everyday print and the sociable spaces — taverns, coffee-houses, parlours — where people handled cards and traded opinions. The quotidian playing card thus became a carrier of social criticism.

pages.description: The "Heer van Schoppen" (King of Spades): a periwigged gentleman holding a balance in which a spade and a sheet of paper outweigh a bag of gold, beneath the verse "De schop, en 't blad papier in de eene schaal gelegen, / Kan ... een goud beurs overwegen" — paper shares weighed absurdly heavier than real coin. Page 1 of 55.

Queen of Spades ("Vrouw", spade pip upper right). A finely dressed woman rests one hand on a spade thrust into the soil while tossing away playing cards with the other. The Dutch verse reads "Men zet de schop vry neêr, ons spelen is verbruid... De blinde razernij van Quinquampoix heeft uit" - the spade is set down, our gambling spoiled, the blind frenzy of Quincampoix is over. Satirises the 1720 stock-jobbing mania (rue Quincampoix, Paris).

Knegt (Jack) of Spades, with spade pip inset. A drummer beats his drum as papers fly from the pack on his back; share-fools straggle off in the distance. Inscribed "Prins Fredriks mars, naar Vianen." The verse beats "prins Fredriks mars" to march "de Actie gekken" like "wind zoldaten" to Vianen, satirising the 1720 share-trading mania and bankrupts fleeing to the debtors' refuge of Vianen.

King of Hearts (inset heart marked "Heer"). A periwigged gentleman in coat and tricorne stands holding a small windmill emblem, labelled "Lauwer Koning" (Laurel King); behind him figures dig and toil on a barren slope. The verse reads "'k Heb de Actiës eerst bedagt, maar een kwade Actiekans / Smyt al myn lof omver, en schent myn lauwer krans" — satirising the 1720 share-trading (Actie) speculation mania of the Bubble year.

Queen of Hearts ("Vrouw"), heart pip top-right. An elegantly gowned woman holds an engraving of a distilling still labelled with shares ("De Actie ketel"). The verse claims the share-kettle distilled "dat het papier in Goud verkeerd" (turns paper into gold), and that the schemer "Stookt goud uit klad papier, en maakt 't Heel-Al vol zotten." Satirises the 1720 stock-share (windhandel/Bubble) mania.

Knave (Jack) of Hearts, labelled "Knegt of Boef" (Knave or Rogue). The engraving shows a man in coat and breeches, shoulder-knife raised, plunging a blade into a large heart on his chest — "Uit Wanhoop" (out of despair). The Dutch verse describes a roguish heart that, led by a "reeks van Actiestreken" (string of share-dealing tricks) to seek profit, is now stabbed through with spite — satirising the 1720 stock-jobbing bubble (Bubblers / Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid).

King of Diamonds (two diamond pips, inset "Heer"). Engraving of a periwigged gentleman in tricorn hat and long coat standing in a doorway, one hand outstretched. Motto: "Ik ben beschermd, Daar ieder kermd" (I am protected, where all lament). Verse mocks shelter from the "Zuid en West hun windgeblaas"—the South Sea and West India bubble companies' wind-trade speculation of 1720.

Playing card, Queen ("Vrouw") of Diamonds (red diamond pip, top right). A finely dressed woman blows through a long pipe into a glass-furnace, forming a fragile glass bubble. Dutch verse: "Door sterk te blázen breekt het glas, / Net als 't met de Actie-bubbels was" — likening the bursting glass to the collapse of the share ("Actie") bubbles of the 1720 speculation mania.

Knave of Diamonds (titled "Knegt," diamond pip upper right). A pipe-smoking man in coat and breeches lights a paper share certificate with a glass under a blazing sun, the smoke verse reading "'t Papier zo duur / Werd rook door 't vuur" (the paper so dear became smoke by the fire). Lower text: "Mijn Actiebrief verteerd..." mocking the 1720 share-trading bubble (Bubbelvlagen).

King (Heer) of clubs. A finely dressed company director clutches papers, an empty sheet slipping from one hand; a monkey beside a funnel-topped pedestal empties coin. Motto: "Eerst gelukkig, Nu drukkig" (first fortunate, now troubled). Verse: "'k Ben Directeur geweest... Het geld droop door de zak" — money slipped through the bag. Satirizes the 1720 share-speculation (windhandel) bubble and its ruined directors.

Queen of Clubs ("Vrouw," klaveren pip top right). An elegantly dressed woman stands holding flowers and a basket; below her a falling Icarus-like figure tumbles toward the sea. The verse links her ruined husband to mythic over-reachers: "Met Ikarus en Faëton verdwijnt mijn man zijn Actie zon" (with Icarus and Phaeton my husband's share-sun vanishes). From a satirical card set mocking the 1720 stock-speculation bubble (windhandel/Actie mania).

Dutch playing card, Jack of Clubs ("Knegt", klaveren pip top-right). A man in a fur coat stands amid broken shackles, ships behind, a discarded lord's coat and hat on a chair. Motto: "'t Geluk brengt my / Uit slaverny." Verse: "Mijn Actie staat heeft mij van knegt tot Heer gemaakt." Satirises the 1720 share-speculation bubble.

Playing card, Ace of Spades (single spade pip, numbered "1"). A hatted street-sweeper shovels and sweeps refuse beside a broom and wheelbarrow, scattered papers at his feet, houses behind. Dutch motto: "Men maakt zig 't vulnis kwyt" — and verse urging "Weg met dit klad papier" (away with this worthless scrip). Satirises the 1720 stock-bubble paper (Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid) as rubbish swept from the street.

Two of Spades (spade pip and numeral "2", top right). A winged, bearded old man—Father Time—stands holding a smoking brazier and an earthen urn, against a barren landscape. The verse reads "Ik ben 't die 't al ondekt, en 't klad papier verbrand... en help den Koopman weer in stand," and the top motto "Van al de Compagnien." Satirises the 1720 share-company bubble, with Time exposing the folly and burning the worthless paper.

Playing card, 3 of Spades (spade pip and "3" at upper right). Three richly dressed women holding distaffs ride aloft balanced on a shovel against the clouds. Dutch verse: "Zie hoe de Zuid, en West, en Missisippi zweven / Op eene schop, wijl zij van lugt en winden leven" — See how South, West and Mississippi float on a shovel, since they live on air and wind. Satirises the South Sea, West India and Mississippi share-bubble speculations of 1720.

Four of Spades (spade pip and numeral "4" inset, top right). A standing gentleman in coat and breeches dangles a small dog by a whip-leash while another dog tumbles on its back below. The Dutch verse mocks: "Hoe schreeuwd de Zuidze-dog zo schrikkelijk" — how dreadfully the South Sea dog howls. Satirises the 1720 South Sea Company stock bubble.

Playing card, five of spades ("5" with spade pip, top right). A man in a long coat holds out a balance bearing the Amsterdam arms (shield with three St. Andrew's crosses, "XXX"); a trumpeting putto flies overhead. Verse: "Zie hoe de vijfde stad van Holland, de Acties plet" — the city weighs/crushes the shares. Satirises the 1720 Dutch "actien" (share) speculation mania.

Playing card, six of spades (pip and "6" inset, upper right). Engraved scene of a shabby, torn-coated speculator standing by a battered chest, brandishing papers. Top motto: "Een Sesje min of meer / Scheeld weinig aan myn eer." Lower verse ends "...maar myne eer, die is verzonken" (my honor is sunk). Satirises the 1720 share-speculation mania.

Seven of spades (pip and numeral 7, top right). A long-coated gentleman stands at a small table strewn with coins, dealing them out, papers in hand and underfoot. Dutch label "Drie en vier Betaald alhier" (three and four paid here); verse below ends "De drie en vier betaald in Quinquempoix 't gelag." Satirises the 1720 stock-jobbing speculation at Quincampoix, the Paris street of John Law's Mississippi bubble.

Eight of Spades (pip "8" upper right, spade emblem). A finely dressed man holds a paper bearing a white falcon in one hand and a lit lantern atop a tall stool in the other. Motto: "Diogenes lantaarn heb ik bij dag van noden... 'k Vong de agtings Witte Valk die and'ren is ontvloden" — using Diogenes' lantern by day to seek the rare white falcon of esteem. Satirises the vain, fruitless speculation of the 1720 share-trading bubbles.

Nine of Spades ("9" with spade pip, top right). A fool in nightcap holds a mirror in one hand and an unrolled print of a crowded scene in the other; a digging spade stands beside him. The Dutch verse runs "De dwaasheid dogt de kunst te delven in het graf, / Maar vrouw Voorzigtigheid keerd deze rampen af" (Folly thought to bury art in the grave, but Lady Prudence wards off these disasters). Satirises the 1720 share-trading bubble mania.

Ten of Spades (pip and "10" inset top right). Engraved scene: a ragged, tattered beggar in a broad hat stands in open country, holding up a coin in one hand and carrying a small box or alms-tray in the other. The Dutch verse reads "Qui donne moiun sou, zo zult ge aanstonds aanschouwen / Wat grafsteê, dat de tijd voor de Actieprins zal bouwen," mocking the ruined "Actieprins" (share-prince) of the 1720 stock-jobbing bubble (Bubble cards, Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid).

Ace of Hearts (heart pip, numeral "1", upper right), titled "Eindeloos" (Endless). A speculator with a hooped net and fishing rod tries to land a mermaid who holds a coin and clutches the empty hook. The verse runs "De Zuidzé Zeesieren dagt ik vergeefs te vangen, Zy knapt het geld, en laat de lege hengel hangen"—he fishes in vain for the South Sea siren, who snaps up the money and leaves the line empty. Satirises the South Sea Bubble of 1720.

Two of Hearts (pip "2" with heart inset, upper right). A walking man holds a flaming torch and carries a basket of round objects (eggs or bubbles) on his arm, in a landscape with cottages and a tower. The motto reads "Zy dryven op en onder" (they float up and under); the verse below speaks of hearts bubbling on "verdwynend Bubbel water" (vanishing bubble water). Satirises the 1720 share-trading mania (Actiewerk) and bubble speculation.

Playing card, three of hearts (pip and "3" inset top right). A broad-hatted, cloaked man holds aloft a ring-and-triangle emblem in one hand and a sheet of paper (a share certificate) in the other, standing in a rural landscape with a pollard willow and cottage. Top motto: "Een ryke vrek / Is dubbeld gek" (a rich miser is doubly a fool). Verse below laments that however high his capital rose through the Actien (shares), his greed left him no richer than before. Satirises the 1720 stock-speculation mania.

Four of Hearts. A well-dressed man stands on a shore tossing coins into the sea, where a fish surfaces toward the falling money. The motto reads "'t Gaat alles naar de zé" (it all goes into the sea); the verse below explains he throws his money in as bait for the fishes rather than lose it on the shares ("de Actiens"). Satirises the 1720 share-speculation mania.

Playing card, 5 of Hearts (heart pip and "5" at upper right). A motley-clad jester in cap holds out a five-pointed star and a paper reading "vyf sin te koop" (five senses for sale). The verse: "Ik ben niet gekker als al de and'ren... Ook hun vyf zinnen voor een Actiebrief verkopen"—mocking speculators who sell their wits for a share certificate during the 1720 bubble (Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid).

Playing card, six of hearts (inset "6" with heart pip). A standing man holds a beam-balance with hanging scales while a tethered monkey crouches at left before a rising sun. Motto: "Ryne Evenaar / Toont alles klaar"; verse below warns that grasping too madly at "'t Actie-werk" leads one across the line "naar 't gloeijend Aapenland." Satirises the 1720 share-trading mania (Bubble speculation).

Playing card, 7 of Hearts (seven heart pips at upper left, suit emblem upper right). A robed man—labelled "Astrologist der Windnegotie" (astrologer of the wind-trade)—peers skyward through a cross-staff toward stars, while a dog runs off behind him. The verse reads "Om Actie voordeel kijk ik yv'rig in de starren" (I gaze eagerly at the stars for share-profit). Satirises stock-jobbing speculation of the 1720 windhandel bubble.

Playing card, eight of hearts (pip "8" and heart, top right). A woman stands amid palm trees; a fox digs in an open chest at left while an ape squats at right beside scattered dice. Verse above: "'k leer Vossen en aapen / Om geld te schrapen"; below: "'t Is vrugteloos op winst gedagt, / Daar de Aep en Vos het voordeel wagt." From the Dutch satirical pack on the 1720 Bubble (Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid), mocking greedy, foolish speculation.

Playing card, nine of hearts (pip and "9" at top). Engraved scene: a man sprawls backward, fallen as a bell-rope (koord) snaps from a bell at upper right; a lantern sits beside him, gabled town behind. Motto: "Ai my wat smak." Verse blames toying "met de Actieklok" — satirising the 1720 share-trading (Actie) speculation folly.

Ten of Hearts (inset heart, "10"). A seated youth in a wooded landscape blows a long clay pipe, producing a large bubble, with a bowl of soap-water at his side. Titled "Windbellen om Nul te koop" (wind-bubbles sold for nothing); verse: "'t Is Wind en Nul en anders niet" — wind and naught, nothing else. Satirises the worthless wind-trade of the 1720 stock bubble.

Ace of Diamonds (single diamond pip with "I", top right). Titled "Actie raadzel" (the shares riddle), the engraving shows a muscular figure—Samson—wrenching open the jaws of a lion in a landscape with a tree. The verse reads "Nog blinder was 't geheim van de Acties, dan voorheen 't Diepzinnig raadzel van den dapp'ren Samson scheen" (the secret of the shares was even darker than Samson's deep riddle). Satirises the 1720 stock-share speculation mania.

Playing card, two of diamonds (pip and "2" upper right). Engraving: a bespectacled pedlar in hat and coat hawks spectacles from a tray; a bat flies overhead and an owl perches on a post at left. Top motto reads "Koop brillen voor blinde Actionisten" (Buy spectacles for blind shareholders); verse below mocks share speculators who squander their money. Satirises the 1720 stock-jobbing bubble mania.

Three of Diamonds (pip and "3" at upper right). A man stands holding a baton, his tunic bearing three escutcheons (city arms); behind him a mounted figure and a small Mercury-like figure with caduceus stand on a shore. The Dutch verse reads "Koe dol de wind ook gilden, Hy stuit op deze schilden" and below "Dit trits der steden houd de Koopgod in zijn stand, Omhelsd Neptuin, en stut het vallend Vaderland." Satirizes the 1720 windhandel (wind-trade) share speculation.

Four of Diamonds (red diamond pip and "4" at upper right). A fur-capped man in a coastal landscape holds out a mariner's compass in one hand and grips a furled flag on a staff in the other, ships visible at sea. Top motto: "'k zoek Zuid nog West, Naar 't oosten 't best"; bottom verse laments that the South and West (Sea/India schemes) did so much harm their names should never again stand on the compass. Satirises the 1720 South Sea and Mississippi/West-India speculation bubbles.

Five of diamonds (pip "5" and single diamond inset). Engraving shows a stooped man labelled "Kromme Cinquen" writing at a table over papers, a crippled beggar on crutches behind. Dutch verse: "De kromme Cinquen daar ik mij op durf vertrouwen" — crooked dealings the speaker fears he will yet regret. Satirises the fraudulent share-trading and speculative folly of the 1720 bubble.

Six of Diamonds (pip and numeral "6" upper right). Engraving of a ruined speculator standing in open country, holding a worthless share certificate while empty reins slip from his hand. Caption "Daar vliegt koets en Paarden" and verse "Mijn koets en paarden zijn mij door den wind ontglipt" — his coach and horses lost to the wind, satirising the 1720 share-trading bubble (windhandel).

Seven of Diamonds (pip top right, rank "7"). A finely dressed man stands holding an open ledger or box and a sheet of paper, titled "'t Duister Actieboek" (the obscure share-book). The verse reads "Zo min als ik den loop der zeven hoofd Planeten / Versta; zo min kan ik het eind der Actien weten" — as little as he understands the course of the seven planets, so little can he know the end of the shares. Satirises the 1720 share-trading bubble speculation.

Eight of diamonds (pip: diamond with "8", top right). A finely dressed standing gentleman in a long coat and wig holds a paper in his left hand and a coin or medallion in his right, before a country house and landscape. The verse reads "Mijn Agtbaarheid is met de zuid, Door schraap- en woekerzugt verbruid" ("My respectability is wedded to the South, ruined by greed and usury"), satirising South Sea / 1720 speculation and the avarice of stockjobbers.

Nine of Diamonds (inset pip top-right shows 9 and a diamond). A standing gentleman labelled "Voor den Koning" holds out a sheet of shares in one hand and a hat in the other, a country house behind. The Dutch verse reads "Sta ruim; want zo ik eens mag den Actie-koning raken... met Zuid en West om mijn fortuin te maken" — aspiring to be "Actie-koning" (share king) by speculating in South Sea and West India stock, satirising the 1720 share mania.

Ten of Diamonds (diamond pip and "10" upper right). A ruined investor stands in a barren landscape, holding out an empty sleeve and offering a ring; the motto reads "Wie koopt 'er myn laasten Ring" (Who will buy my last ring). The verse warns "Wie stenen koopt bij nagt laat zig altoos bedotten... Zo ging 't in Quinquempoix ook met al de Actie zotten," satirising the share mania at the rue Quincampoix (Mississippi/Law bubble) that left speculators destitute.

Ace of Clubs (single club pip, marked "1"). Titled "Pelgrim van de waarheid" (Pilgrim of truth), it engraves a robed pilgrim with broad hat, walking staff, and a radiant sun-face emblem on the breast, set in a landscape with a tower. The Dutch verse laments: "Ik zoek naar waarheid, en naar wysheid... ik vind de wereld even dwaas" — mocking the universal folly exposed by the 1720 speculation bubbles.

Two of Clubs. A full-length engraving of a periwigged gentleman in a long coat standing in open countryside, arms spread and palms empty, his pockets turned out; a small devil-like figure lurks at upper left. The Dutch verse reads "Ach! lege handen, kaal van beurs, en dol van hoofd... Heeft menig Actie..." (empty hands, bare purse, mad in the head). It satirises a ruined share speculator of the 1720 stock-jobbing bubble.

Playing card, three of clubs (pip in upper-right corner). Engraving shows a stooped, bespectacled man in a cap, leaning on a crutch and clutching a paper share, a cottage behind him. The Dutch verse — "'k Dagt voor mijn Actie winst een heerlijk heidt te kopen / Maar, ach! zy doen in 't end my op drie beenen lopen" — mocks a ruined 1720 stock (Actie) speculator left to hobble on three legs.

Playing card, 4 of Clubs. An engraved scene shows a fashionably dressed man striding across windswept country, holding a tiny windmill aloft as cherub heads blow gusts from the clouds; a hat tumbles beside him. The Dutch motto reads "Door 't malen / Moet ik 't halen" ("By grinding/milling I must get it"), with verses below on the winds that drive many to ruin. Satirises the speculative "wind trade" (windhandel) of the 1720 stock bubble.

Five of Clubs (pip and "5" upper right). A standing man in 18th-century coat and breeches fans out playing cards in his raised hand, the other pressed to his chest. An inset reads "'t Gaat wel ik mijn het spel"; the lower verse, "'k Heb lanterlu, maar op een valsse wijs gekregen," invokes the card game lanterlu (lanterloo) to satirise the cheating gamble of 1720 bubble speculation.

Playing card, 6 of clubs (pip top-right). A finely dressed gentleman tips a flask to his mouth but spills it, while a small peasant points at him beside a clock-towered church. Dutch verse: "Bombario gij zijt een Guit... 'k Heb dorst naar Actidrank, maar giet myn mond juist mis." Satirises the 1720 stock-share mania, "Actidrank" punning on "actie" (shares).

Seven of Clubs (pip top right). An engraved scene shows a finely dressed man standing on a bluff, holding a sieve through which coins fall to the ground while torn share certificates lie at his feet. The Dutch verse reads "'k Wierd ryk door 't Actiespel, schoon 't was een snode vond" ("I grew rich through the share game, though it was a wicked scheme"), the money sifting away to the ground. Satirises the 1720 stock-speculation mania (windhandel/Actiespel) in which paper-trade fortunes drained to nothing.

Eight of Clubs. A man labeled "Actie-agtig" (share-mad) takes a chisel to a large numeral 8 on a table, cutting it through the middle. The verse reads "Zo 'k de 8 door midden snij, zo hou ik maar twe nullen, Die net zo goed zijn, als de Wind-negoties prullen" — splitting the 8 leaves only two zeros, worthless like the wind-trade trash. Satirizes the 1720 share-speculation bubble.

Nine of Clubs (top-right pip "9" with a single club). A standing gentleman in a long coat holds a blank sheet in one hand and a placard bearing concentric diamonds in the other. Top motto: "'k Hou voor 't dolen / Een oopen molen". Lower verse jeers that whoever holds such a game ("spel") need not fear loss but is assured of winning — satirising the worthless paper shares and lottery folly of the 1720 speculation bubble.

Playing card, ten of clubs (inset "10" with club pip). Engraving shows a standing figure beset by birds perched on head and shoulders, one a large eagle (Adelaar), holding a sheet of paper. Verse: "De windnegotie maakte mij een halve sneês" and "Nu maakt mij de Adelaar en blind, en vol van vrees." Satirises the 1720 wind-trade (windhandel) share-bubble mania.

Title-page card (no suit/rank visible). An engraved banner held by cherubs reads "Pasquins Windkaart op de Windnegotie Van 't Iaar 1720," with a fool's pointed hat and bauble above and faces below. A verse jests "Prins Fredriks mantel is voor al de lauws-gezinden." Frontispiece to a satirical card set mocking the 1720 "wind trade" (Bubble) stock-jobbing folly.

Not a suited playing card but an engraved satirical plate. A large rooster stands in a landscape, holding in its beak a placard ("windkaart") showing grazing sheep. The Dutch caption reads that "Deze nieuwe Windkaarten worden gemaakt en verkogt... by Lautje van Schotten in den geldzoekenden Haan" — these new wind-cards are made and sold at the money-seeking Rooster. It mocks the 1720 Dutch share-trading mania (windhandel).

Not an engraved card but a sheet of decorated paper: a dense, repeating block-printed pattern of concentric rosettes formed from rings of dots in brown, black, green, and cream on a brick-red ground. No suit, rank, scene, motto, or financial subject is present; likely a binding endpaper or cover sheet of the set.

creator: Anonymous
notes: From the Dutch Windkaarten (Wind Cards) satirical playing card deck, ca. 1720 (Netherlands)
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Heer

De schop, en 't blad papier in de eene schaal gelegen,
Kan, door de Graversshoop, een goud beurs overwegen.

**English translation:**

Lord/Sir

The spade and the sheet of paper placed in one scale,
Can, through the Engraver's heap, outweigh a purse of gold.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Heer" (Lord/Sir)
- Spade symbol (♠) in top right corner
- Engraved illustration in woodcut or engraving style typical of 17th-century Dutch playing cards
- Text formatted as couplet/verse at bottom of card
- Part of "Pasquins Windkaart" series from "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Theatre)

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Vrouw

Men zet de schop vrij neer, ons
spelen is verbruijd
De blinde razernij van Quimquampoix
heeft uit.

**English translation:**

Woman [Lady]

One sets down the spade freely, our
playing is exhausted
The blind madness of Quincampoix
has ended.

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Vrouw" (Woman/Lady)
- Spade suit symbol (♠) in top right corner
- The text at bottom appears to be in 17th/18th century Dutch
- This is identified as "Queen of Spades" from the Pasquins Windkaart (Wind Chart) playing cards series from "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Theater)
- The reference to "Quimquampoix" (Quincampoix) appears to be referencing the Mississippi Bubble financial crisis

# Transcription

Kauegl

Prins Freddryks
mars, naar Vrianen

al de Schiegeleken.
Als wrind zoldaeten, naar Vrianen toe
te trekken.

**English translation:**

King [of Spades]

Prince Frederick's
march, toward Vriane

I sleep Prince Frederick's march, in order to meet
all the [soldiers/troops].
As wind soldiers, toward Vriane to
proceed.

# Transcription

Heer

Laurier Konning

't Heb de Achtes ceirst bedagt, maar een krâde Artiekans, Smit al mijn lof omwer, en schent mijn lauswer krans.

**English translation:**

Lord

Laurier King

I have the Eights first considered, but a crude Articans [or similar], Smite all my praise around, and shame my Laurel wreath.

# Transcription

Vrouw

De Actie ketel disteleerd,
Dat het papier in Goud verkeerd

Myn Lauwersman zolleerd om ieder

Stookt goud uit klad papier, en
maakt't Geld al vol zotten.

**English translation:**

Woman

The active kettle is distilled,
That the paper turns to gold

My Laurius man will swear to fool everyone,
Cooks gold from scrap paper, and
makes all the money foolish people.

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Knegel of Boef

Uit Wanhoop.

Min boeye hart, 't geen door een reeks van Ackestrekken, Mij deed naar voordeel zien, word nu van spijt doorsteken.

**English Translation:**

Knave of Hearts

Out of Despair.

My boyish heart, which through a series of tricks seemed to me advantageous, is now pierced through with regret.

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Ik ben beschermd, Heer
Daar ieder kermd.

'k Ben voor de Zuid en West hun
windgeblaas beschut
Verwijl een laauwe Goest 't Heel Al

**English translation:**

I am protected, Sir,
While everyone complains.

I am sheltered from the South and West winds' blast
While a lukewarm breeze helps the whole [thing] slumber.
