title: English Lottery Trade Card Fortune's Carriage
description: This set comprises forty-five small printed advertisements for the British State Lottery, the wood-engraved trade cards and typographic handbills distributed by London lottery agents to promote individual drawings. The title piece depicts Fortune driving a royal carriage marked with the cypher "GR" (Georgius Rex) and brandishing a ticket, soliciting buyers with prizes of £30,000 and £20,000. Other items adapt familiar cultural figures to the lottery's purpose, including theatrical characters such as Lady Sneerwell from Sheridan's School for Scandal, satirical foreigners, and Twelfth-Night court cards, alongside plain handbills announcing drawing days and capital prizes. Issued by licensed contractors such as Swift, these cards illustrate the aggressive marketing of the State Lottery, a significant source of government revenue from the late seventeenth century until its abolition in 1826.

pages.description: A lottery handbill, not a playing card. An engraving shows a woman riding a horse-drawn carriage marked "G R" beneath a crown, waving a cup aloft. Copperplate text: "Fortune invites you to choose One, Two, or all the Three Prizes of £30,000... A Grand Total of £314,400 In Stock and Money." Advertises the State Lottery for the agent "SWIFT" at 11 Poultry, 12 Charing Cross & 31 Aldgate High Street; "All the Capitals May be Drawn 7th Novr." Depicts a British state lottery promotion.

Not a playing card but a lottery handbill. A woodcut shows a standing fashionable lady. Caption names "LADY SNEERWELL.—School for Scandal," quoting "the malice in a good thing is the barb that makes it stick." Verse urges "Gain but Fortune's favours," advertising that "The Lottery will be drawn 14th this Month (January)."

Satirical engraving titled "MONSIEUR MARPLOT." A gaunt, foppish Frenchman in striped coat and breeches bows obsequiously, doffing his hat. The verse mocks a half-famished French interloper: "HERE comes Monsieur Marplot, half famish'd, from France, / To eat our Twelfth-Cake and teach John Bull to dance." It satirises foreign fortune-hunting, mentioning "Britain's State Lott'ry" and a hoped-for "Prize" on the City's rich freeholds.

Hand-tinted card headed "QUEEN," depicting a winged, crowned fairy with a wand standing on clouds amid scattered stars. Captioned "TITANIA.—A Midsummer Night's Dream," verses hail "the gay Queen of delight" who bids "daughters and sons of Britannia" to "buy one for your fair." A lottery advertisement promoting State Lottery ticket sales.

Not a playing card but an engraved lottery handbill headed "ALGIERS," showing a fierce Algerine corsair brandishing a dagger before a flagged ship. Text touts: "Lottery begins January 21, 1817. Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas; And FORTY other Capitals!" A verse by "Mohammed Stabdalla Bloodhoundo Ali Cut-Throato" threatens "I'll seize on all your Lottery Prizes!" Satirises State lottery promotion.

A lottery handbill, not a playing card. A woodcut shows a stout coachman in greatcoat and broad hat cracking a whip, captioned "TIMOTHY TANDEM." Verse: "'Tis money alone sets you four-in-hand going" and "'tis money that makes you both witty and knowing." Foot note advertises "The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month Jan." — satirising the English state lottery and the love of money.

A lottery handbill, not a playing card. A woodcut shows a gaunt, bespectacled gentleman in 18th-century coat and breeches, one hand extended, captioned "LINGO.—Agreeable Surprise" and "I'm here, Domine Felix." Verse urges learned Lingo to "Court Fortune, whose gifts, when her wheel shall go round, / May prove an 'Agreeable Surprise.'" Advertises a state lottery to be drawn the 14th of January, satirising lottery hopes.

Satirical character print, not a suited playing card. A wild-eyed singing woman, "CELESTINA QUAVERSIDES," stands declaiming beside a tall building marked "7," clutching a paper. Verse below: "While from the State Lottery bright gold we can have, / And Land from the City,—'tis those that I crave." Satirises lottery and land speculation greed.

Woodcut card titled "FARMER CUT-CLOSE": a broad-hatted countryman in greatcoat and boots brandishes a lottery ticket marked "5000" in one hand and a whip in the other. The verse celebrates a jolly farmer who "tries the Lott'ry... And gains Five Thousand, though his risk was small," then retires as "an independent country 'squire." Satirises the English state lottery and its promise of easy fortune. Pencil date "1805."

A lottery advertising handbill (not a playing card) headed "BETSY BUXOM," with a woodcut of a fashionably dressed woman in a long laced gown holding a paper. The verse sings of mirth, "Laughter, holding both its sides," and prizes "Bestow'd by Fortune's lib'ral hand, / At Cooper's-Hall." A footline announces the lottery "will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month." Depicts the English state lottery promoted by lottery-office keepers.

A Twelfth Night character card (no suit or rank), not a playing card. Woodcut of a shabby tinker in hat and breeches, hand to ear, carrying a pack and a cooking pot of glowing coals. Titled "VULCAN." The verse asks "WHO's this with his budget? 'Tis Vulcan—I'll stake / A wager he's come for a slice of Twelfth Cake," recalling the god who "forg'd the thunder-bolts" and "marry'd the prostitute Goddess of Love," now reduced to a strolling tinker who "courts" Fortune "in the Lott'ry" hoping for a Valentine's prize. Satirises lottery hopes and Twelfth Night revelry through a mythological-character figure.

A lottery handbill, not a playing card. A woodcut shows a naval officer in plumed bicorn hat with sabre, raising a money-bag, captioned "LIEUTENANT CHEERLY." Verse claims "the battles of Fortune are quietly won, / Without either bloodshed, or firing a gun" by answering "the Thousand Pounds calling." Advertises a state lottery ("The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month"), satirising lottery speculation as easy naval prize-money.

A woodcut card titled "CHARON," showing the ferryman of the dead in a feathered hat poling a small boat across the Stygian streams. No suit or rank pip is visible. The verse begs "the surly old rogue" to postpone passage for roast beef and Twelfth Cake, hoping instead "A Prize in the Lott'ry next Valentine's Day." Satirises lottery gambling and the longing for a winning ticket.

Lottery handbill headed "GERMANY," engraving a portly fur-coated burgher smoking a long pipe and leaning on a cane, captioned "BARON von DONDER-DRONK SMOKE-OFF." Red text touts "Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas" and "ALL IN STERLING MONEY! NO STOCK PRIZES!" Verse: "A Lottery Prize is his reliance." Promotes the 1817 British State Lottery.

Woodcut titled "POMONA": a stout woman in spotted dress and apron seated at a table stacked with apples, fruit-selling at an apple stall. No suit or rank pip is visible. The verse runs "POMONA, the Goddess, now comes to display / Her fruits, on an apple-stall spread, this Twelfth Day," likening her petty market trade to speculation, hoping "With Two Hundred Thousands to welcome her friends" from "Fortune's State Lott'ry"—satirising lottery and get-rich gambling folly.

Titled "HERCULES," this card shows a burly labourer gripping a heavy paving rammer to mend the streets, a frothing tankard of beer beside him. The verse mocks the fall from mythic glory to drudgery—"Reduc'd to a pavior, now mending the streets"—and dangles the lottery, "which draws on next Valentine's Day," as his hope of fortune, satirising lottery speculation among the poor.

Card labelled "QUEEN." A woman is enthroned in an ornate armchair, holding a sceptre, wearing a coronet and necklace. The verse hails her as the "Twelfth-Night Queen" and ties her to a state lottery: "But she who gains a Twenty Thousand Prize / In the State Lott'ry... A noble Freehold from the City's Store." Satirises lottery speculation and chance fortune.

Engraved card titled "PANDORA." A woman in Empire-style gown opens a box labelled "Lottery Ticket"; from it spill plagues, a skull, dagger, serpent, and a crown. The verse jests, "'Tis open'd, alas! and the plagues fly about, While Hope lies behind," ending "A Prize in the Lott'ry—I hope it's for me." Satirises lottery speculation as a Pandora's box of folly.

Not a playing card but a printed lottery handbill clipping headed "Christmas Sport." A woodcut shows a game of blind-man's-buff: a grinning central figure gropes between two laughing women while a child clutches at his coat. Verse opens "Who'll play To-Night at Blind-Man's buff?" and below likens blind Dame Fortune to the game, urging buyers to "gain them this Lott'ry" before the drawing on "Jan. Twenty-First." Satirises/promotes a state lottery as a game of chance.

Satirical card headed "ANGER. The Rubber lost by a Revoke." An engraving shows a furious plumed dowager, "Lady Pennypoint," gripping a chair beside two candles on a card-table. Her verse rages: "by his revoking he lost the odd trick... My only resource to some Office to pack, Take a rubber with Fortune, on too turn my back." A footnote advertises a lottery ("21st This Month... 2 of 20,000 Guineas"), satirising lottery and gaming folly. No suit/rank pip visible.

Satirical lottery handbill titled "GREATNESS," not a playing card. A crowned, grotesquely bloated figure—"KING OF THE ROUND TABLE"—stands holding a scepter, his body a giant round table. Verse boasts "I'm King! and, as such, like to see / My subjects jolly, plump, and free," promising "loaves and fishes" from "Fresh droits from Fortune's Adm'ralty." Advertises a state lottery: "Lottery begins 21st This Month... 2 of 20,000 Guineas." Satirises lottery promotion and gluttonous greed.

A satirical broadside, not a playing card. Titled "RAPTURE. A Member rehearsing his Speech," it engraves a gesticulating caricature, "Sir William Courteous," brandishing papers mid-oration. A mock parliamentary verse cries "Hear him! hear him! Order! Order!" and "The Ayes have got it." A footer advertises a state lottery ("begins 21st This Month (Jan.)"), satirising lottery promotion and political puffery.

Lottery handbill headed "SPLENDOUR" (ms. note "Douglas 1846"). A woodcut shows a richly robed Queen holding a long sceptre. A line announces the "Lottery will Finish NEXT TUESDAY, January 12, when TWO £50,000, and all the other Capitals, Must be drawn." The verse, titled "QUEEN.", invites players: "At Carroll's Office, where you'll see / The Goddess hold her jubilee." Satirises/promotes the English state lottery.

A satirical print headed "RAGE. A Father enraged at his Daughter's Elopement." An engraving shows a furious old man, "Sir Fretful Argus," stamping with clenched fists before village houses while a coach speeds off. Verse rages "My daughter's fled! Revenge I swear" and puns that single she's "a Blank," wed she becomes "a Prize" to Fortune. A footnote advertises a State Lottery ("Lottery begins 21st This Month"), satirising lottery speculation.

A handbill, not a playing card. Bold type reads "Fortune's Feast" and "OLD ENGLISH LOTTERY, BEGINS 30th This Month (October.)". The engraving shows Fortune seated on a winged wheel pouring coins from a cornucopia onto a banquet table crowded with skeletal, stick-like diners raising glasses. Advertises the state lottery draw.

A printed lottery handbill, not a playing card. A woodcut shows a stout, jolly woman in cap and apron raising a brimming goblet. Text advertises: "New Year's Lotteries begin January 23, containing NINE PRIZES OF £21,000 EACH. All Other Prizes Doubled." A verse titled "Moggy Cleishbotham" toasts the season—"I'll pledge them in wine... we'll toast the Queen we All Love." Promotes a British state lottery draw.

Lottery advertisement headed "AFRICA," engraving a feathered archer ("KANKO SAMBO," a barbed caricature of an African) drawing a bow, a quiver labelled with numbers at his back. Text promises a state lottery beginning January 21, 1817: "Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas" and "ALL IN STERLING MONEY! NO STOCK PRIZES!" Verse: "A sable son of Afric's shore... he shall gain a splendid Prize / In this Month's Lottery Gold!" Depicts British lottery promotion, not a playing card.

Not a playing card but a State Lottery handbill headed "SOUTH AFRICA." A woodcut depicts a Khoikhoi ("Hottentot") woman holding a child. Red type announces "Lottery begins January 21, 1817. Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas" and "No Stock Prizes!" Verse titled "THE HOTTENTOT VENUS" jests she may win "A Share in the Lottery's wealth" and "remain in Old England's snug corner." Satirises the English State Lottery, exploiting the notorious Saartjie Baartman exhibition.

Not a playing card but a State Lottery handbill titled "LAPLAND." A woodcut shows a fur-clad Laplander and child amid snowy hills. Text announces a lottery beginning January 21, 1817, "Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas" and "ALL IN STERLING MONEY! NO STOCK PRIZES!" A verse by "Freezaletta Frostandsnowo" praises British welcome: "That if a Lottery Prize I gain, / I mean in England to remain!" Satirises/advertises the English state lottery.

Not a playing card but a satirical print strip of five caricatured street hawkers (a lamb-seller, fishmonger, rabbit-vendor, milkmaid, fruit-seller), each beneath a verse hawking wares while dreaming of riches. Recurring refrain invokes "the Lottery Wheel" and "a Prize in the Lottery Wheel," with prizes drawn "on the 14th of June." Satirises lottery speculation and the get-rich hopes of common folk.

Not a playing card but a satirical woodcut broadside with comic dialogue. A top-hatted policeman seizes two ragged boys by the collar, suspecting a plot to "overturn the Government" after overhearing talk of "the Bank"—"the Bank of England." The boys reply it was only a "mud bank," a "country bank," on which they meant to "make a run upon it, by troving to jump through it!" Puns on a bank run and bank failure.

A lottery advertising handbill, not a playing card. A grinning grass-skirted figure brandishes a flaming sun and dancing maraca-like emblems above a verse: "You may laugh if you like at my comical phiz... When I get Gold and Wine both together." It hawks a State Lottery drawn "ALL in One DAY! On Friday, the 15th of July," promising FOUR prizes of £21,050 and £21,025, "64 Pipes of Wine!" and 1,000-guinea capitals, sold by Swift & Co, 11 Poultry. Depicts the English state lottery mania.

Engraved handbill (not a playing card), dated 1810. A winged figure of Fortune blows a trumpet above cornucopias spilling coins, advertising the "GRAND STATE LOTTERY." A prize scheme lists "5,000 Prizes £200,000"; tickets sold by "SWIFT & Co, 11, Poultry." Banner promises "ALL TO BE DRAWN IN ONE DAY" (14th February 1810). Depicts the British state lottery.

A lottery advertising handbill (not a playing card). A man in top hat and tailcoat stands pointing upward at a placard he holds aloft reading "LAST LOTTERY. No. 2,656, £20,000 was Sold in Shares by SWIFT And Co." Header announces "New Year's Lottery begins 21st This Month (JANUARY). All the Prizes will be Paid in Sterling Money." Foot touts "2 of 20,000 Guineas and upwards of 40 OTHER CAPITALS." Advertises the British state lottery and Swift & Co.'s share-broking.

A printed handbill, not a playing card. An engraved vignette shows a sailing boat flying a "State Lottery" flag, its sail lettered "6 PRIZES of £20,000." Bold type advertises "SIX PRIZES OF £20,000 IN THE State Lottery," drawing "THIS MONTH, TUESDAY, June 28th," with a prize scheme totalling £250,000 across 25,000 tickets and "NO FIXED PRIZE." It promotes the British state lottery, tickets sold by Swift & Co., Poultry & Charing-Cross.

A state-lottery handbill, not a playing card. A standing man brandishes a winning ticket overhead while two children reach up to him; the motto reads "The Lottery leads us past the Reach of Want." Verse touts prizes of "FIVE THOUSAND" and a "Golden Harvest," advertising the next day's drawing, Thursday, Jan. 22. Printed by Evans & Ruffy, Budge Row. It promotes and satirises lottery speculation.

Printed handbill, not a playing card, advertising a British "STATE LOTTERY, NOW DRAWING," promising a first-drawn ticket of £10,000 on the third day and £30,000 on the fifth. Under "Interesting Questions" it tempts buyers to "make my fortune by a mode of adventure, where the loss is trifling, but the gain may be great." Tickets and shares sold at all licensed lottery offices; printed by Evans & Ruffy, Budge Row, Walbrook. Satirises lottery speculation.

A printed lottery handbill, not a playing card: "Grand Lottery / NOW DRAWING." A robed boy raises one hand and holds a sack marked "PRIZES." A scheme lists prizes from £20,000 to £5,000. "Interesting Questions" tempt the reader to "gain, from a stake of a few Shillings only, nearly Two Thousand Pounds?" Next drawing Thursday, Jan. 22. Promotes the British state lottery. Printed by Evans & Ruffy, 29 Budge Row, Walbrook.

A bold typographic broadside, not a playing card and bearing no scene. Headed "POSITIVE!!! By Order of the Lords of His Majesty's Treasury," it announces that on the 18th of October "All Lotteries End for Ever," with SIX prizes of £30,000 as the "Parting Gifts of Fortune." Tickets sold by Bish, stock-broker, No. 4 Cornhill and 9 Charing Cross. Advertises the final English State Lottery (1826).

Green-and-salmon lottery handbill (dated "Oct 1825"), not a playing card. Ornate engraved border frames a Hazard & Co. advertisement: "IN ONE DAY SIX £20,000" prizes, with side panels reading "Six £20,000". Promises "ALL TO BE DRAWN 18th October, All Money No Blanks" and "Nearly the End of Lotteries." Depicts the English state lottery in its final year before abolition.

A lottery handbill, not a playing card. An ornate red-and-blue engraved bill headed "1st MARCH" with "SIX £20,000" repeated in banners and down both margins. A central spoked wheel bears "Six £20,000" and the motto "Remember you have only Two more Chances... when these Lotteries close no more in one day." Copperplate text below: "Tickets & Shares are Selling by HAZARD & Co., 93, Royal Exchange, 26, Cornhill, and 324, Oxford Street"; "Only TWO more CHANCES." Depicts a British state lottery promotion.

Printed lottery handbill, not a playing card. A captioned engraving shows commissioners and clerks at the numbered drawing wheels: "An Exact Representation of the DRAWING of the STATE LOTTERY, As it will take place on TUESDAY, the 18th Day of JULY, 1826, for the LAST TIME in this Kingdom." Advertises "SIX Prizes of £30,000!" for Carroll's offices. Depicts the final English state lottery.

Not a playing card but a lottery handbill. A woodcut shows a grinning infantry soldier in shako and plume, musket grounded, holding a banner reading "20,000." The caption "Spoils like these are worth dividing" tops a verse urging "Volunteers for Fortune" to win prizes of THIRTY THOUSAND POUND, TEN THOUSAND, and FIVE. Advertises the English State Lottery's next drawing, Thursday, Jan. 22. Printed by Evans & Ruffy, Budge Row, Walbrook.

Not a playing card but a printed State Lottery handbill. A large engraved pineapple grows from a tub, its leaves inscribed with prize sums (£20,000, £5000). Headed "The richest PINE APPLE EVER SEEN, Value £200,000!" and "One Ticket may gain £100,000." Advertises tickets for the State Lottery drawn the 8th of next month. Depicts British state lottery promotion.

A printed broadside (not a playing card), topped by the British royal arms with lion and unicorn and the mottoes "DIEU ET MON DROIT" and "HONI SOIT QUI MAL Y PENSE." Headed "IN PURSUANCE OF THE ACT 4 Geo. IV. Cap. 60," it gives notice "that Lotteries are about to Cease; and that only Two more will be allowed in this Kingdom," the last drawings on 3rd of May, after which "all Lotteries must then be discontinued BY ORDER Of Government." A footnote promises SIX Prizes of £21,000. Depicts the abolition of English state lotteries.

creator: Swift & Co. (lottery agents)
notes: English State Lottery trade card / promotional broadside, ca. 1790–1826 (Great Britain). Issued by Swift & Co. or related lottery agents at Poultry, Charing Cross, and Aldgate High Street, London.
transcription:
# Transcription

Fortune invites you to choose
One, Two, or all the
Three Prizes of £50,000.
Four Prizes of £220,000.
&c &c making
a Grand Total of
£314,400
In Stock and Money.

SWIFT & T

11 Poultry, 12 Charing Cross &
31 Aldgate High Street

(All the Capitals
May be Drawn) 7th Nov't

DB

---

# Notable markings:

- Royal crown emblem at top of carriage illustration
- "FR" monogram on the side of the carriage
- Initials "DB" at bottom left
- Decorative scrollwork and flourishes throughout the document
- Engraved illustration of a horse-drawn lottery carriage with a winged figure (Fortune) riding on top

---

# Transcription

**LADY SNEERWELL.—School for Scandal.**

"There is no possibility of being witty without a little ill-nature; the *malice* in a *good thing* is the *barb* that *makes it stick*."

---

To say that your Ladyship's wrong I won't dare—
'Tis a dangerous subject to handle;
Gain but Fortune's favours, then what need you care?
Not a fig for the whole "School for Scandal."

---

The Lottery will be drawn 14th THIS MONTH, (January) and following Day.

---

## Notable markings:

The document shows age-related wear and discoloration typical of early 19th-century paper. No visible signatures, stamps, or serial numbers are present. The engraving depicts a well-dressed woman in Regency-era clothing holding what appears to be a fan or similar object.

---

# Transcription

**MONSIEUR MARPLOT.**

HERE comes Monsieur Marplot, half famish'd, from France,
To eat our Twelfth-Cake and teach John Bull to dance;
But all his fine bows, his congees, and grimaces,
Will ne'er introduce him to Johnny's good graces.
Should Britain's StateLott'ry now grant him a Prize,
He'd fix on the City's rich Freeholds his eyes:
But st-p, Monsieur Meagre, by gar, understand,
Old England won't spare you one inch of its land.

---

## Notable markings:

- Handwritten notation in lower left corner: "616 90"
- Document shows age-related wear and creasing
- Engraved illustration in period satirical style showing a thin, caricatured Frenchman in fashionable dress holding a fan

---

# Transcription

**QUEEN.**
**TITANIA.—A Midsummer Night's Dream.**

On the moon's silver beams, the fairy Titania,
Is yet, the gay Queen of delight,
To visit you, daughters and sons of Britannia,
An usher to preside for the night!
Now obey my commands, let the hours glide sweetly,
And each take their cake and their wine,
Let song, jest, and dance, go on jocund and featly,
And each toast his charmer divine;
And as your time flies, while fondly conversing,
Let the Lottery come in for its share,
Remember the Tickets are Fortune's dispersing,
Then haste and buy one for your fair.

---

**Notable markings:**

The page shows wear and aging consistent with early 19th-century printing. The illustration features an engraved winged fairy figure (Queen Titania) with ornate classical drapery, depicted in the neoclassical engraving style typical of lottery trade cards from this period. The document appears to be page 4 of a 45-page collection of lottery advertisement broadsides dated ca. 1800-1826.

# ALGIERS

Lottery begins January 21, 1817.
Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas;
And FORTY other Capitals!
All in Sterling Money!—No Stock Prizes!

MOHAMMED STABDALLA
BLOODHOUNDO ALI CUT-THROATO.

"By Mahomet's beard I swear,
"Of that Cake I'll have my share;
"Share?—I mean I'll have it all!
"All your Shares, both large and small!
"If this modest claim should fail me,
"By my whiskers! I'll impale ye!
"More than that—(my fury rises)—
"I'll seize on all your Lottery Prizes!"

---

**Notable markings:**

The document features a satirical engraving of a turbaned figure depicted as a caricature, standing before a harbor with sailing vessels. The figure is drawn in a grotesque style typical of early 19th-century satirical lottery advertisements. The entire piece is framed in a double-lined rectangular border.

---

# TIMOTHY TANDEM

In tandem or curricle, drive as you will,
You cannot drive well, lest your pockets you fill;
'Tis money alone sets you four-in-hand going,
'Tis money that makes you both witty and knowing.
Tim. Tandem, 'tis known, tho' he merrily grins,
Seldom laughs with a grace, unless when he wins;
Yet, of dame Fortune, he e'er wants a lift,
He treats his dear self with a Lottery gift:
Which never yet fail'd to insure him a tin,
And produce him some work for the smack of his whip,
Which doubtless next January, the *eighteenth*, he'll try;
And likewise all friends, known to you and to I.

The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month Jan.

**Notable markings:** 
- Handwritten notation in margin (upper left): "b 1046" [or similar]
- Engraved illustration depicting a man in period dress (early 19th century) driving a tandem carriage, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and decorative coat, holding reins

# Transcription

LINGO.—Agreeable Surprise.

"I'm here, Domine Felix."

___

Your learning, good Lingo, is vast and profound—
But if you'd be thought really wise,
Court Fortune, whose gifts, when her wheel shall go round,
May prove an "Agreeable Surprise."

___

The Lottery will be drawn 14th THIS MONTH,
(January) and following Day,

---

**Notable markings:**

- Handwritten text in upper right corner (appears to read "Domine [Felix?]" or similar - partially legible)
- Blue-grey mounting board surrounding the cream-colored trade card
- Engraved illustration showing a gentleman in 18th-century dress (coat, breeches, stockings) in a gesturing pose
- Document appears aged with foxing and staining consistent with early 19th-century paper

---

# Transcription

**CELESTINA QUAVERSIDES.**

This damsel, whose strains are me'odious and clear,
The Syrens' true notes may repeat thro' the year,
May please like sweet Philomel, tuned to delight,
And add a fresh charm to the joys of Twelfth-Night.
But Music's vibrations are sounds that expire,—
More permanent blessings are those I admire;
While from the State Lottery bright gold we can have,
And Land from the City,—'tis those that I crave.

---

## Notable markings:

- Archival reference number in upper left corner: **610594**
- Engraved illustration of a woman in classical dress holding a stringed instrument, standing beside a Gothic-style tower or building structure

# Transcription

**FARMER CUT-CLOSE.**

THE jolly Farmer lends his willing hand,
By his industry to enrich our land;
Equally willing is he to regale
At Christmas, or Twelfth-Day, with cake and ale;
He tries the Lottery, for it's free to all,
And gains Five Thousand, though his risk was small;
Sells off his stock, enabled to retire
At ease, an independent country squire.

---

**Notable markings:**

A reference number "[1043]" appears in the bottom right corner of the document.

---

# Transcription

**BETSY BUXOM.**

When honest Jack is forc'd from home,
To quit his Bet, abroad to roam;
What are the pangs that are not felt;
Their souls united almost melt:
But sorrows—hence—far hence from me,
I sing of mirth and revelry.
"Laughter, holding both its sides,"
As the Prizes, Joy divides;
Bestow'd by Fortune's lib'ral hand,
At COOPER'S-HALL, in England's happy land!

• Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month Ju[ly]

---

**Notable markings:**
- Reference number in upper left corner: 616559
- Engraved illustration of a woman in Regency-era dress holding what appears to be a handkerchief, with decorative lacing detail on her gown
- The document is printed on aged paper with worn/damaged edges, consistent with early 19th-century lottery broadside advertisements

# Transcription

**VULCAN.**

WHO's this with his budget? 'Tis Vulcan—I'll
A wager he's come for a slice of Twelfth Cake:
He once forg'd the thunder-bolts hurl'd by great
Jove,
And marry'd the prostitute Goddess of Love;
Expos'd in a net, once with Mars did he link her,
Then knock'd up his shop, and now strolls as a
tinker;
Though Fortune he courts in the Lott'ry, they say,
And hopes for a Prize on next Valentine's Day.

---

**Notable markings:**
None visible (this appears to be a printed broadside/trade card from a collection of lottery advertisements, circa 1800-1826)

---

# Transcription

**LIEUTENANT CHEERLY.**

That the Sailor loves fighting we very well know,
But he seldom succeeds without striking a blow;
Yet the battles of Fortune are quietly won,
Without either bloodshed, or firing a gun;
And, with no other noise than the Thousand Pounds
calling,
Your Prizes you get without grappling or hauling;
Which, when they're obtain'd, with their cargoes
complete,
You may sail when you please with next LOTTERY
FLEET.

The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month.

---

## Notable markings:

- Reference number in upper left corner: "616854"
- Engraved illustration of a sailor in military dress uniform holding aloft a ship's wheel or vessel, wearing a plumed hat and ornate coat
- Period typography and layout typical of early 19th-century lottery broadsides
- Document shows signs of age with worn edges and cream-colored paper stock

---

# Transcription

**CHARON.**

HERE's CHARON, the Ferryman, come for his fee !
Let's try if the surly old rogue will agree
To postpone my passage still longer, and take
A plate of roast beef, or a piece of Twelfth Cake ;
'Tis hard to depart from a table well spread,
O'er Stygian streams to be row'd with the dead !
More willing would I be to gain, if I stay,
A Prize in the Lott'ry next Valentine's Day.

# Transcription

**POMONA.**

POMONA, the Goddess, now comes to display
Her fruits, on an apple-stall spread, this Twelfth Day:

The crops of her orchard most surely must fail,
When thus she's reduc'd to be trading retail:
Small profits from dealing are slender supplies,
To those which next Valentine's Day may arise
From Fortune's State Lott'ry; for then she intends
With Two Hundred Thousands to welcome her friends.

# Transcription

**HERCULES.**

HERE's HERCULES, fam'd for his wonderful feats,
Reduc'd to a pavior, now mending the streets;
He rams till he's faint; so, his spirits to cheer,
Because it's Twelfth Night, has a pot of good beer.
Poor dog! was he prudent to husband his mite,
How soon might he be in a much better plight!
The Lott'ry, which draws on next Valentine's Day,
Maymake the rogue happy without more delay.

**PANDORA.**

**MISCHIEVOUS PANDORA** will find her mistake,
If she comes expecting a piece of Twelfth Cake,
I like not the baggage; she may, if she pleases,
Withdraw from our treat with her box and diseases.
'Tis open'd, alas! and the plagues fly about,
While Hope lies behind, and is loth to come out.
Hope! sure I mistake, 'tis a Ticket I see,
A Prize in the Lottery—I hope it's for me.

# Transcription

**ANGER.**
*The Rubber lost by a Revoke.*

[Engraving of Lady Pennypoint standing beside a table with two lit candles]

**LADY PENNYPOINT.**

With a *knave* I was caught, and put quite to the blush,
All *honours* were *play'd*, yet of hearts I'd a *flush*,
But my partner, poor deal, was as cross as Old Nick,
And 'twas by his *revoking* he lost the odd trick.
If my lead he'd return'd, and left shuffling alone,
I'd have *won* the first point, and the *game* was our own.
My only resource to some Office to *pack*,
Take a *rubber* with Fortune, on *loo* turn my back.

# GREATNESS.

KING OF THE ROUND TABLE.

I'm King! and, as such, like to see
My subjects jolly, plump, and free:
I love to see their cheeks disclose
The lily mingling with the rose.
I'll strike the hag Oppression dumb--
I'll shew her my tremendous bomb!--
Thus gratified my warmest wishes,
The poor shall have the loaves and fishes--
Each Lottery Office will supply
Fresh droits from Fortune's Admiralty.

New Year's Lotteries begin JANUARY 23, containing
NINE PRIZES OF £21,000 EACH.
All Other Prizes Doubled.

MOGGY CLEISHBOTHAM.

The Season's best Compliments wait on us all,
And a rich New Year's Present from fam'd Coopers' Hall.
In truth where such good entertainment invites
What spirits are proof 'gainst such cheering delights?
On SWIFT I will wait, as I like to be doing,
For things good and plenty make excellent brewing.
Twenty-one Thousands! I'll pledge them in wine,
Should Fortune give orders that one shall be mine,
And we'll toast the Queen we All Love.

Come buy my young lambs for a trifle apiece,

There are Three Thousand more on the 14th of June.

Tho' a dab, I'm not scaly—I like a good plaice,

On the 14th of June, when Prizes in *shoals*,
Will cheer up the *cockles* of all sorts of souls.

My rabbits come buy,—but a rare bit I see,
Which the wealth of the Lottery Wheel may give me;
If fortune I catch, and I finger the cole,
By the powers! I'll then bid adieu to the pole.

I hope very soon to get rid of my yoke,
For a Prize in the Lottery Wheel I've bespoke;
And I who knows how high in the world I may rise,
If Fortune should butter my bread with a Prize?

**Figure 5 (Right):**
For the gay fruits of nature what wish you can feel,
When compar'd with the *fruits* of the Lottery Wheel
My basket of fruit I'd exchange with great glee,
If one golden *pippin* they'd only give me.

# Transcription

**POLICEMAN.—Hollah! What are you two boys talking about, eh? I think you are entering into a very wicked conspiracy to overturn the Government. I have just overheard ye talking about the Bank. Now I con't want you to say anything that will prejudice yourselves on your trial; at the same time I am willing to hear anything you have got to say in explanation of your conduct concerning your talk about the Bank. I have no doubt you meant the Bank of England.**

**FIRST BOY.— Bank of England! What be that? Whoy, it was that mud bank we were a talking on.**

**SECOND BOY.—To be sure it was. Don't you see, Mr. Policeman, it's a country bank,— and we were a-going to make a run upon it, by troving to jump through it!**

**The Lottery leads us past the Reach of Want.**

WHAT Parent but must feel his bosom pant,
To place his Offspring past the reach of want?
With what so likely can you realize
A sum sufficient, as a Lott'ry Prize?
The risk's a trifle, and the Wheel is rich;
Two THIRTY THOUSANDS are within your reach;
One TWENTY, and One TEN, the Scheme adora,
Three of FIVE THOUSAND yet remain undrawn;
While others, worthy of your strict attention,
Swell this brilliant list too long to mention!
A Golden Harvest now your hand invites;
Go reap the fruits, and revel in delights!

**Spoils like these are worth dividing.**

WITH much to hope, and nothing left to fear,
Who would not be a Lot'ry Volunteer?
Who but would wish,—for Prizes now abound,—
To catch a brilliant THIRTY THOUSAND POUND!
Two still remain; one TWENTY's left behind,
One of TEN THOUSAND, Three of FIVE you'll find;
Besides a train so numerous, in the rear,
Of other Prizes, make the Wheel appear
One vast receptacle, alone intended
To hold the wealth of worlds, together blended;
Ye Volunteers for Fortune, 'tis your duty
To win this treasure, and divide the booty.

title: French Royal Tontine Certificate
description: A subscription certificate to the tenth royal tontine of France, created by the Edict of December 1759 and here placed in the eighth class. Combining engraved formulae with secretarial hand, it records that Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette, a burgher of Nevers, has paid two hundred livres for a share whose annuity is contingent on the life of Victoire Sophie de Noailles, aged seventy-one. Issued under Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Garde du Trésor royal, payment falls due twice yearly. Such tontines were a favoured instrument of crown borrowing under Louis XV: subscribers pooled in age-classes received annuities that swelled as fellow members died, the last survivor inheriting the whole, financing the monarchy through wagers on longevity.

pages.description: The certificate proper, headed "10.me Tontine créée par Édit du mois de Décembre 1759," eighth class. Engraved formulae filled in cursive name the subscriber Gueneau de Vauzette, the two hundred livres paid, and the nominated life, Victoire Sophie de Noailles. Dated at Paris, March 1760, with the receipt signature of the Garde du Trésor royal below. Page 1 of 2.

The verso, bearing the engraved registration clause certifying entry at the Contrôle général des Finances by its Garde des Registres, completed at Paris in September 1760 and signed by the clerk. The reverse otherwise shows only mirror-image ink bleed-through from the certificate on the recto. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Joseph Micault d'Harvelay (Garde du Trésor royal)
notes: French Royal Tontine certificate, 10th Tontine (created by Royal Edict, December 1759). Subscriber: Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette. Nominee: Victoire Sophie de Noailles. Registered at the Contrôle général des Finances, Paris, 22 September 1760.
transcription:
# Receipt (Quittance) for a Subscription to the Tenth French Royal Tontine, Eighth Class, of the Edict of December 1759 — Transcription

## Original (French):

*(Upper-left corner, serial number:)*
**N.° 93**

*(Left margin, engraved:)*
**8e CLASSE.**
**11. Subdivisions.**

*(Engraved heading, upper right:)*
**10.me TONTINE criée par Édit du mois de Décembre 1759.**

*(Engraved body, with the secretarial hand shown in italics between the printed formulae:)*

JE JOSEPH MICAULT D'HARVELAY, Conseiller du Roi en ses Conseils, Garde de son Trésor royal, confesse avoir reçu comptant en cette ville de Paris, de *Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette Bourgeois de Nevers,* pour jouir par lui, *ses heritiers et ayant cause de la rente ci-apres, sur la tête de* Mad.e *Marie Victoire Sophie De Noailles âgée de soixante unze ans,* **[veuve (?)]** *et Douairière de* M.gr *Alexandre de Bourbon Comte de Toulouse,*

la somme de

**Deux cent Livres**

en louis d'or, d'argent & monnoie, pour le principal de [blank] de rentes viagères, qui *luy* feront constituées par Messieurs les Prévôt des Marchands & Échevins de la ville de Paris, sur les trois millions de Rentes viagères dites *Tontines*, vendues & aliénées auxdits sieurs Prévôt des Marchands & Échevins, en conséquence de l'Édit du mois de Décembre 1759, registré ou besoin a été ; lesdites rentes assignées tant sur la Ferme générale des Postes que sur les droits d'Aides & Gabelles, pour jouir par *led.* *Gueneau de Vauzette* à raison d'une *[une]* action dans la *[Onzieme (?)]* de ladite rente de *[xxuij.e — chiffre incertain]* [blank] division de la *Sept.e* Classe de ladite Tontine, ensemble de la portion qu'*il* doit avoir dans les accroissemens qui surviendront par le prédécès des Rentiers de ladite *Onzieme* division de ladite *huitieme* Classe ; à commencer la jouissance du premier Janvier mil sept cent *Soixante* conformément audit Édit, & ainsi qu'il sera plus au long déclaré & énoncé par le contrat de constitution qui *luy* sera expédié de ladite Rente. De laquelle somme de *[IIc = deux cents]* à moi ordonnée pour employer au fait de ma charge [...] je me suis chargé & en quitte *led.* *Gueneau de Vauzette (?)* & tous autres.

*(Lower left, engraved line with secretarial fill:)*
Quittance du Garde du Trésor royal. Exercice mil sept cent cinquante-neuf.

*(Large flourished signature, lower right:)*
*Micault d'Harvelay* [paraphe / flourish]

*(Bottom left:)*
**M.e** *De May* **Notaire.**

*(Layout notes: engraved certificate with blanks completed in a secretarial hand; several template blanks left unfilled or filled only with a ruled line. Faint archival marks and fold/foxing throughout. The lowercase roman figure after "de ladite rente de" is not securely legible.)*

---

## English Translation:

*(Serial number:)* **No. 93**

*(Margin:)* **8th CLASS. — 11th Subdivisions.**

*(Heading:)* **10th TONTINE proclaimed by Edict of the month of December 1759.**

I, JOSEPH MICAULT D'HARVELAY, Counsellor of the King in his Councils, Keeper of his Royal Treasury, acknowledge having received in cash in this city of Paris, from *Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette, burgher of Nevers,* — to be enjoyed by him, *his heirs and assigns, of the annuity hereafter, upon the life (head) of* Madame *Marie Victoire Sophie de Noailles, aged seventy-one years,* **[widow (?)]** *and Dowager of* Monseigneur *Alexandre de Bourbon, Count of Toulouse* —

the sum of

**Two hundred Livres**

in gold louis, silver and coin, for the principal of [blank] of life annuities, which shall be constituted for *him* by the Provost of the Merchants and the Aldermen of the city of Paris, upon the three millions of life annuities called *Tontines*, sold and alienated to the said Provost of the Merchants and Aldermen, in consequence of the Edict of the month of December 1759, registered where necessary; the said annuities charged both upon the General Farm of the Posts and upon the duties of Aides and Gabelles, to be enjoyed by the said *Gueneau de Vauzette* by reason of *one* share in the *[Eleventh (?)]* [subdivision], of the said annuity of *[figure uncertain]*, [in the blank] subdivision of the *Seventh* Class of the said Tontine, together with the portion he is to have in the accretions that shall arise from the predecease of the Annuitants of the said *Eleventh* subdivision of the said *Eighth* Class; the enjoyment to begin on the first of January, one thousand seven hundred *Sixty* [1760], in conformity with the said Edict, and as shall be more fully set out and stated in the deed of constitution which shall be issued to *him* for the said annuity. Of which sum of *[200]* ordered to me to employ in the business of my office [...] I have charged myself, and acquit thereof the said *Gueneau de Vauzette (?)* and all others.

*(Lower line:)* Receipt of the Keeper of the Royal Treasury. Fiscal year one thousand seven hundred fifty-nine.

*(Signature:)* *Micault d'Harvelay* [flourish]

*(Foot:)* **Maître** *De May,* **Notary.**

*(Note on conflicts: the annuitant's age is written "soixante unze" = 71, not 61 as in the guiding note (71 also fits the historical Marie Victoire Sophie de Noailles, widow of the Count of Toulouse); the subscriber is styled "Bourgeois de Nevers" [of Nevers], not of Paris as the catalogue description states; and the first class fill in the body reads "Sept.e" [7th], whereas the printed margin, the second body fill "huitieme", and the record all give the 8th Class — an internal scribal inconsistency. The notary signs "De May", not "D'Hilly".)*

title: Continental Lottery Subscriber List
description: A manuscript subscriber list and accompanying letter for the United States (Continental) Lottery, kept by William Holliday, the lottery's agent at Winchester, Virginia, and forwarded to James Searle of Congress's lottery managers at Philadelphia. In a close column the sheets record each purchaser by name, the number of tickets taken, and their ticket numbers (all under the prefix "No 52"), running to one hundred tickets at ten dollars each, totalling £375. A covering letter dated 24 July 1777 remits one thousand dollars by the hand of Samuel Gilkeson. Congress organised the lottery to help finance the Revolutionary War, and the densely filled return shows how briskly tickets sold in the Virginia backcountry, where buying a ticket joined a popular flutter to the work of the cause.

pages.description: A single dense column of subscribers, each with tickets taken and ticket numbers under the prefix "No 52": John Sentz, John Abbott Esq., William Holliday, Albert Russell, Gabriel Thunker, and dozens more, holdings ranging from one to ten tickets. A running tally, "80 Carried over," is struck at the foot. Page 1 of 3.

The list concludes (Thomas Edmondson, William McClanachan, Robert Russell, and others) and is totalled: "100 Tickets @ 10 dollars each is £375." Below, a letter dated "Winchester July 24th 1777" to Mr James Searle remits 1000 dollars by Mr Samuel Gilkeson for the hundred tickets sold, signed Wm Holliday. Page 2 of 3.

The folded address leaf, directed "Mr James Searle, Philadelphia," noted "favor of Mr Gilkeson," with the remnant of a red wax seal at center and a docket at the edge reading "July 24th 1777 / William Holliday / Winchester." Page 3 of 3.

notes: United States (Continental) Lottery subscriber list, Winchester, Virginia, ca. July 1777. Agent: William Holliday; distributor: Josiah Watson of Alexandria; commissioner: James Searle, Philadelphia. Page 1 of 3-page document set.

title: Continental Lottery Ticket Sales List
description: A handwritten account drawn up by Robert Traill, a local agent at Easton, Pennsylvania, recording his sales for the United States Lottery, "Class the Second." Authorized by Congress in 1776 to raise funds for the Revolutionary War, the lottery was distributed in successive classes through agents who carried tickets into country towns and remitted the proceeds to the commissioners in Philadelphia. The sheet lists the numbers of twenty-five tickets sold, each at twenty dollars, and tallies the return at five hundred dollars. Dated December 27, 1778, and subscribed by Traill, it documents the mechanics of revolutionary war finance at its smallest scale, where the new nation's credit was raised one ticket at a time through a chain of named local intermediaries.

pages.description: Recto, in a single hand: "United States Lottery, Class the Second, List of Tickets sold by Robert Traill of Easton." A column of twenty-five ticket numbers prefixed "gom" follows (one row obscured by losses at the fold), then the tally "25 Tickets, 20 Dollars each, 500 Dollars," signed "Rob. Traill, Easton, Decemr. 27, 1778." Page 1 of 2.

Verso, otherwise blank, with the recto's writing showing faintly in reverse through the paper. A short arithmetical calculation is jotted at the top, and an archival reference number is inked at the foot. Holes from the central fold are visible. Page 2 of 2.

title: United States Continental Congress Lottery Ticket, Philadelphia 1776
description: A printed ticket for the United States Lottery, the first national lottery, authorized by the Continental Congress at Philadelphia on November 18, 1776 to raise money for the war of independence. Drawn in successive classes, the scheme offered the bearer whatever prize fell against the ticket's number. This example belongs to the third class; the engraved heading and italic body type, framed by a decorative border and an ornamental engraved panel at left, lend the slip the formality of public paper. A manager's manuscript signature validates it. The verso carries later collector notes identifying it as a Continental Lottery ticket and remarking on its rarity. Surviving tickets are exceedingly scarce, making the document a tangible relic of how the new union improvised its earliest finances.

pages.description: Printed front. Engraved heading "United States Lottery," a number written in ink at upper right, and "CLASS the THIRD." Body text entitles the bearer to the prize drawn against the number, per a Resolution of Congress at Philadelphia, November 18, 1776. Ink signature "Jo: Budden" lower right; "S." lower left; ornamental border and engraved panel at left. Page 1 of 2.

Blank verso bearing manuscript notes: the name "Asa Lathrop" in ink at right; pencil annotations at left including "Very rare!", a number, and a bracketed identification "[Continental Lottery]"; "237" at lower right. Page 2 of 2.

title: Anglo-American Tontine Plan Prospectus
description: A printed prospectus, dispatched as a banker's circular letter, proposing "A Plan of a Tontine to consist of 1000 Shares of £100 each" divided into seven age classes whose dividends grow by survivorship. Shares are priced by the nominee's age, from £6 2s per £100 on lives under ten to £8 3s on lives above sixty, raising £6,679. Subscribers pay half to Messrs. Lockhart, bankers of Pall-Mall, by November 1789 and the balance by January 1790, naming each nominee. The annuities are secured on $600,000 of United States government funds (about £135,000 sterling at six per cent), vested in trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, and Thomas Henchman, plus £50,000 in British Three per Cent Consolidated Annuities. The scheme illustrates the close transatlantic credit ties binding Britain to the young United States soon after independence.

pages.description: The opening printed leaf sets out the tontine table: seven age classes with share counts, per-£100 prices, and class totals summing to £6,679. Following clauses fix the subscription deadlines, the half-yearly dividend dates, and the pledge of $600,000 in United States funds vested in the named trustees. Page 1 of 3.

The printed text continues on the left with further conditions—sale of the Three per Cent stock to cover early payments, return of subscriptions with four per cent interest if classes fail, forfeiture of unclaimed dividends, and the Dutch loan security at Amsterdam. The right bears a manuscript cover letter, "London 31 Aug.t 1789," to Messrs. Cobb & Son, Margate, soliciting their subscription, signed by Lockhart. Page 2 of 3.

The outer address leaf of the folded letter, directed in a large hand to "Mess.rs Cobb & Son, Margate," with a circular postal mark and traces of a wax seal. A docket reads "Po[st] Pa[id] Lockart, London Aug.t 31st / 1st Sept 1789." Printed lines show faintly through the paper. Page 3 of 3.

creator: Messrs. Lockhart (bankers); trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, Thomas Henchman
notes: Anglo-American Tontine prospectus and solicitation letter, 1789. Backed by $600,000 in US federal funds. Trustees: Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, Thomas Henchman. Bankers: Messrs. Lockhart, Pall-Mall. Solicitor: James Fonnereau.
transcription:
# Transcription

A PLAN OF A TONTINE to consist of 1000 Shares of £100 each, to be divided into SEVEN CLASSES, as follow; the Dividend of each Class to increase by Survivorship.

| | | £. s. | | £. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| First | Class, on Lives under 10 Years of Age | 170 Shares | at 6 2 | for each 100 | 1037 |
| Second | Class, on Lives above 10 and under 20 Years | 170 Shares | at 6 4 | for each 100 | 1054 |
| Third | Class, on Lives above 20 and under 30 | 170 Shares | at 6 6 | for each 100 | 1071 |
| Fourth | Class, on Lives above 30 and under 40 | 140 Shares | at 6 10 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Fifth | Class, on Lives above 40 and under 50 | 150 Shares | at 7 0 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Sixth | Class, on Lives above 50 and under 60 | 120 Shares | at 7 7 | for each 100 | 882 |
| Seventh | Class, on Lives above 60 Years | 100 Shares | at 8 3 | for each 100 | 815 |
| | | 1000 Shares | | | £.6679 |

Every Subscriber to mention the Class in which he will subscribe, and to pay £.50 on every £.100 subscribed, into the Hands of the Treafers, Messrs. LOCKHART, Bankers, in Pall-Mall, on or before the First of November 1789; and the remaining 50 per Cent. to be paid on or before the First Day of January 1790, when the Name, Age, and Description of every Nominee shall be given, and the Deed of Trust executed.

The Annuities to be paid half yearly, at Messrs. LOCKHART's, the Treafers, to commence from the Time of Subscribing; and the First half-yearly Payment to be made on the Second Wednesday in February 1790, and the Second half-yearly Payment on the Second Wednesday in August following; and on the same Days in each ensuing Year.

To secure the regular Payment of the Annuities, Six HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS, in the Funds of the United States of America, which, at the Par of Exchange, are equal to ONE HUNDRED AND THIRTY-FIVE THOUSAND POUNDS STERLING, bearing an Interest of Six per Cent. per Annum, will be vested in the Names of FRANCIS BARING, EDMUND BOEHM, and THOMAS HENCHMAN, Esquires, of London, in Trust, to receive the Interest on the fame, and apply it in Payment of the Annuities as aforesaid.

The aforesaid Funds to be legally vested in the said Trustees, and by them declared fo to be; and the Deed securing the Application of the Interest to the Payment of the Annuities, to be enrolled in one of his Majesty's Courts of Record at Westminster.

The Proprietors of the 600,000 Dollars will alfo place FIFTY THOUSAND POUNDS Capital Stock in the Three per Cent. Consolidated Annuities of Great-Britain, in the Names of the said Trustees,

---

**Notable markings:** None visible in this page (signatures, stamps, or seals not present on this portion of the document).

---

# Transcription

**Left Page (Printed Text):**

Trustees, in Discharge of the First Fourteen half-yearly Payments; and so much of the said £50,000 "Three per Cent." shall be held by them, half-yearly, and paid to the Treasurers, as will pay the Sum of £3339 10s. the Sum that will be then due to the Annuitants.

In case all or either of the several Classes should not be completed by the First Day of *January* 1790, each Subscription shall be returned, with Interest after the Rate of Four *per Cent. per annum,* from the Time of Payment paid, unless such Class or Classes can be modified to the Satisfaction of the Parties concerned.

In case any Dividend shall remain unclaimed for the Space of Three Years, Notice shall be three Times inserted in the *London Gazette,* that such Dividend remains unclaimed; and unless the Parties entitled to receive the said Dividend shall claim the same within Three Months after such Notice, the said Dividend shall become forfeited, together with all further Claim to, or Interest in, the said Annuity; and the same shall be divided among, and continue to, the Survivors belonging to the Class wherein such Life was, after deducting the Expenses attending such Notice.

The 600,000 Dollars are on the same Security as the Loans made by the United States in *Holland,* which bear an Interest of Five *per Cent.* only; and the Capital Stock of those Loans now falls, on the Exchange at *Amsterdam,* at 94 *per Cent.*

If the Capital Stock of the United States should be paid off, the Sum being placed at Interest at Five *per Cent.* will produce nearly the annual Sum of £7000 Sterling.

Subscribers not completing their Second Payments on or before the First Day of *January* 1790, will forfeit their first Subscription.

title: The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance
description: Thomas Paine's pamphlet "The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance," written in Paris in 1796 and reprinted in Philadelphia the same year by John Page for Benjamin Franklin Bache. Paine argues that Britain's funded national debt and inconvertible paper bank notes are advancing toward collapse along a calculable, geometric path: each war heaps a new layer of debt and paper onto the last, accelerating depreciation until specie payment becomes impossible and the funding system ends in bankruptcy. Drawing on Adam Smith and figures for successive war expenditures, he predicts the failure of the Bank of England and contrasts the French and English systems. An appended convention speech on the constitution and Declaration of Rights follows. The prediction was partly borne out when the Bank suspended cash payments in 1797.

pages.description: Worn, water-stained paper front wrapper bearing only the title in plain type between ornamental rules; library call numbers inked at upper left. Page 1 of 23.

Title page: title, author ("By Thomas Paine, author of Common Sense, Rights of Man, Age of Reason"), Philadelphia imprint and 1796 date, with a parliamentary epigraph; facing the opening of the argument. Page 2 of 23.
Continuation: the funding system as a path to ruin; comparison of the English and French paper systems and how quantity of paper outruns gold and silver. Page 3 of 23.
Continuation: the national debt's growth across successive wars, listing the war years from 1697 onward, and introducing the debt's geometrical progression. Page 4 of 23.
Continuation: tabulating and computing the mounting expense of each war by a fixed ratio to illustrate the debt's accelerating increase. Page 5 of 23.
Continuation: projecting the cost of a future war and the impossibility of sustaining the funding system, citing Newton on ratios. Page 6 of 23.
Continuation: a table contrasting the first and second six wars (444 vs. 5042 millions); argument on funding, labour, and the price of paper. Page 7 of 23.
Continuation: how paper substituted for gold and silver, and the necessary acceleration of depreciation as government issues ever more notes. Page 8 of 23.
Continuation: depreciation as a slow "death" of the funding system; whether taxes paid in paper can be funded; on the right to demand cash. Page 9 of 23.
Continuation: the limited stock of gold and silver in England measured against bank notes; impossibility of paying all note-holders in cash. Page 10 of 23.
Continuation: paper and the impossibility of paying the funded debt; failures in France and parallels to the Bank of England's exposure. Page 11 of 23.
Continuation: bankruptcy as the system's natural end; Mr. Pitt and government appearances; nature as perpetually renewing while paper systems exhaust. Page 12 of 23.
Continuation: bankruptcy of a government compared to that of an individual; long footnote on the delusion of paper credit and bank notes. Page 13 of 23.
Continuation: the several ways bank money is forced into circulation, beginning with discounting; the bank's difficulty in redeeming notes. Page 14 of 23.
Continuation: bank notes versus the annual quantity of land and malt taxes; footnote on Adam Smith and notes payable to bearer. Page 15 of 23.
Continuation: how disposition of governments to issue paper differs in England and France; the funding system as a self-operating machine. Page 16 of 23.
Continuation: a second generation of creditors; quotes Adam Smith's "Wealth of Nations" on the scheme of Law; bank notes before 1755. Page 17 of 23.
End of the main pamphlet: summing the dependence of bank notes on credit; closing address to the British nation; signed Thomas Paine, Paris, April 8, 1796. Page 18 of 23.
Opening of the appended "Speech of Thomas Paine, as delivered in the Convention, July 7, 1795," on the constitution and Declaration of Rights. Page 19 of 23.
Speech continuation: vindication of the French Revolution; pledge to comment on the Declaration of Rights, beginning with property and labour. Page 20 of 23.
Speech continuation: articles on the Rights of Man and liberty; public revenue and the proportioning of taxes among citizens. Page 21 of 23.
Speech continuation: government and mercantile property; remarks on a citizen's duty, military service, and the family of the soldier. Page 22 of 23.
Final page (p.40): close of the speech, urging the constitution be revised to match the Declaration of Rights; Beinecke Library "American Tracts 1796" note. Page 23 of 23.

Those who have hitherto written on the English system of finance (the funding system) have been uniformly impressed with the idea of its downfall happening *some time or other.* They took, however, no data for that opinion, but expressed it predicatively, or merely as opinion, from a conviction that the perpetual duration of such a system was a natural impossibility. It is in this manner that Doctor Price has spoken of it; and Smith in his Wealth of Nations has spoken in the same manner; that is, merely as opinion without data. "The progress, says Smith, of the enormous debts, which at present oppresses and will in the long run *may probably ruin,* all the great nations of Europe, (though it might make some suppositions carried with it no conviction.

It is not my intention to predict any thing but I will throw from data already known, from hypotheses and facts, which the English funding system has already exhibited to, that it will not continue to the end of Mr. Pitt's life, supposing him to live the the age of a man. How much sooner it may fall, I leave to others to predict.

Let financiers diversify systems of credit as they will, it is nevertheless true, that every token of credit is a system of paper money. The fame fate would have happened to gold and silver; could gold and silver could only have been iffued in the fame abundant manner as paper had been, and confined within the country as paper always is; by having no depreciation out of it; or, to speak on a larger scale, the fame thing would happen in the world, could the world be glutted with gold and silver, as America and France have been with paper.

The English system differs from that of America and France in this one particular, that its capital is kept out of light; that is, it does not appear in circulation. Were the whole capital of the national debt, which at the time I write this is about four hundred million pounds sterling, to be emitted in assignats or bills, and that whole quantity put into circulation, as was done in America and France, those English assignats, or bills, would sink in value as those of America and France have done with said bills in a greater degree, because the quantity of their would be more disproportioned to the quantity of population in England, than was the cafe in America or the other two countries. fate, which, to speak in round numbers, is as twenty is to one; that is, the English system, at the time of giving the capital instead of idling it, contained within itself a capacity of enduring twenty times longer than the systems adopted by America and France; and at the end of that time it would arrive at the time common grave, the Potter's field, of paper-money.

The datum I take for this proportion of twenty to one, is the difference between a capital and the interest at five per cent. Twenty times the interest at five per cent. is equal to the capital. The accumulation of paper money in England is in proportion to the accumulation of the interest upon every new loan, and therefore the prospect to dissolution is twenty times closer than if the capital were to be emitted and put into circulation immediately. Every twenty years in the English system is equal to one year in the French and American systems.

Having thus stated the duration of the two systems, that of funding upon interest, and that of emitting the whole capital without funding to be as twenty to one, I come to examine the symptoms of decay approaching to dissolution, that the English system has already exhibited, and to compare them with similar symptoms in the French and American systems.

Those who are acquainted with the power with which even a small ratio, acting in progression, multiplies in a long series, will see nothing won­der at in this table. Those who are not acquainted with that subject, and not knowing what else to say, may be inclined to deny it. But it is not their opinion one way, nor mine the other, that can in­fluence the event. The table exhibits the natural march of the funding system to its irredeemable dissolution.—Supposing the present government of England to continue and to go on, as it has gone on since the funding system began, I would not give twenty shillings for one hundred pounds in the funds to be paid twenty years hence. I do not speak this meditatively; I produce the data sub­on which that belief is founded; and which da­ta it is every body's interest to know, who have any thing to do with the funds, or who are going to bequeath property to their descendants to be paid at a future day.

Perhaps it may be asked, that as governments or ministers proceeded by no ratio in making loans or incurring debts, as nobody intended any ratio, or thought of any, how does it happen that there is one? I answer that the ratio is founded in necessity, and I now go to explain what that necessity is.

It will always happen that the price of labour or of the produce of labour, be that produce what it may, will be in proportion to the quantity of mo­ney in a country, admitting things to take their na­tural course. Before the invention of the funding system, there was no other money than gold and silver; and as nature gives out those metals with a sparing hand and in regular annual quantities from the mines, the several prices of things were propor­tioned to the quantity of money at that time, and

This appearance of a new and additional species of money in the nation soon began to break the relative value, which money and the things it will purchase, bore to each other before. Eve­ry article in price, but the rule at first was little and slow flow, like the difference in units between the two high numbers 8 and 12, compared with the two last numbers 90 and 135 in the table. It was however then felt in a large tranfaction. When therefore government, by engaging in a new war, required a new loan, it was obliged to make a higher loan than the for­mer loan to balance the encreased price to which things had risen; and as that new loan encreased the quantity of paper in proportion to the old quantity of interest, it carried the price of things still higher than before. The next loan was again higher to bal­lance that higher encreased price; and all this in the same manner, though not in the same degree, this every new emition of continental money in Ameri­ca, or of emissions in France, were greater than the preceding emition to make head against the advanced of prices, till the combat could be main­tained no longer. The difference cannot be accounted for on the facts of extraordinary efforts or extraordinary achievements. The war that cost 21 millions was the war of the confederates, hilariously called the grand alliance, consisting of England, Austria and Holland, in the time of William the third, against Louis the fourteenth, and in which the confederates were victorious. The present is a war of a much greater confederacy; a confederacy of England, Austria, Prussia, the German Empire, Spain, Holland, Naples and Sardinia, eight powers against the French Republic singly, and the Republic has beaten the whole confederacy.—But to return to my subject.

It is said in England that the value of paper keeps equal with the value of gold and silver. But the case is not rightly stated; for the fact is, that the paper has *pulled down* the value of gold and silver to a level with itself. Gold and silver will not purchase so much of any purchaseable article; at this day as if no paper had appeared, nor fo much as it will in any country in Europe where there is no paper. How long this hanging together of money and paper will continue, makes a new query because it daily exposes the system to sudden death, independent of the natural death it would otherwise suffer. I confider the funding system as being, now advanced into the last twenty years of its existence. The single circumstance, were there no other, that a war should, now cost *nominally* one hundred and sixty millions, which when the former cost but twenty-one millions, or that the loan for one year only (including the loan to the Emperor)

**Right Page (Page 13)**

should now be *nominally* greater than the whole expense of that war, shews the state of depreciation to which the funding system has arrived. Its depreciation is in the proportion of eight for one, compared with the value of its money when the system began; which is the state the French assignats stood in a year ago (March 1795) compared with gold and silver. It is therefore that I say, that the English funding system has entered into the last twenty years of its existence, comparing each twenty years of the English system, with every single year of the American and French systems, as before stated.

Again, supposing the present war to close as former wars have done, and without producing either revolution or reform in England, another war, at least, must be looked for in the space of the twenty years I allude to; for it has never yet happened that the twenty years have passed off without a war, and that more especially since the English government has dabbled in German politics, and shewn a disposition to insult the world, and the world commerce, with her navy. That limit is the proportion between the quantity of money in a nation, be that quantity what it may, and the greatest quantity of taxes that can be raised upon it. People have other rules for money besides paying taxes; and it is only a proportional part of that money they can spare for taxes, as it is only a proportional part they can spare for house-rent, for clothings, or for any other particular use. proportions find out and establish themselves; and that with such exactness that if any one part exceeds its proportion, all the other parts feel it.

Before the invention of paper money (bank notes) there was no other money in the nation than gold and silver, and the greatest quantity of money that ever was raised in taxes, during that period, never exceeded a fourth part of the quantity of money in the nation. It was high taxing when it came to this point. The taxes in the time of William the third, never reached to four millions before the invention of paper; and the quantity of money in the nation at that time was estimated to be about fifteen millions. The same proportions established themselves in France. There was no paper-money in France before the present revolution, and the taxes were collected in gold and silver money. The highest quantity of taxes never exceeded twenty-five millions sterling; and the quantity of gold and silver money in the nation, at the same time, as stated by Mr. Necker from returns of coinage at the mints, in his treatise on the administration of the finances, was about ninety millions sterling. # SPEECH OF THOMAS PAINE.

As delivered in the Convention, July 7, 1795.
Wherein he alludes to the preceding Work.

ON the motion of Lanthenas, "That permission be granted to Thomas Paine, to deliver his sentiments on the Declaration of Rights and the Constitution, Thomas Paine attended; an opposition being made to the motion, one of the Secretaries, who stood by Mr. Paine, read his speech, of which the following is a literal translation:

CITIZENS,

The effects of a malignant fever, with which I was afflicted during a rigorous confinement in the Luxembourg, have thus long prevented me from attending at my post in the bosom of the Convention, and the magnitude of the subject under discussion, and no other consideration on earth, could induce me now to repair to my station.

A recurrence to the vicissitudes I have experienced, and the critical situations in which I have been placed in consequence of the French Revolution, will throw upon what I now propose to submit to the Convention, the most unequivocal proofs of my integrity, and the rectitude of those principles which have uniformly influenced my conduct.

In England I was proscribed for having vindicated the French Revolution, and I have suffered a rigorous imprisonment in France for having pursued a similar moit

mode of conduct. During the reign of terrorism, I was a close prisoner for eight-long months, and remained so above three months after the era of the 10th Thermidor. I ought, however, to state, that I was not persecuted by the people either of England or France. The proceedings in both countries were the effects of the despotism existing in their respective governments. But, even if my perfection had originated in the people at large, my principles and conduct would still have remained the same. Principles which are influenced and judged to the control of tyranny have not their foundation in the heart.

A few days ago I transmitted to you, by the ordinary mode of distribution, a short Treatise, entitled "Dissertation on the First Principles of Government." This little work I did intend to have dedicated to the people of Holland, who, about the time I began to write it, were determined to accomplish a Revolution in their Government,—rather than to the people of France, who had long before effected that glorious object. But there are, in the Constitution which is about to be ratified by the Convention, certain articles, and in the report which preceded it, certain points, to repugnant to reason, and incompatible with the true principles of liberty, as to render this Treatise, drawn up for another purpose, applicable to the present occasion, and under this impression I presumed to submit it to your consideration.

If there be faults in the Constitution, it were better to expose them now, than to abide the event of their mischievous tendency; for certain it is, that the plan of the Constitution which has been presented to you is not confilient with the grand object of the Revolution; nor congenial to the sentiments of the individuals who accomplished it.

To deprive half the people in a nation of their rights as citizens, is as easy matter in theory or on paper; but it is a moist dangerous experiment, and rarely practicable in the execution.

I shall

---

**Right page (35):**

I shall now, proceed to the observations I have to offer on this important subject; and I pledge myself that they shall be neither numerous nor dilative.

In my apprehension, a constitution contains two distinct parts or objects, the *Principle* and the *Practice*; and it is not only an efficient, but an indispensable provision, that the practice should emanate from, and accord with the principle. Now I maintain, that the entire of this proposition is the case in the plan of the Constitution under discussion. The first article, for instance, of the POLITICAL STATE OF CITIZENS, (v. TITLE II. OF THE CONSTITUTION) [says]:

"Every man born and resident in France, who, being twenty-one years of age, has inscribed on the Register of his Canton, and who has lived afterwards one year on the territory of the Republic, and who pays any direct contribution whatsoever, real or personal, is a French citizen."

I might here ask, if those only who come under the above description are to be confounded as citizens, what designation do you mean to give the rest of the people? I allude to that portion of the people on whom the principal part of the labour falls, and on whom the weight of indirect taxation will in the first instance press. In the furniture of the local fabric, this class of people are infinitely superior to that of the whole body qualification is their wealth or territorial possessions. For what is trade without cultivation? And what is the produce of the land without manufacturers? But to turn to the subject.

In the first place, this article is incompatible with the three first articles of the Declaration of Rights, which precede the Constitutional part.

The first article of the Declaration of Rights [says:—

"The end of society is the public good; and the institution of government is to secure to every individual the enjoyment of his rights."

But

---

**Notable markings:**

- Page numbers: ( 34 ) and ( 35 ) at top
- Faint stamp/seal visible on left page (appears to be institutional marking)
- Visible age-related staining and foxing throughout both pages

---

# Transcription

**Left Page (36)**

But the article of the Constitution to which I have just advertised proposes as the object of the tax, the public good, or in other words, the good of all, but a partial good, or the good only of a few; and the Constitution provides solely for the rights of this few, to the exclusion of the many.

The second article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"The Rights of Man in society are Liberty, Equality, Security of his person and property."

But the article alluded to in the Constitution has a direct tendency to establish the converse of this position, inasmuch as the persons excluded by this provision can neither be laid to possess liberty, nor security against oppression. They are condemned justly to the caprice and tyranny of the rest.

The third article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"Liberty consists in such acts of volition, as are not injurious to others."

But the article of the Constitution, on which I have observed, breaks down this barrier. In enabling the liberty of one part of society to destroy the freedom of the other.

Having thus pointed out the inconsistency of this article to the Declaration of Rights, I shall proceed to comment on that part of the same article which makes a direct contribution a necessary qualification to the right of citizenship.

A modern document on the object of public revenue has divided the taxes or contributions into two duties, the direct and the indirect, without being able to define precisely the distinction, or difference between them, because the effect of both is the same.

Those are designated indirect taxes which fall upon the consumers of certain articles, on which the tax is imposed, because the tax being included in the price, the consumer pays it without taking notice of it.

The same observation is applicable to the territorial tax. The land proprietors, in order to reimburse them-

**Right Page (37)**

selves, will rack-rent their tenants: the farmer, of course, will transfer the obligation to the miller, by enhancing the price of grain; the miller to the baker, by increasing the price of flour; and the baker to the consumer, by raising the price of bread. The territorial tax, therefore, though called *direct*, is in its consequence *indirect*.

To this tax the land proprietor contributes only in proportion to the quantity of bread and other provisions that are consumed in his own family. The defecit is furnished by the great mass of the community, which comprehends every individual of the nation.

From the logical distinction between the direct and indirect taxation, some emolument may result. I allow, to auditors of public accounts, &c. but to the people a large capital in their respective pursuits, can ever, according to this system, attain the pre-eminence of a citizen. On the other hand, any upstart, who has by speculation or management, got possession of a few acres of land, or a miserable tenement, may exultingly exercise the function of a citizen, although perhaps he neither possesses a hundredth part of the worth of property of a simple mechanic, nor contributes in any proportion to the exigencies of the state.

The
