## English Translation:

(Reverse side of the bond. Large denomination lettering across the top-left; the numeral "50" appears in a medallion at upper right and in a rosette at lower center.)

**FIFTY RUBLES**

**50**

**CONDITIONS OF THE STATE INTERNAL LOTTERY LOAN OF 1982**

1. The State Internal Lottery Loan of 1982 is issued for a term from 1 January 1982 to 1 January 2002.

2. The bonds of the loan are freely sold and purchased by the savings banks.

3. The loan is issued in separate series-blocks (razryady), of 500 million rubles in each block. Each block of the loan consists of 100,000 series, from number 200001 to number 300000. The bonds in each series bear numbers from 01 to 100.

4. The bonds of the loan are issued in denominations of 100, 50, and 25 rubles. A bond of 100 rubles consists of two fifty-ruble bonds of one series bearing two numbers. A bond of 25 rubles is one half of a fifty-ruble bond.

5. On the bonds of the loan the entire income is paid in the form of prizes. The total amount of prizes is set, on average over the twenty-year term of the loan, at the rate of 3 percent per year.

Over the twenty-year term of the loan, 32 percent of all bonds win prizes, while the remaining 68 percent of the bonds, upon expiry of the loan's term, are redeemed at their nominal value. Bonds that have won are retired and excluded from further prize drawings.

Prizes on the loan are set in the amounts of 10,000, 5,000, 2,500, 1,000, 500, 250, and 100 rubles per fifty-ruble bond, inclusive of the nominal value of the bond.

When prizes of equal amount — 100, 250, 500 rubles — fall to all numbers of a winning series on a hundred-ruble bond, which bears two numbers, 200, 500, 1,000 rubles respectively are paid out; when a prize of 500, 1,000, 2,500, 5,000, or 10,000 rubles falls to one of the numbers of a bond, a prize of 100 rubles falls to the second number, and on the hundred-ruble bond 600, 1,100, 2,600, 5,100, or 10,100 rubles respectively are paid out. On a bond of 25 rubles denomination, half of the prize that fell to the fifty-ruble bond is paid out.

6. Over the twenty-year term of the loan, 160 prize drawings are held — 8 drawings annually. The drawings are held beginning in 1982, on dates established by the Ministry of Finance of the USSR.

7. In the prize drawings, for each series-block of the loan the following number of prizes is drawn:

(Table. Stub heading: "Amount of prize per fifty-ruble bond (including the nominal value of the bond)". Column group heading: "Drawn", divided into "in one drawing" and "in all 160 drawings"; each divided into "number of prizes" and "amount of prizes (in rubles)".)

| Amount of prize per fifty-ruble bond (incl. nominal value) | In one drawing — number of prizes | In one drawing — amount (rubles) | In all 160 drawings — number of prizes | In all 160 drawings — amount (rubles) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10,000 rubles | 1 | 10,000 | 160 | 1,600,000 |

8. Bonds on which prizes have fallen, as well as non-winning bonds subject to redemption at their nominal value, may be presented for payment until 1 January 2004. After the expiry of this term, bonds not presented for payment lose their validity and are not subject to payment.

9. The sale of bonds is carried out at prices established by the Ministry of Finance of the USSR, and the purchase is at nominal value.

title: Nationalist Government Whampoa Harbour Lottery Bond, Second Issue, Canton, 1926
description: This is a five-yuan (伍圓) lottery public bond (有奬公債), the Second Issue (第貳次) floated by the Ministry of Finance of the Nationalist Government (國民政府財政部) of China, dated the first day of the eighth month of Year 15 of the Republic (中華民國十五年八月一日發行 = 1 August 1926). The summary of regulations (條例摘要) states that the government issued this second series, totalling ten million yuan, expressly to develop the commercial harbour at Whampoa (黃埔商港) in fulfilment of the late Premier Sun Yat-sen's construction plan, with the debt secured on national revenue assigned to the Central Bank. Rather than paying fixed coupons, principal is retired in three stages through bi-monthly drawings that also distribute cash prizes to lucky holders—a lottery-redemption structure meant to attract subscribers. Signed by Finance Minister T. V. Soong (宋子文) and Council Chairman Wang Jingwei (汪兆銘), the note dates the Canton government to its revolutionary year on the eve of the Northern Expedition; its dense purple guilloche borders and large central denomination project the fiscal credibility a fledgling regime needed to borrow.
creator: Republic of China, Ministry of Finance
notes: Republic of China National Government, Ministry of Finance, Third Patriotic Lottery Bond (Zhonghua Minguo Zhengfu Caizhengbu Di San Ci Aiguo Jiangquan); 5 Yuan (Wu Yuan); issued January 8, 1926 (Republic of China Year 15); No. 0439850
transcription:
# Nationalist Government Ministry of Finance Second Lottery Public Bond, Five Yuan — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

(Upper curved banner, arc reading right→left:)
**國民政府財政部第貳次有奬公債**

(Lower curved banner, arc reading right→left:)
**中華民國十五年八月一日發行**

(Large central medallion:)
**伍圓**

(Denomination character 伍 printed large in each of the four corner roundels.)

(Right-hand text block, vertical columns read right→left; heading in large characters at the far right:)

**條例摘要**

一　國民政府為開闢黃埔商港實行　先總理建設計劃特由財政部續發第二次有奬公債壹千萬圓

二　此項有奬公債由財政部以國家收入撥交中央銀行為還本給奬之擔保並由中央銀行負完全責任

三　此項有奬公債分三期還本以十個月為一期第一期每月還本百分之二第二期每月還本百分之三第三期每月還本百分之五至第三十個月全數還清每屆還本由財政部抽籤定之抽籤後於還本票中再行開獎其還本給奬事宜由財政部委託中央銀行及殷實商號辦理

四　中籤及中獎債票如逾三個月不領欵即作無效

五　此項有奬公債之利息全數撥充獎金計一等獎一張獎五萬元二等獎二張各給獎貳千元三等獎四張各給獎伍百元四等獎十張各給獎壹百元五等獎二十張各給獎伍拾元六等獎五十張各給獎叁拾元七等獎壹百張各給獎拾伍元八等獎三百張各給獎拾元九等獎六百張各給獎伍元十等獎除前列九等獎外其餘每次中籤債票均各給獎金五毫

(Left-hand text block, vertical columns read right→left — item 五's prize list continues here, followed by items 六–八 and the signatures:)

六　此項有奬公債於發行日起定兩個月募集即行抽籤還本在未沽銷完竣時為優待捷足應募者起見得增加特別抽獎其辦法由財政部另定之

七　此項有奬公債為無記名式得自由買賣並作公務上保證金銀行准儲金及中央銀行抵押政欵之抵押品

八　經理此項公債之官吏或商民對於此項公債如有損毀信用之行為按照刑律治罪

(Two signature columns at far left, each with a cursive signature and a red square seal in seal script below:)
國民政府委員主席　汪兆銘　(red seal, seal script — illegible)
國民政府財政部長　宋子文　(red seal, seal script — illegible)

(Bottom centre, in red:)
**No 0439850**

## English Translation:

(Upper banner:) **Nationalist Government Ministry of Finance, Second Issue Lottery Public Bond** (Guómín Zhèngfǔ Cáizhèngbù Dì-èr-cì Yǒujiǎng Gōngzhài)

(Lower banner:) **Issued on the 1st day of the 8th month of the 15th year of the Republic of China** (1 August 1926)

(Centre:) **Five Yuan**

**Summary of Regulations**

1. In order to open up the Whampoa (Huángpǔ) Commercial Harbour and carry out the construction plan of the late Premier [Sun Yat-sen], the Nationalist Government hereby, through the Ministry of Finance, issues this second series of Lottery Public Bond, totalling ten million yuan.

2. For this lottery bond, the Ministry of Finance assigns national revenue to the Central Bank as security for redemption of principal and payment of prizes, and the Central Bank bears full responsibility therefor.

3. This lottery bond is redeemed in three stages, each stage being ten months. In the first stage 2% of principal is repaid each month; in the second stage 3% each month; in the third stage 5% each month, so that the whole is fully repaid by the thirtieth month. Each redemption is determined by lot drawn by the Ministry of Finance; after the drawing, a further prize drawing is held among the bonds due for redemption. Redemption and prize matters are entrusted by the Ministry of Finance to the Central Bank and to reputable commercial firms.

4. Bonds drawn for redemption and prize-winning bonds shall become void if the money is not collected within three months.

5. The interest on this lottery bond is applied in full to prize money, as follows: First prize, 1 ticket, prize of 50,000 yuan; Second prize, 2 tickets, 2,000 yuan each; Third prize, 4 tickets, 500 yuan each; Fourth prize, 10 tickets, 100 yuan each; Fifth prize, 20 tickets, 50 yuan each; Sixth prize, 50 tickets, 30 yuan each; Seventh prize, 100 tickets, 15 yuan each; Eighth prize, 300 tickets, 10 yuan each; Ninth prize, 600 tickets, 5 yuan each; Tenth prize — apart from the nine prize classes listed above, every other bond drawn shall each receive prize money of five mao (0.5 yuan).

6. From the date of issue, a subscription period of two months is fixed for this lottery bond, after which the drawing for redemption is immediately conducted. While the bonds are not yet fully sold, in order to reward the fleetest subscribers, additional special drawings may be held, the method to be separately determined by the Ministry of Finance.

7. This lottery bond is in bearer form; it may be freely bought and sold, and may serve as an official-service guarantee deposit, as a bank reserve deposit, and as collateral for Central Bank advances.

8. Any official or merchant managing this bond who commits an act damaging the credit of this bond shall be punished according to the criminal law.

(Signatures:)
Chairman of the Nationalist Government Council — Wang Zhaoming (Wang Jingwei) (red seal)
Minister of Finance of the Nationalist Government — Song Ziwen (T. V. Soong) (red seal)

(Serial number, in red:) No. 0439850

title: Republic of China Nationalist Government Lottery Bond
description: A lottery bond of five dollars in Canton currency, issued under the Second Nationalist Government Lottery Loan in the fifteenth year of the Republic of China, namely 1926. The loan, totalling $10,000,000 and sanctioned by Nationalist Government Mandate of 25 June 1926, was raised to finance construction of the Port of Whampoa near Canton, and was secured on the revenues of the Nationalist Government with the Central Bank of China as Government Depositary. Repayment ran over thirty monthly instalments, with public drawings held on the fifth of each month at the General Chamber of Commerce, Canton; the winning numbers then entered a second drawing for prizes ranging from a single first prize of $50,000 down to six hundred ninth prizes of $5, undrawn-but-blanked bonds earning a uniform fifty cents' interest. The instrument fuses public borrowing with the lottery's allure, mobilising small subscribers for the Kuomintang's revolutionary regime in Canton on the eve of the Northern Expedition. The English text and restrained green guilloché border address foreign and treaty-port investors. Dated 1 August 1926 and signed by Wang Ching Wai (Wang Jingwei), Chairman of the Nationalist Government, and the Minister of Finance.
creator: Republic of China, Nationalist Government
notes: Republic of China Second Nationalist Government Lottery Loan – $5 Bond, Canton Currency (1926)
transcription:
# SECOND NATIONALIST GOVERNMENT LOTTERY LOAN
## OF THE
### FIFTEENTH YEAR OF THE REPUBLIC OF CHINA, BEING THE YEAR 1926.
#### BOND FOR FIVE DOLLARS, CANTON CURRENCY.

The Regulation of the Lottery Loan for financing the construction of the Port of Whampoa was sanctioned by the Nationalist Government Mandate of the 25th June, 1926.

A summary of the said Regulations appears below:—

**Amount issued:—** $10,000,000.
**Denomination:—** $5.
**Security:—** The loan is secured on the revenue of the Nationalist Government, and the Central Bank of China, which is the Government Depository, unconditionally guarantees the payment of prizes and the repayment of principal at maturity.

**Repayment:—** The loan is repaid in thirty monthly instalments, without interest.
- 1st to 10th month 2½ of the bonds are drawn each month.
- 11th " 20th " 3% " " " "
- 21st " 30th " 3½ " " " "

Public drawings for prizes are held on the 5th day of each month, at the General Chamber of Commerce, Canton, and directly after the winning numbers are drawn a second drawing will be made from the said winning numbers for the awarding of prizes.

The following prizes will be drawn each month:—
- 1 First Prize......................... $ 50,000.— | 50 Sixth Prizes, each $ 30............ $ 1,500.—
- 2 Second Prizes, each $ 2,000...... $ 4,000.— | 100 Seventh " " $ 15............. $ 1,500.—
- 5 Third, " $ 500............. $ 2,500.— | 300 Eighth " " $ 10............. $ 3,000.—
- 10 Fourth, " $ 100............. $ 1,000.— | 600 Ninth " " $ 5.............. $ 3,000.—
- 20 Fifth, " $ 50............... $ 1,000.—

All bonds, which have been drawn for repayment of principal, can be used as security for loans at 50 cents each, in addition to the repayment of principal.

Drawn Bonds should be presented to the Central Bank of China, Canton, for repayment of principal and payment of prizes five days after the drawing, and not later than three months thereafter.

**Use of Goods:—** The Bonds are transferable, and may be bought, sold or mortgaged, or used as security or reserve fund for public transactions.

Dated the 1st day of the 8th Month of the 15th Year of the Republic of China, being the 1st of August, 1926.

- **Signatures:** 
  - Lower left: "Wang Chung Wei" (Chairman of the Nationalist Government)
  - Lower right: "H.H. Kung" (Minister of Finance)

- **Stamps:** Purple circular stamps visible on upper right portion of document

- **Decorative border:** Ornate engraved border with repeating pattern and corner medallions featuring decorative elements

title: Imperial Russian Government 4% Consolidated Railway Bond, 1889
description: A four-percent consolidated railway bond of the Imperial Russian Government (Консолидированныя Российскія Четырёхпроцентныя Желѣзнодорожныя Облигаціи, 1st Series), with a face value of 125 gold rubles bearing interest at four percent. The instrument forms part of a vast consolidated loan of 175 million gold rubles, issued on the authority of the Imperial Ukase of 20 February 1889 to refund and unify the Treasury's scattered railway debts. The denomination is restated for every market in which the paper was placed: 500 francs, 404 German marks, 19 pounds 15 shillings 6 pence sterling, 239 Dutch guilders, and 96.25 gold dollars — a ledger of the foreign capitals on which Russian railway expansion depended. Printed in dark brown, crowned by the imperial double-headed eagle, the bond's sober engraving and multilingual valuation announce a borrower courting the savers of Paris, Berlin, London and Amsterdam at once.
creator: Imperial Russian Government

записанная въ Государственную Долговую Книгу, кн. *10* стр. *102* *(book/page nos. handwritten in ink)*

**НА ПРЕДЪЯВИТЕЛЯ.**

*(red French fiscal stamp, right:)* 31 PARIS 1898 / 50% COMPLÉMENT DE DROIT / 10

ВЛАДѢЛЕЦЪ сей облигаціи имѣетъ право на сумму сто двадцать пять рублей золотомъ, приносящую **четыре** процента въ годъ дохода до погашенія, освобожденную навсегда отъ всякихъ русскихъ налоговъ. Проценты уплачиваются по третямъ въ годъ, 20 Марта / 1 Апрѣля, 19 Іюня / 1 Іюля, 19 Сентября / 1 Октября, 20 Декабря / 1 Января, по предъявленіи купоновъ, по желанію владѣльца:

въ С.-ПЕТЕРБУРГѢ въ ГОСУДАРСТВЕННОМЪ БАНКѢ;
въ ПАРИЖѢ у Гг. братьевъ Ротшильдъ;
въ ЛОНДОНѢ у Гг. Н. М. Ротшильдъ и сыновья;
въ БЕРЛИНѢ въ Учетномъ Обществѣ и у Г-на С. Блейхредеръ;
во ФРАНКФУРТѢ на М. у Гг. М. А. фонъ-Ротшильдъ и сыновья;
въ АМСТЕРДАМѢ за счетъ Гг. братьевъ Ротшильдъ;
въ БРЮССЕЛѢ } въ мѣстахъ, которыя будутъ назначены
въ НЬЮ-ЮРКѢ } ими для этой цѣли.

Облигаціи сіи подлежатъ выкупу по нарицательной цѣнѣ [...] 0,084281% съ нарицательной суммы займа, съ присовокупленіемъ [...]. Тиражъ облигацій производится въ Государственномъ [Банкѣ] каждаго года, начиная съ 20 Декабря 1889 / 1 Января 1890 года, въ теченіе 81 года. Для этой цѣли отчисляется въ каждое полугодіе [...] 2% на всѣ погашенныя облигаціи. [Коммиссіею] Погашенія Долговъ [тиражъ производится] 19 Іюня / 1 Іюля и 20 Декабря / 1 Января [...].

Уплата капитала по вышедшимъ въ тиражъ облигаціямъ производится въ слѣдующій за тиражемъ срокъ платежа процентовъ, въ тѣхъ же городахъ и въ тѣхъ же мѣстахъ, какъ и уплата купоновъ. Купоны, срокъ коимъ истекаетъ послѣ срока уплаты капитала [...]; въ случаѣ [...] сумма недостающихъ купоновъ будетъ удержана при уплатѣ капитала. Облигаціи сіи снабжены купонами до 19 Іюня / 1 Іюля 1900 [года]. По истеченіи срока купонныхъ листовъ предъявителю [облигаціи] будутъ выданы безплатно новые купонные листы, въ мѣстахъ [...]. Облигаціи сіи, вмѣстѣ съ купонами, должны быть представлены [для полученія капитала]; въ противномъ случаѣ капиталъ по вышедшей въ тиражъ облигаціи [не выдается]. Купоны [...] года и талономъ для возобновленія купоновъ. Купоны отъ облигацій, которыя до тѣхъ поръ не вышли въ тиражъ, [оплачиваются]. Правила относительно обращенія именныхъ облигацій въ предъявительскія и обратно утверждаются Министромъ Финансовъ.

issued on the authority of the Imperial Ukase of 20 February 1889,

*(composition of the issue:)* in the quantity of: 505,390 sheets of one bond each (of 125 r.), Nos. 000001 to 505390; 28,317 sheets of 5 bonds each (of 625 r.), Nos. 505391 to 646975; 40,900 sheets of 10 bonds each (of 1,250 r.), Nos. 646976 to 1055975; and 13,761 sheets of 25 bonds each (of 3,125 r.), Nos. 1055976 to 1400000,

*(fine print listing the railway loans being consolidated)* for the conversion and redemption of the 5% Consolidated bonds of the Russian railways, 1st and 2nd issues, realized for the railways: the Moscow–Brest, Odessa, Riga–Bolderaa, Tambov–Saratov, [...] and Poti–Tiflis lines; as well as part of the bonds of the 1st, 2nd, 4th and 7th issues, realized for the railways: the Ivanovo–Kineshma, Gryazi–Tsaritsyn, [...], Moscow–Kursk, Kiev–Brest, Lozovaya–Sevastopol, Landvarovo–Romny, Kharkov–Nikolaev, Vologda, Mitau, Ryazhsk–Vyazma, Rostov–Vladikavkaz, Morshansk–Syzran, Kozlov–Voronezh–Rostov, Siberian, Catherine (Ekaterininskaya), Baskunchak, Ekaterinburg–[Tyumen?], [...] Ural, Lozovaya, Orel–Gryazi[, ...] lines.

---

**1st SERIES. A. BOND — 125 GOLD RUBLES.**

No. 236902

**FOR ONE HUNDRED AND TWENTY-FIVE RUBLES IN GOLD**

= 500 francs = 404 German marks = £19 15s. 6d. sterling = 239 Dutch guilders = 96.25 gold dollars,

registered in the State Debt Book, book no. *10*, page *102* *(book/page nos. entered by hand)*

**TO BEARER.**

*(red French fiscal stamp:)* 31 PARIS 1898 / 50% SUPPLEMENTARY DUTY / 10

The HOLDER of this bond is entitled to the sum of one hundred and twenty-five gold rubles, yielding **four** percent per annum in income until redemption, forever exempt from all Russian taxes. Interest is paid in thirds of the year, on 20 March / 1 April, 19 June / 1 July, 19 September / 1 October, 20 December / 1 January, upon presentation of the coupons, at the holder's option:

at ST. PETERSBURG at the STATE BANK;
at PARIS at Messrs. Rothschild Brothers;
at LONDON at Messrs. N. M. Rothschild & Sons;
at BERLIN at the Discount Company and at Mr. S. Bleichröder's;
at FRANKFURT-am-Main at Messrs. M. A. von Rothschild & Sons;
at AMSTERDAM for the account of Messrs. Rothschild Brothers;
at BRUSSELS } at places which shall be designated
at NEW YORK } by them for this purpose.

These bonds are subject to redemption at their nominal value [...] 0.084281% of the nominal amount of the loan, with the addition of [...]. The drawing of the bonds is carried out at the State [Bank] each year, beginning on 20 December 1889 / 1 January 1890, over the course of 81 years. For this purpose there is set aside in each half-year [...] 2% on all redeemed bonds. The [Commission] for the Redemption of Debts [carries out the drawing] on 19 June / 1 July and 20 December / 1 January [...].

Payment of the capital on bonds drawn is made at the interest-payment date following the drawing, in the same cities and the same places as the payment of coupons. Coupons falling due after the date of capital repayment [...]; in the event [...] the amount of the missing coupons will be withheld upon payment of the capital. These bonds are furnished with coupons up to 19 June / 1 July 1900. Upon expiry of the coupon sheet, new coupon sheets will be issued to the bearer free of charge, at the places [...]. These bonds, together with their coupons, must be presented [to obtain the capital]; otherwise the capital on the drawn bond [is not paid]. The coupons [...] and a talon for the renewal of the coupons. Coupons of bonds that have not yet been drawn [are paid]. Rules concerning the conversion of registered bonds into bearer bonds and vice versa are approved by the Minister of Finance.

**Manager of the State Commission for the Redemption of Debts** [signature — illegible]

**Accountant** [signature — illegible]

Extract from the Imperial Ukase of 20 February 1889 addressed to the Minister of Finance.

title: Imperial Russian Government Consolidated Railroad Bond (Conditions)
description: This sheet sets out the conditions and the bearer certificate of an Imperial Government of Russia Consolidated 4 per cent Railroad Bond, Series 1, in the denomination of 125 gold roubles (rendered as 500 Francs, 404 German Marks, 19 Pounds Sterling 15 shillings 6 pence, 239 Dutch Florins, and 96.25 United States gold dollars). Printed in parallel French, German, and English, it was issued under the Imperial Ukase of 20 February / 4 March 1889 as part of a vast loan of 175 million gold roubles (700 million Francs), divided into 505,390 titles. Interest, paid quarterly and free of every Russian tax, runs at four per cent until redemption by drawing. The loan consolidated the 5 per cent obligations of numerous state-backed railways, payable across St. Petersburg, Paris, London, Berlin, Frankfurt, Amsterdam, and New York. The plain typographic frame and multilingual columns reveal an instrument conceived less as ornament than as a passport into the great money markets of Europe, by which a fiscally pressed empire refinanced costly railway debt on cheaper foreign terms.
creator: Imperial Russian Government

title: Shanghai Pudong Qiangsheng Taxi Co. Share Certificate (Shanghai, 1992)
description: A share certificate (股票) of the Shanghai Pudong Qiangsheng Taxi Co., Ltd. (上海浦東強生出租汽車股份有限公司), representing ten shares of ten yuan each, a face value of one hundred yuan in renminbi (本股票拾股計人民幣壹佰元整). The total registered capital is stated as 1,100,000 shares, or 11,000,000 yuan. Issued at Shanghai and dated the twelfth of February 1992 (一九九二年二月十二日), it bears the approval of the People's Bank of China, Shanghai Branch, together with the signed seal of the company's legal representative. The certificate belongs to the earliest wave of joint-stock conversions among Shanghai service enterprises following the resumption of share trading, when the city's taxi firms were among the first to raise public capital. The green vignette of motor coaches and sedans plainly advertises the business whose shares the holder owns.
creator: Shanghai Pu Dong Qiang Sheng Taxi Co., Ltd.

title: Shanghai Taxi Company Stock Certificate (Reverse)
description: The reverse of a share certificate of the Shanghai Pu Dong Qiang Sheng Taxi Co., Ltd. (上海浦東強生出租汽車股份有限公司), each full certificate representing ten shares of ten yuan, or one hundred yuan renminbi in all, issued at Shanghai and dated to 1992 on the obverse. The face carries no financial terms but two administrative apparatus essential to a registered equity. A printed notice (說明) in three clauses declares the stock an ordinary share offered by the company to work units and individuals, valid only when bearing the company seal, and forbidden as a circulating instrument: it may not be transferred, bought, sold, gifted, inherited, or mortgaged except through the designated registrar and the securities authorities. Below, a ruled 股份轉讓記錄 (transfer-of-shares record) awaits entries of date, transferor, transferee, and transfer agent. A red company chop and a guilloche security ground confirm authenticity. The document records an early, tightly controlled experiment in joint-stock ownership during China's market reforms, when equity was permitted yet hedged against free trading.

title: Société de l'Ikelemba Share Statutes (Congo Free State)
description: Printed extract of the statutes (Extrait des Statuts) of an anonymous joint-stock company, the Société de l'Ikelemba, set in French in two columns within an ornamental engraved border. Article 1 constitutes the société anonyme; Article 2 fixes its registered office in the Congo (État Indépendant du Congo), in the Ikelemba river basin, with an administrative seat at Brussels. The capital is set at 500,000 francs, divided into 5,000 capital shares of 100 francs each, alongside 5,000 dividend (founders') shares of no par value. Louis Lamberts contributes the company's right to acquire some 1,000 hectares of land in the Ikelemba basin, receiving two-tenths of the dividend shares in return. The first financial year closes 31 December 1899 and the first annual general meeting is to be held in 1900, dating the constitution to 1899. The text reflects the concession-company model by which Leopold II's Congo Free State licensed private syndicates to exploit equatorial land and produce. The restrained typographic frame, with scrolled corner flourishes and no allegorical imagery, presents the venture as a sober legal instrument rather than a speculative prospectus.
creator: Unknown Belgian Congo company
notes: Belgian/French company document, Extrait des Statuts (corporate statutes extract) in French; references to Louis Lambert, general assembly in Brussels, dividends and board of directors; p.1/2
transcription:
# Extract of the Statutes of the Société de l'Ikelemba — Transcription

## Original:

(Printed extract set within an ornamental engraved border with scrolled corner flourishes; a faint intaglio image shows through from the reverse. The text is arranged in two columns beneath a centered display heading.)

**EXTRAIT DES STATUTS**

(small ornament — ⋇ — beneath the heading)

*(Left column:)*

**ARTICLE PREMIER.** — Il est constitué, par les présentes, une société anonyme sous la dénomination de L'Ikelemba.

**ART. 2.** — La Société a son siège social au Congo (Etat Indépendant), dans le bassin de l'Ikelemba, et son siège administratif en Belgique, à Bruxelles ou dans l'agglomération bruxelloise.

Elle pourra avoir des sièges administratifs en tous autres pays où s'exercera son industrie ou son commerce.

**ART. 3.** — La Société est fondée pour une durée de trente ans, mais elle peut prendre des engagements et acquérir des concessions pour un terme excédant cette durée.

Celle-ci pourra être prolongée ou réduite comme il sera dit à l'article 34 des présents statuts.

**ART. 5.** — Le capital social est fixé à 500,000 francs, représenté par 5,000 actions de capital de 100 francs chacune.

Il est, en outre, créé 5,000 actions de dividende sans désignation de valeur.

M. Louis Lamberts, prénommé, fait apport à la Société de l'Ikelemba du droit d'acquérir, pour elle, un bloc de 1,000 hectares de terres, dont la propriété a été demandée par lui et dont les comparants déclarent avoir connaissance, à déterminer dans le bassin de l'Ikelemba.

En rémunération de cet apport, il lui est attribué les deux dixièmes des actions de dividende.

Les 4,000 actions de dividende restantes sont attribuées aux souscripteurs, ci-après, des 5,000 actions de capital, proportionnellement à leur souscription.

Toutes les actions de dividende sont inaliénables pendant les deux premières années après la date des présentes, à moins d'une autorisation expresse du Conseil d'administration.

Le nombre des actions de dividende ne pourra jamais être augmenté, pas même par voie de modification aux statuts.

*(Right column:)*

**ART. 30.** — Les assemblées générales se réunissent à Bruxelles ou dans l'un de ses faubourgs, au lieu désigné par le Conseil d'administration.

L'assemblée générale annuelle se tient le dernier samedi de juin de chaque année, à 3 heures de relevée.

La première assemblée générale annuelle aura lieu en 1900.

**ART. 37.** — Au 31 décembre de chaque année, et pour la première fois le 31 décembre 1899, les livres de la Société sont arrêtés et le Conseil d'administration dresse le bilan.

**ART. 41.** — L'excédent favorable du bilan, déduction faite de tous les frais généraux, constitue le bénéfice de la Société ; ce bénéfice sera réparti comme suit :

1. 5 p. c. pour la réserve légale, jusqu'à ce qu'elle ait atteint la dixième partie du capital social ;

Le Conseil d'administration pourra augmenter cette réserve et, en outre, créer une réserve spéciale ou un fonds de prévision ;

2. Un premier dividende de 5 p. c. sur la partie appelée des actions de capital, dans le cas où les bénéfices nets d'une année ne seraient pas suffisants pour payer ce dividende aux actions de capital ; la somme qui manquera sera prélevée, sans intérêts de retard, sur les bénéfices des années subséquentes, après dotation de la réserve légale.

Le solde se répartit comme suit :

2 p. c. à chaque administrateur et à chaque commissaire un tiers du tantième revenant à chaque administrateur.

Le minimum à attribuer à chaque administrateur et à chaque commissaire est réservé à la décision de l'assemblée générale, qui se tiendra immédiatement après la constitution de la Société.

Le surplus sera distribué par moitié aux actions de capital et par moitié aux actions de dividende.

(A decorative scrolled flourish is printed below the two columns, centered.)

---

## English Translation:

**EXTRACT OF THE STATUTES**

*(Left column:)*

**ARTICLE ONE.** — There is hereby constituted, by these presents, a joint-stock company (société anonyme) under the name of L'Ikelemba.

**ART. 2.** — The Company has its registered office in the Congo (Independent State), in the basin of the Ikelemba, and its administrative seat in Belgium, at Brussels or in the Brussels agglomeration.

It may have administrative offices in any other countries where it carries on its industry or its commerce.

**ART. 3.** — The Company is founded for a term of thirty years, but it may enter into commitments and acquire concessions for a term exceeding this duration.

The latter may be extended or reduced as shall be stated in article 34 of the present statutes.

**ART. 5.** — The share capital is fixed at 500,000 francs, represented by 5,000 capital shares of 100 francs each.

There are, moreover, created 5,000 dividend shares without par value.

Mr. Louis Lamberts, named above, contributes to the Société de l'Ikelemba the right to acquire, for it, a block of 1,000 hectares of land, the ownership of which has been applied for by him and of which the appearing parties declare they have knowledge, to be determined in the basin of the Ikelemba.

In remuneration for this contribution, there are allotted to him two-tenths of the dividend shares.

The remaining 4,000 dividend shares are allotted to the subscribers, hereafter, of the 5,000 capital shares, in proportion to their subscription.

All dividend shares are inalienable during the first two years after the date of these presents, unless by express authorization of the Board of Administration.

The number of dividend shares may never be increased, not even by way of amendment to the statutes.

*(Right column:)*

**ART. 30.** — General meetings are held at Brussels or in one of its suburbs, at the place designated by the Board of Administration.

The annual general meeting is held on the last Saturday of June of each year, at 3 o'clock in the afternoon.

The first annual general meeting shall take place in 1900.

**ART. 37.** — On December 31 of each year, and for the first time on December 31, 1899, the books of the Company are closed and the Board of Administration draws up the balance sheet.

**ART. 41.** — The favorable surplus of the balance sheet, after deduction of all general expenses, constitutes the profit of the Company; this profit shall be distributed as follows:

1. 5 percent for the legal reserve, until it has reached one-tenth of the share capital;

The Board of Administration may increase this reserve and, in addition, create a special reserve or a contingency fund;

2. A first dividend of 5 percent on the called-up portion of the capital shares, in the case where the net profits of a year would not be sufficient to pay this dividend to the capital shares; the amount that is lacking shall be drawn, without interest for arrears, from the profits of subsequent years, after endowment of the legal reserve.

The balance is distributed as follows:

2 percent to each administrator and to each auditor, one-third of the share accruing to each administrator.

The minimum to be allotted to each administrator and to each auditor is reserved to the decision of the general meeting, which shall be held immediately after the constitution of the Company.

The surplus shall be distributed one-half to the capital shares and one-half to the dividend shares.

title: Belgian Congo Company Share, Coupon Sheet
description: This is the dividend-coupon sheet (the second of two pages) belonging to a share in a Belgian société anonyme operating in the Congo, the company recorded on its companion certificate. Thirty coupons, numbered 1 to 30 and arranged in three columns of ten, each sit within an ornamental engraved cartouche; all remain blank, neither dated nor filled with an amount, so the share was never cut for payment. The sheet embodies the mechanics of dividend distribution in the era before bank book-entry: each numbered slip was to be detached and surrendered in turn as successive dividends fell due, a paper ledger of the shareholder's future claims. The flowing, asymmetric foliate scrollwork framing each number, with its whiplash curves, reflects the Art Nouveau idiom then fashionable in Brussels, lending an artisanal elegance to an otherwise utilitarian instrument of colonial finance.

notes: Dividend/interest coupon sheet (p.2/2) for goetzmann1008; 30 numbered coupons (Nos. 1-30) in decorative engraved cartouches arranged in three columns

title: Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co. Receipt for Russian Railway Bond Talons
description: A printed receipt (kwitantie) of the Amsterdam banking house Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co., acknowledging that it has taken over ("overgenomen") from Heer Burdet, Druyvestejn the talons of 3 per cent. bonds of the Grand Russian Railway Company (Groote Russische Spoorweg Maatschappij), third emission of 1881, the talon values noted at Rb. 125 and Rb. 625. Issued at Amsterdam on 12 June 1924. The talon, the stub from which a fresh sheet of interest coupons was drawn once the old sheet was exhausted, here passes through an Amsterdam intermediary years after the 1917 revolution had repudiated Russia's foreign railway debt. A green admonition urges that the receipt be kept "voor eene eventueele afstempeling," and an older stamp records a coupon paid 1/14 June 1917, the dual date marking the gulf between the Julian and Gregorian calendars. The document is a small relic of Dutch holders still administering Russian securities whose value the new regime had already disowned.
creator: Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co.
notes: Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co. (Amsterdam) coupon talon receipt; Overgenomen van de Heer Burdet, Druyvestejn — Een Talons van 3 pCt. title: Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co. Receipt (Reverse)
description: This is the reverse of a printed receipt issued at Amsterdam by the banking house Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co., the firm having handled the renewal of interest coupons (talons) on 3 percent bonds of the Grand Russian Railway Company. The back carries only two markings: a boxed cancellation stamp reading "OORSPRONKELIJKE KWITANTIE AFGETEEKEND. L. R. & Co." ("original receipt signed off") and, in green, the formula "AAN DEN INHOUD VOLDAAN" ("satisfied as to the contents") above a place-and-date line "AMSTERDAM, 19[—]" left blank. The document speaks to Amsterdam's role as a clearing centre for Russian railway securities held by Dutch investors, where a bank's stamp converted a promise into a settled account. The near-empty field, a mere acknowledgement of payment, marks the quiet administrative end of a cross-border credit relationship.
creator: Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co.
notes: Reverse/back of goetzmann1010 (Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co. talon receipt); blank with stamps Oorspronkelijke Kwitantie Afgeteekend, L.R. & Co. (original receipt signed) and Aan den inhoud voldaan (satisfied as to content); Amsterdam

title: Hoogheemraadschap van den Lekdijk Bovendams Rentebrief
description: This document is an allonge—a coupon-renewal sheet appended to a rentebrief, the perpetual annuity bond of the Hoogheemraadschap van den Lekdijk Bovendams, the water board charged with maintaining the upper Lek river dike. The underlying obligation, drawn on a principal of one thousand guilders (f 1.000), yields twenty-five guilders (f 25) annually, a rate of 2.5 per cent, payable each year on 12 January. Issued ('Afgegeven') at Utrecht on 8 January 1944 and authenticated by the Dijkgraaf and Secretaris, the sheet exists because the original coupons were exhausted: the body of the document is a dense ledger of redeemed interest, its lettered entries running from January 1944 to 2003. Such water-board annuities are among the longest-lived securities in financial history, obligations rooted in the communal duty of dike upkeep rather than in any sovereign treasury, and still honouring their promise three centuries after the genre began. The engraved seal of LEKDIJK BOVENDAMS, bearing a lion-flanked shield, asserts the board's corporate authority over the perpetual debt.
creator: Hoogheemraadschap van den Lekdijk Bovendams
notes: Allonge (coupon renewal supplement) to rentebrieff folio 25, No. 74, for f 1,000 guilden; Hoogheemraadschap van den Lekdijk Bovendams (Lekdijk water board), Utrecht, Netherlands; annual interest f 25 payable January 12; issued January 8, 1944; signed by Dijkgraaf and Secretaris; accompanied by two handwritten coupon payment record sheets covering 1944-2003; No. 74

title: Dutch Manuscript Annuity Obligation (ca. 1703)
description: This heavily worn manuscript is a Dutch interest-bearing obligation, an obligatie or redeemable annuity (losrente), penned in an abbreviated notarial hand on heavy paper or parchment. The dense early-eighteenth-century Dutch body records a debt carrying fixed yearly interest of twenty-five guilders, payable each 12 January, and is subscribed by several parties, among them a Nicolaes and one styled van Roossbergh. Its true substance lies in the margins, where a long column of dated receipts logs that annual payment year after year: the entries run from 1703 down through the 1720s and onward across the head and right edge into the early 1780s. Issued around 1702 to 1703, the instrument testifies to the durability of the Dutch credit market, where such annuities passed between holders and quietly yielded their coupon for the better part of a century. Plain and unornamented, the sheet speaks not through emblem but through its accreted ledger of fidelity: a market that paid on the same January day for some eighty years.
creator: Unknown (Dutch)
notes: 18th-century Dutch manuscript bond on vellum, recto with principal text. See goetzmann1024 for verso. Together form a 2-page vellum manuscript bond document.

[illegible] … van Lochorst … acknowledge by these presents, in all [illegible] and after [illegible] resolution of [illegible], to have received [illegible] … the [interest?] of the aforesaid bond [illegible], and therewith the approbation of [illegible] to have the prior charge upon the [illegible] of Utrecht charged thereon [illegible] …

Nicolaes de Meijer, together with the [illegible] under-minute, alderman [illegible], formerly [illegible] received for Nicolaes de Meijer [illegible] guilders, of 25 [illegible], concerning the bond [illegible], offered [illegible], the said bond providing [illegible] yearly, each year, [illegible] annuity (losrente) to be paid [illegible] on the [illegible] of November 1640, and the other half on 12 May 1649, and so [illegible] the redemption of the same [illegible] until the discharge [illegible] all [illegible] money [illegible] …

[illegible legal formula] … renouncing [illegible] to the parties [illegible] bound [illegible] as above, the tenor of this [instrument] [illegible], the notary [illegible] to pass [illegible], certification [illegible], for myself and my descendants [illegible], resolution [illegible], the parties respectively [illegible] in this [illegible], so named in my capacity [illegible], under my seal [illegible].

**Closing:** [illegible] on the [illegible] of May [illegible], passed as above [illegible].

**Signature:** van Hooghenboeck.

**Ledger of receipts:** A running list of annual interest payments, each reading "Paid, 12 January [year]," almost all for the sum of 25 guilders. The receipts run without break from 1702 down the left margin and along the foot (1723–1744), continue up the right side into the 1750s, and resume along the head of the sheet, where lettered blocks (a, b, c …) carry the payments through the 1770s, 1780s and 1790s, the last legible entries reaching 1797–1799 and possibly 1800 — testifying to roughly a century of interest of 25 guilders paid each 12 January.

---

*Notes on conflicts with the supplied description:*
1. *The description dates the last marginal receipts to "the early 1780s"; the image's top-ledger clearly continues to 1795, 1796, 1797 and beyond (≈1799/1800). Trusting the image.*
2. *The description names a subscriber "van Roossbergh"; the clear signature at lower right reads "van Hooghenboeck." "Nicolaes [de Meijer]" is confirmed in the body.*
3. *The 25-guilder interest and the 12-January payment date are both confirmed by the image. The body additionally shows original half-yearly constitution dates of November 1640 and 12 May 1649, suggesting the underlying rente predates the 1703 receipt run.*

title: 18th-Century Dutch Manuscript Bond (Reverse)
description: This is the verso of an early modern Dutch manuscript obligation (obligatie), a registered debt instrument written by hand on folded vellum. The recto carries the body of the bond and its principal terms; the back, shown here, is given over entirely to the document's administrative afterlife. Fold lines divide the skin into a grid, and within that grid clerks have packed columns of dated entries — interest payments received, transfers of title from one holder to the next, and marginal reckonings — alongside a central inscribed block that appears to record an assignment of the bond. Such a verso is the working ledger of a security that changed hands repeatedly, and it speaks to the depth of the Dutch market for transferable debt: a single obligation could circulate among investors for decades, each endorsement extending its life. The dense, accreted hands make most individual entries illegible in the scan.
notes: Verso (p.2/2) of the Dutch vellum manuscript bond; see goetzmann1023 for recto.
transcription:

## Original Text:

The document is heavily overwritten with multiple layers of endorsements and transfer records. Due to the age, deterioration, and overlapping nature of the handwritten annotations, much of the text is difficult to read with complete certainty. The following represents the most legible passages:

[Upper left section - heavily faded and overlapping]
[illegible - multiple handwritten endorsements layered]

[Center and middle sections]
Various numerical notations and date references appear throughout, including what appear to be years in the 1700s and amounts in guilders or other currency denominations.

[Right side]
[illegible - dense handwriting with multiple corrections and annotations]

[Lower center area - slightly more legible]
What appears to be signature marks and transfer notations, though specific names and amounts remain [unclear] due to fading and script overlap.

[Bottom section]
Additional administrative marks and notations suggesting payment records or transfer confirmations.

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Multiple signatures/initials** scattered throughout (legible initials include what may be abbreviated names, but specific identification is uncertain)
- **Fold lines** indicating the document was folded for historical storage and circulation
- **Staining and age spots** consistent with 18th-century vellum
- **Erasures and corrections** visible in several sections
- **Layered endorsements** indicating successive transfers of ownership over time

The deteriorated condition and overlapping nature of the handwritten entries make complete transcription challenging. Professional archival analysis with specialized imaging may yield additional legible content.

title: Great Ming Circulating Treasure Note (Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao), Ministry of Revenue, Hongwu reign
description: A note of the Great Ming Circulating Treasure Note (大明通行寶鈔), the fiat paper currency issued by the Ministry of Revenue (戶部) of the Ming dynasty. The heading runs across the top, and a central red-bordered cartouche names the denomination, 壹貫 — one guan, equal to a thousand cash. Below it the engraving depicts ten strings of a hundred coins each, rendering that value to the eye for an often-illiterate public, while flanking columns of text declare the note current throughout the realm on a par with copper coin and warn that counterfeiters will be punished and informers rewarded with the offender's property. Printed from a woodblock on coarse mulberry-bark paper and overstruck with vermilion official seals, the note bears the reign-mark 洪武 (Hongwu) but leaves the year, month, and day blank. Unbacked and over-issued, such notes embody an early and ultimately inflationary experiment in sovereign paper money.
creator: Ministry of Revenue (戶部), Ming Dynasty
notes: Recto (p.1/2); see goetzmann1026 for verso. Ming dynasty paper currency, among the earliest printed banknotes in history. First issued 1375 under Hongwu Emperor.

## English Translation:

Denomination: **One Guan** (壹貫 / yi guan — one string, equal to one thousand cash)

[Illustration of ten strings of one hundred copper coins, totalling one guan.]

The Great Ming Treasure Note circulates on a par with copper coin. Counterfeiters shall be beheaded (zhan); one who informs on and apprehends [a counterfeiter] shall be rewarded with 250 taels of silver (yin er bai wu shi liang) and shall additionally be granted the property of the criminal.

Hongwu reign — [ ] year, [ ] month, [ ] day. (year, month, and day left blank)

title: Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao (Reverse)
description: The verso of a Ming-dynasty paper note, the Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao (大明通行寶鈔), the empire-wide circulating treasure certificate issued under imperial monopoly by the Ministry of Revenue (戶部). Printed by woodblock on coarse grey mulberry-fibre paper, this back carries no text of its own: the dark rectangular impression at the top is the bleed-through of the framed face design, while the substance of the side is a large vermilion official seal stamped near the lower centre, the mark of state authorization that gave the note its standing. Denominated on the front at one guan (貫, nominally a string of one thousand copper cash), the issue belongs to the great Ming experiment in pure fiat currency begun in the Hongwu reign. Unbacked by metal and printed without redating across reign-years, such notes depreciated steadily as issue outran demand, and the worn, near-effaced surface here testifies to long, hard circulation of an over-printed money.
creator: Ministry of Revenue (戶部), Ming Dynasty
notes: Verso (p.2/2); see goetzmann1025 for recto. Red official seal on reverse.
transcription:
# Great Ming Circulating Treasure Certificate (Da Ming Tongxing Baochao), Reverse — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

(The reverse side carries no printed text of its own.)

(Upper center: a dark rectangular impression — the bleed-through / show-through of the framed design printed on the face, appearing here in mirror image. No characters can be read as reverse-side text; the faintly visible strokes are the reversed remnants of the obverse block and are not transcribable as legible characters. [illegible])

(Lower center: a single large square vermilion official seal stamped on the paper. The seal is a multi-character seal-script (九疊篆-style) impression arranged in vertical columns within a square border, but it is heavily worn and abraded; the individual characters cannot be reliably distinguished.)

**(seal: [illegible] — square vermilion seal, seal script, worn)**

(No endorsements, cash-string denomination illustration, or other inked annotations are legibly present on this side.)

---

## English Translation:

This is the reverse (verso) of a Great Ming Circulating Treasure Certificate (*Da Ming Tongxing Baochao*, 大明通行寶鈔), the empire-wide fiat paper note of the early Ming, nominally one *guan* (貫, "string" of 1,000 copper cash).

The back bears no text of its own. The dark rectangle in the upper center is the show-through of the framed design printed on the front, seen in reverse; its strokes are not legible as independent characters. The only feature belonging to the reverse is a large square vermilion official seal (*yin*, 印) stamped at the lower center — the mark of state authorization. The seal is executed in ornamental seal script but is badly worn, and its characters are illegible. No further endorsements or annotations are legibly present.

title: United States (Continental) Lottery Ticket, Philadelphia, 1776
description: This is a printed bearer ticket for the United States Lottery, marked "CLASS the FIRST," entitling its holder "to receive such Prize as may be drawn against its Number, according to a Resolution of CONGRESS, passed at Philadelphia, November 18, 1776." It is signed in manuscript by the lottery manager "D. Jackson" and bears the class mark "I." at the lower left. The Continental Congress established this lottery by that resolution to raise money for the Revolutionary War, structuring it in successive classes of tickets that promised cash prizes drawn by lot. As a bearer instrument redeemable by whoever held it, the ticket functioned much like a public subscription, converting the patriotic gamble of ordinary purchasers into wartime revenue. The engraved script heading and the ornamental foliate border running down the left edge lend an air of official solemnity to what was, in effect, an early instrument of public finance.
creator: D. Jackson (lottery manager)
notes: United States (Continental) Lottery ticket, Class the First, No. 16m769. Authorized by Resolution of Congress, Philadelphia, November 18, 1776. Signed by D. Jackson.

- **Location:** Philadelphia (printed text)

title: South Sea Annuities Dividend Warrant
description: A printed dividend warrant completed in manuscript, issued at South-Sea House on the 30th day of April 1730 and headed "South-Sea Annuities at 4 per Cent." It authorises payment of half a year's annuity to a named annuitant out of the South Sea Annuities, the fund created when Parliament divided the company's capital stock into two moieties and converted one into annuities for its members. The warrant binds an interest holding to a fixed 4 per cent rate, falling due at the quarter day, and carries a printed instruction to take a discharge on the reverse. Authorised by the company's officers and endorsed for further payment, it documents the orderly servicing of the South Sea Company's reconstituted obligations in the decade after the Bubble.

pages.description: Front of the warrant, headed with an ornamental "14th" block and "South-Sea Annuities at 4 per Cent." Addressed to Mr. Conrade De Gols, it directs payment to Mr. John Bardin of half a year's annuity at 4 per cent, with the sum "£16 8" entered in the right margin and the printed clauses on the moiety division. Signed below by Williams and E. Anderson. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse of the warrant, where the printed front shows through the thin paper. Bears the discharge endorsement "John Bardin Jun'r" and a manuscript order "Pay Mr. John Bardin Jun'r" above a signature, completing the transfer of payment. Page 2 of 2.

creator: South Sea House (Williams; E. Anderson)
notes: South Sea Annuities dividend warrant, No. 624, 14th payment, to Conrade de Gols, directing payment to John Bardin. £16 8s on £820 annuity at 4% p.a. South Sea House, 30 April 1730.

title: Antwerp Exchange Obligation, Generale Keyserlyke Indische Compagnie
description: A printed-and-manuscript Dutch obligation issued at Antwerp "bij de Borse" (at the Exchange) by the printer A. Du Castel, recording a forward contract for shares in the Generale Keyserlyke Indische Compagnie, the Imperial Ostend Company chartered to trade with the Indies. The signer, acting as voordeel-Man (profit-agent), binds himself and his goods to deliver and pay for the shares on an appointed transport-day, against a cash premium, and sets terms for reimbursement of legitimate costs, payment of dividends, and liability for non-delivery. An engraved Imperial coat of arms crowns the sheet, asserting Habsburg sanction over a venture that rivalled the Dutch and English East India trades before diplomatic pressure forced its suspension.

pages.description: Recto. Printed obligation form headed "tot Antwerpen by A. Du Castel op de Borse," with an engraved Imperial double-headed-eagle coat of arms above and a decorative initial "I" block beside the body text. Manuscript ink fills the names, sums, and dates; signatures of the obligor and witnesses run across the foot, where a note references transport days into October 1730. Page 1 of 2.

Verso. A manuscript endorsement across the top cedes the contract to the order of Joan Kramp, "sonder guarand" (without guarantee), signed Joan Parcolo Wittebol. A faint embossed seal and the printed recto text show through in reverse from the thin sheet. Page 2 of 2.

creator: J.B. Franciscus (voordeel-Man); witnessed by Jan van Lancker
notes: Dutch-language exchange obligation, Antwerp Exchange (Borse), 1725. Issued by A. Du Casté at Antwerp. Guarantor: Joan Parcolo Wittebog. Page 1 of 2.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

tot Antwerpen bij A. Di. Civili
bij de Bourse 1728

[Coat of arms with "SPIRO SPERO" motto]

I. B. Tongelins Sculpist Antw:

Ck Onderschreve —, —, —, belove en obligiere my, mits een Constante premie, die tot myn contentement hebbe ontfangen, aen [handwritten: Mijn Heer Grammond?] ofte ordre, op het verthood deser tuschen heden en den eersten transportdagh naer [handwritten: Anno 1730 vijfde maande] 1730 — te leveren en te transporteeren [handwritten: Jan 28 —] Actien, inde Generacle Keyserlyke Indise Compagnie althier, onder conditie, van aen my benefens het transport te rembourfseren alle her gene nu reels is, en mildschen noch op die Actien in die tustschen-teyt sal gefourniert worden, en boven dien noch te gelyek aen my te betaelen [handwritten: andere Summe van Vierhondert en Vijftig ponden] voor tweede Premie a [handwritten: Dateerend Voordagh per Cento Agto] op ieder Actie, fullende daer regens aen de gene die dese Actien sal aenvoerden, ende betaelen, ter selver tyde uyt-keeruen mijn bovenfchreve alle voor de Compagnie in die tutschen-teydt op die Actien ceniger wyfe sal divideren, rembourleren, ofte geven, al beleere duyck meer als zijn bovenfchreve te doene naer betaelichge, maer [handwritten: voor de tweede Male den 1730] voorseyt, dese Actien aen my niet opgevraeght fijnde, en op den eersten transpot-dagh naer de op-vraeging niet getransporteerde betaelt fijnde, waer toe den op-vraeger gehouden is, dan blyft dese Obligatie ten mynen laste Nulle, en van geender waerde meer, fijnde dan die Actien gelyck oock den contanten premie, die my voor nu voor al-too is opgeleverd, en onder-roepelyek toebeboort, met alles wat van de Compagnie sal ontfangen. Aldus gecontractaert ter interventie van [handwritten: Makelaar Wijkman?]

onder verbintenisse van myn Persoon en goederen in Antwerpen den [handwritten: Twelfde April —] zeventien hondert [handwritten: en twintig]

[Signature] Jean Van Lancker

voor Actien à 192 P. C. in optie tot [handwritten: Eerste October 1730] inden tustschen tyde 2 daghen voor transpot op te vraegen

[Handwritten annotations and signature: Carlo Brittele / Jean Crampel?]

## English translation:

In Antwerp by A. Di. Civili
at the Exchange 1728

[Coat of arms with "I BREATHE I HOPE" motto]

I. B. Tongelins Sculptor Antwerp:

I, the undersigned —, —, —, promise and obligate myself, with a constant premium, which I have received to my satisfaction, to [handwritten: My Lord Grammont?] or order, on the obligation hereof between now and the first delivery day after [handwritten: Anno 1730 fifth month] 1730 — to deliver and transport [handwritten: January 28 —] Shares in the General Imperial Indian Company here, under the condition that I will reimburse my beneficiary for the transport of all that is now due, and further still on those Shares in the interim period shall be furnished, and beyond that still to pay equally to me [handwritten: another Sum of Four Hundred and Fifty pounds] for a second Premium at [handwritten: Dated Postage of Per Cent Ajo] on each Share, with the understanding that for those delivering these Shares and paying, at the same time the above-mentioned all for the Company in the interim period on the Shares anything shall divide, reimburse, or give, all much less than his above-mentioned to do toward settlement, but [handwritten: for the second time the 1730] aforesaid, these Shares not having been requested of me, and on the first delivery day after request not being transported [and] paid for, whereby the requester is obligated, then this Obligation remains to my charge as Null and void of any value more, whereby those Shares like also the cash premium, which I for now for all time have received, and subordinately belong to me, with all that the Company shall deliver. Thus contracted with the intervention of [handwritten: Broker Wijkman?]

under obligation of my Person and goods in Antwerp the [handwritten: Twelfth April —] seventeen hundred [handwritten: and thirty]

[Signature] Jean Van Lancker

for Shares at 192 P.C. in option until [handwritten: First October 1730] in the interim period 2 days for transport to be requested

title: Alexander Hamilton Treasury Circular on Subscription of State Debt Certificates
description: This manuscript circular, headed "Treasury Department Oct. 13. 1790" and signed "A. Hamilton," carries an administrative ruling from the Secretary of the Treasury to the commissioners of loans. Hamilton reports that one commissioner had read the law narrowly, supposing that certificates of the state debts could not be subscribed to the new loans unless they expressly stated they were issued for services or supplies toward prosecution of the late war. He rejects that construction: such a reading would exclude every certificate that merely acknowledges a sum due, and all certificates for monies loaned to the states, which is not the intent of the law. Where a certificate appears, from its tenor or from public records, to have been issued for expenditures toward the war and the defense of the United States, it is to be received. Written weeks into the assumption of state debts under the Funding Act of August 1790, the letter shows Hamilton settling, case by case, the eligibility rules that would convert disparate state obligations into a single funded federal debt.
creator: Alexander Hamilton (Secretary of the Treasury)
notes: Alexander Hamilton circular letter, Treasury Department, October 13, 1795. On eligibility of state debt certificates for subscription to new federal loans under the Funding Act of 1790.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Treasury Department Oct 13 1795**

(Circular)

Sir,

I find from a letter from one of the Commissioners of Loans, that it is conceived, certificates of the State Debts cannot be subscribed to the new Loans unless that they express that they are issued for services or supplies towards the prosecution of the late war.—

By this construction all certificates containing a simple acknowledgement of debt to a certain amount and all Certificates for monies loaned to the States would be excluded which is not the intent and meaning of the laws. If a Certificate of any State shall be issued and it shall not appear by the tenor of it, if it cannot be ascertained from any public record act or document to have been issued for some purpose, other than common transactions and expenditures for forces or supplies towards the prosecution of the late war and the defence of the United States, or some part thereof during the said war, it is consistent with the directions of the law to receive it on loan.—

I am Sir

Your Obedient Servant

**A Hamilton**

---

**Notable markings:**

- Signature: "A Hamilton" (Alexander Hamilton)
- Document classification: "(Circular)" - indicating this is a circular letter distributed to multiple recipients
- Date: October 13, 1795
- Header: "Treasury Department"

title: Massachusetts Bills of Exchange Schedule
description: A handwritten accounting schedule headed "Massachusetts," tabulating bills of exchange drawn for that state and signed at Philadelphia on 22 September 1778 by Thomas Hopkinson of the loan office. Ten denominations from $12 to $1,200 are arrayed against the number of sets and the running serial ranges, each row carrying its amount in dollars; the columns foot to 1,803 sets totalling $93,000. Produced amid the Continental Congress's wartime efforts to fund the war through loan-office certificates and bills payable abroad, the document captures the granular bookkeeping by which the new states' shares of a common debt were apportioned, denomination by denomination, before the bills passed into circulation.

pages.description: The schedule itself, headed "Massachusetts," ruled into four columns: Denomination, Sets, Numbers, and Amount in Dollars. Rows run from $12 to $1,200, footing to 1,803 sets and $93,000. Dated "Philad.a 22.d Sept.r 1778" and signed by Thos. Hopkinson, L.L. Page 1 of 2.

The verso, blank but for the table faintly showing through. An endorsement reads "List of Bills Exchange / Rec.d Oct.r 12.th 1778"; a filing note along the right margin records its receipt for Massachusetts. Page 2 of 2.

title: Rowley, Massachusetts Selectmen's Fine Warrant
description: A handwritten warrant of the Selectmen of Rowley, Massachusetts, dated 15 August 1782, directing Constable Samuel Searl to collect from each person on the attached list his proportion of the fine levied on the town's Sixth Class "for not procuring a man to Serve in the Continental Army." It is signed by the selectmen Thomas Mighill, Daniel Spofford, Joseph Poor, Joshua Pickard, and Isaac Smith. The document records the Massachusetts wartime practice of class-based conscription, under which townspeople were grouped into classes, each bound to furnish a soldier or pay an assessment in lieu of service. The fine is apportioned among the class members rather than charged to any single delinquent.

pages.description: The selectmen's warrant proper: an order to Constable Samuel Searl to collect from each named person his proportion of the fine on the Sixth Class for failing to procure a Continental Army soldier. Dated "August 15th 1782" and subscribed by Thomas Mighill, Daniel Spofford, Joseph Poor, Joshua Pickard, and Isaac Smith, Selectmen of Rowley. Page 1 of 3.

The accompanying assessment list, ruled into two columns with money columns headed for pounds, shillings, and pence. Each line pairs a name with the sum due, including Jacob Pearson, Henry Poor, the Jackmans, the Tenneys, and others of Rowley and neighbouring towns. Page 2 of 3.
The blank verso, stained and worn along the folds, carrying only the docket endorsement "of the Sixth Class" inscribed near the top and again at the foot. Page 3 of 3.

notes: Rowley, MA Selectmen's fine warrant to Constable Samuel Searl, August 15, 1782. Fines for Sixth Class members failing to provide a Continental Army recruit. Page 1 of 3.

title: State of Massachusetts-Bay War Bond issued to Seth Davenport, 1777
description: A printed and manuscript bond of the State of Massachusetts-Bay, dated 26 February 1777, acknowledging twenty pounds lawful money borrowed from Seth Davenport for the use and service of the state. The treasurer promises repayment of the principal by 1 June 1780 in Spanish milled dollars at six shillings each, or in coined silver and gold rated by a colonial-era act of the twenty-third year of George the Second, with interest at six per cent paid annually. Issued to fund the Revolutionary War effort, it represents the direct emission of state debt that would later be assumed and consolidated, the verso bearing endorsements recording its settlement in the following decade.

pages.description: The engraved face of the bond, headed "State of the Massachusetts-Bay" in ornate script within a decorative ruled border, a seal of an arm bearing a sword at upper left. Printed text with manuscript insertions records twenty pounds borrowed of Seth Davenport at six per cent, repayable 1 June 1780. Signed below "Witness my Hand," with D. Jeffries and committeeman J. Sumner, and a cancellation cross. Page 1 of 2.

The blank verso, faintly showing the printed face through the paper along its fold lines. Centered manuscript endorsements in brown ink read "Seth Davenport," "£23/14th," "Consolid'd 1734-8," "May 1783," and "No 7959" - later accounting notations marking the bond's consolidation and settlement. Page 2 of 2.

creator: State of Massachusetts Bay (Treasurer D. Jeffries; Committee)
notes: State of Massachusetts Bay war bond, £20 at 6% p.a., issued to Seth Davenport, February 26, 1777. Due June 1, 1780. Page 1 of 2.
transcription:
# Transcription: State of Massachusetts Bay War Bond, 1777

**State of the Massachusetts-Bay**

Sept 26, 1777

**Borrowed and received of** Seth Davenport **the Sum of** Twenty Pounds **Lawful Money, for the Use and Service of the STATE of** Massachusetts-Bay; **and in behalf of said State I do hereby promise and oblige myself and Successors in the Office of Treasurer or Receiver-General, to re-pay the Boffeller by the First Day of** June 1780, **the aforesaid Sum of** Twenty Pounds **Lawful Money, in Spanish mill'd Dollars at** Six Shillings **each, or in the several Species of coined Silver and** Gold **enumerated in an Act made and passed in the Twenty third Year of his late Majesty King** George **the Second, entitled an Act for ascertaining the Rates at which coined Silver and** Gold, **English Half Pence and Farthings may pass within this Government, and according to the Rates therein mentioned, with Interest to be paid annually at** Six per Cent.

**Witness my Hand,**

D. Saffrie [or Affre?]
[Illegible] Committee
J. Summer [or Sumner]

[Signature - appears crossed out]

title: Dutch Bond for the French Bourbon Princes in Exile
description: This printed Dutch-language obligation, with face value of one thousand guilders Hollands Courant ("EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollands Courant"), records a share in a loan raised in the Netherlands for the exiled Bourbon princes. Its French header is "Donné à Hamm en Westphalie sous nos sceaux ordinaires & sous le contreseing du Maréchal Duc de Broglie, Ministre d'Etat," dated the twenty-fifth day of September 1793 and the first year of the King's reign—the seat of the émigré court of Louis Stanislas Xavier (Comte de Provence) and Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois), brothers of Louis XVI. The Amsterdam bankers J. Bourcourd and the Widow ("Weduwe") E. Croese & Comp. acknowledge receipt of the capital, the supporting Lettres Patentes dated 7 September and the original obligation of 25 September 1793 deposited with the notary; the issue is capped at twelve thousand numbers. Actum Amsterdam, 28 September 1793, by order of their Royal Highnesses and the Garde du Trésor Royal. Issued as Republican France and the émigrés stood at war, the instrument shows the counter-revolution financed through Amsterdam's capital market, its sober typographic frame and unbroken royal formulary asserting dynastic continuity even as the throne it invoked had fallen.
creator: Louis Stanislas Xavier (Comte de Provence); Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois); J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp. (Amsterdam)
notes: Dutch bond (1,000 guilders) issued by French Bourbon princes in exile (Louis Stanislas Xavier and Charles Philippe) through Amsterdam bankers J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp., September 28, 1793. Signed on behalf of the Garde du Trésor Royal.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original:**

DONNÉ À HAMM EN WESTPHALIE (que nos [illegible] ordinaires & faits le contrefçing du Maréchal Duc de Broglie, Ministre d'État, le vingt cinquième jour de Septembre l'an de Grace Mille fept cent quatrevingt treize, & du Regne du Roi le premier.

LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER,
CHARLES PHILIPPE.

(L. S.)

LE MARÉCHAL, DUC DE BROGLIE.

N°. 373

Wy ondergeschrevene J. BOURCOURD en Weduwe E: CROESE & COMP., als daar toe, gelyk in vorenftaande Obligatie breder ftaat gemeld, geauthenticeerd zynde, bekennen voor rekening van hunne Koninglyke Hoogheden de Prineen van Vrankvyk, ontfangen te hebben uit handen van

de Somma van EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollands Courant voor Aandeel in dezelve Obligatie, en zyn de Coupons hiertoe behorende getekend [illegible]

Actum Amsterdam, den 28. September, 1793.

f 1000::-

Deze gecontrafigneerd en ten Prothocole aangetekend, [illegible] behorende de door my getekende Aandeclen niet boven het getal van Twaalf Duyzend Nummers. En zyn de Letters Patentes dato 7 September 1793, benevens de-Originele Obligatie van dato 25 September 1793 gedeponeerd en [illegible] berusttende onder my.

Par ordre de leur Altesses Royales.

Garde du Tresor Royal.

---

**English translation:**

GIVEN AT HAMM IN WESTPHALIA (that our [illegible] ordinaries & made the countersigning of the Marshal Duke of Broglie, Minister of State, the twenty-fifth day of September in the year of Grace One Thousand seven hundred ninety-three, and of the Reign of the King the first.

LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER,
CHARLES PHILIPPE.

(L. S.)

THE MARSHAL, DUKE OF BROGLIE.

No. 373

We undersigned J. BOURCOURD and Widow E: CROESE & COMP., as thereto, just as in the aforementioned Obligation further stated, authenticated hereby, acknowledge for the account of the Royal Highnesses the Princes of France, to have received from the hands of

the Sum of ONE THOUSAND GUILDERS Holland's Current [currency] for Share in the same Obligation, and the Coupons hereto belonging are signed [illegible]

Done Amsterdam, the 28th September, 1793.

f 1000::-

This countersigned and noted in the Protocol, [illegible] belonging through me the signed Shares not exceeding the number of Twelve Thousand Numbers. And the Letters Patent dated 7 September 1793, together with the-Original Obligation of date 25 September 1793 have been deposited and [illegible] remaining in my custody.

By order of their Royal Highnesses.

Keeper of the Royal Treasury.

---

- Serial number: **N° 373** (printed and handwritten)
- Date: **28. September, 1793** (printed and handwritten)
- Amount: **f 1000::-** (1000 Dutch Guilders)

- Notation of currency: **Hollands Courant**
- Official stamp/seal: "Par ordre de leur Altesses Royales" / "Garde du Tresor Royal"

title: Russian Imperial Treasury Note
description: This is a short-term interest-bearing Treasury note of the Imperial Russian State Treasury ("Билетъ Государственнаго Казначейства") for fifty rubles, dated 1915, the year repeated in roundels flanking the central text. Printed in green and black, it carries a 4% annual rate stated in the lower corners and declares itself valid until 1 August 1929 ("Билетъ дѣйствителенъ по 1 Августа 1929 г."). A column of four detachable coupons at left, numbered five through eight and each worth one ruble, provides for periodic interest payments falling due in 1919. The note bears the engraved double-headed imperial eagle and the signatures of the Manager of the State Treasury, the Director, and a bookkeeper. Such казначейские билеты were issued to fund the Imperial government as the strains of the war of 1914 deepened its fiscal deficits; the dense engraved acanthus and heraldic framing project the authority of a regime that would fall before the note matured.
creator: Imperial Russian State Treasury
notes: Russian Imperial Treasury Note, 50 rubles, 4%, No. 352814, 1915. Valid until August 1, 1929. With four interest coupons. Never redeemed due to collapse of Imperial government in 1917.

title: Kingdom of Greece 2½% Guaranteed Gold Loan Bond, Athens, 1898
description: A bearer bond of the Kingdom of Greece (Βασίλειον της Ελλάδος), issued for the gold-guaranteed loan of 1898: Δάνειον εις Χρυσόν Ηγγυημένον 2½% 1898, headed in French along the border as Emprunt Hellénique Garanti 2½% Or de 1898. The certificate carries a face value of 2,500 drachmas in gold and a coupon of 2½ per cent. Printed in Athens and authenticated in London, it bears the signature of the Greek Minister of Finance and a counter-signature for the Governor and Company of the Bank of England, the loan's paying agent. The 2½ per cent gold loan followed Greece's defeat in the war with the Ottoman Empire of 1897 and the sovereign suspension of 1893; it was floated under the International Financial Commission of foreign powers, whose guarantee—reflected in the modest coupon—is the document's true subject. The engraved frame, with Greek-key borders, the royal arms beneath a crown, and draped classical figures, asserts Hellenic continuity even as the terms record a treasury placed under outside supervision.
creator: Kingdom of Greece (Ministry of Finance); guaranteed by Bank of England
notes: Kingdom of Greece Gold-Guaranteed Loan bond, 2½% 1898, 2,500 drachmas, No. 30,731. International guarantee; authenticated by Bank of England. Issued under International Financial Commission supervision following Greek default of 1893.
transcription:
# Kingdom of Greece 2½% Guaranteed Gold Loan Bond of 1898, for 2,500 Drachmas / £100 — Transcription

Сему займу присвоена совмѣстная и солидарная гарантія
Россіи, Франціи и Великобританіи.
Облигація въ 937 руб. 50 коп. на предъявителя.

**(Greek terms — left wide column):**
Τὸ κεφάλαιον καὶ οἱ τόκοι τοῦ παρόντος δανείου εἰσὶν ἀπηλλαγ-
μένοι ὡσεὶ ἀπὸ παντὸς φόρου Ἑλληνικοῦ. Ὁ κομιστὴς δικαιοῦται εἰς
κεφάλαιον 2,500 Φράγκων ἀποφερόντων ἐτησίως τόκον πρὸς 2 1/2 0/0

ἐκδότου ἔτους (ἓ. ν.). Οἱ τόκοι οὗτοι παραγράφονται μετὰ πενταετίαν.
Ἡ ἀπόσβεσις τοῦ κεφαλαίου ἐνεργηθήσεται εἰς τὸ ἄρτιον διὰ κληρώ-
σεων ἐνεργηθησομένων κατὰ πᾶσαν 1 Ἰανουαρίου καὶ 1 Ἰουλίου
(ἓ. ν.) ἢ δι' ἐξαγορᾶς. Ἡ πρώτη κλήρωσις ἐνεργηθήσεται τῇ 1
Ἰανουαρίου 1903. Τὸ κεφάλαιον τῶν κληρουμένων ὁμολογιῶν παρα-
γράφεται μετὰ τριακονταετίαν. Μέχρι τῆς 1ης Ἀπριλίου 1918 δὲν
χωροῦσιν οὔτε προπληρωμαὶ ἐπὶ τοῦ δανείου οὔτε τροπὴ αὐτοῦ.

**(Greek terms — right column; line-ends partly cut by the page's punched holes):**
Καθ' ὅσον ἡ ὑπηρεσία τοῦ δανείου δὲν ἠθέλησεν ἀσφαλισθῆ
ὑπὸ τοῦ ἄρθρου 21 τοῦ Βασιλικοῦ Διατάγματος τῆς 22 Ἀ[πριλίου]
(4 Μαΐου 1898) προβλεπομένων προσόδων, ἡ ἀλλη[λέγγυος ἐγγύησις]
τῆς Γαλλίας, τῆς Μεγάλης Βρεταννίας καὶ τῆς Ρω[σσίας] [illegible]
[illegible] τὰς ῥήτρας καὶ τοὺς ὅρους τῆς συμβάσεως τῆς συ[ν-]
ἐν Παρισίοις τῇ 17/29 Μαρτίου 1898 μεταξὺ τῶ[ν τριῶν]
δυνάμεων καὶ τῆς Ἑλλάδος. Ἡ ἐγγύησις ἐπετράπη [illegible]
τοῦ Νόμου τῆς 8 Ἀπριλίου 1898 καὶ τοῦ Διατάγμ[ατος τῆς 28]
(ἓ. ν.), ἐν Ἀγγλίᾳ δυνάμει τοῦ Νόμου τῆς 1 Ἀπ[ριλίου 1898]
[ἓ.] ν.), καὶ ἐν Ρωσσίᾳ διὰ τῆς αὐτοκρατορικῆς κυρώσε[ως]
ἀπὸ 28 Μαρτίου / 9 Ἀπριλίου 1898.

**(French terms):**
Le capital et les intérêts de cet emprunt sont à jamais affranchis de tout impôt hellénique.
Le porteur a droit à un capital de 2,500 fr. produisant un intérêt annuel de 2 1/2 p. o/o, payable par semestre, les 1ᵉʳ avril et 1ᵉʳ octobre de chaque année. Ces intérêts se prescriront par cinq ans.
L'amortissement de l'emprunt s'effectuera au pair, par voie de tirages opérés les 1ᵉʳ janvier et 1ᵉʳ juillet de chaque année, ou au moyen de rachats sur le marché.
Le premier tirage aura lieu le 1ᵉʳ janvier 1903. Le capital des titres amortis se prescrira par 30 ans. Jusqu'au 1ᵉʳ avril 1918 il ne pourra être procédé ni à des remboursements anticipés, ni à la conversion de l'emprunt.
Si le service de l'emprunt ne peut être assuré par les ressources prévues à l'article 21 du décret royal du 22 avril / 4 mai 1898, la garantie collective et solidaire de la France, de la Grande-Bretagne et de la Russie s'exercera suivant les clauses et conditions de la convention conclue à Paris les 17/29 mars 1898, entre ces trois Puissances et la Grèce.
La garantie est autorisée en France par la loi du 8 avril 1898 et le décret du 28 mai suivant, en Angleterre par la loi du 1ᵉʳ avril 1898 et en Russie par la ratification impériale de la convention en date du 28 mars / 9 avril 1898.

**(English terms):**
The capital and interest of this loan are exempt for ever from every Greek tax.
The Bearer is entitled to a capital sum of £100 with interest at the rate of £2:10s. per cent. per annum payable half-yearly on the 1ˢᵗ April and 1ˢᵗ October in each year. Coupons not presented within five years of their due dates will be forfeited.
The redemption of this loan will be effected at par, by means of drawings which will take place on the 1ˢᵗ January and 1ˢᵗ July in each year, or by means of purchases in the market. The first drawing will take place on the 1ˢᵗ January 1903. Drawn bonds not presented within thirty years will be forfeited. Until the 1ˢᵗ April 1918, no repayment in advance may be made nor can the loan be converted until that date.
If the resources prescribed by article 21 of the Royal Decree of the 22 April/4ᵗʰ May 1898 are insufficient for the service of the loan, the joint and several guarantees of France, Great Britain and Russia will become operative under the clauses and the conditions of the Convention concluded in Paris, the 17ᵗʰ/29ᵗʰ March 1898, between the three Powers and Greece.
The guarantee is authorized in France by the law of the 8ᵗʰ April 1898 and the decree of the 28ᵗʰ May following; in England by the law of the 1ˢᵗ April 1898, and in Russia by the Imperial Ratification of the Convention of March 28ᵗʰ/April 9ᵗʰ 1898.

**(Russian terms):**
Капиталъ и проценты сего займа освобождаются навсегда отъ всякихъ греческихъ налоговъ. Владѣлецъ сей облигаціи имѣетъ право на сумму въ девятьсотъ тридцать семь рублей пятьдесятъ копѣекъ, приносящую 2 1/2 процента годоваго дохода, выплачиваемаго по полугодно, 1 Апрѣля и 1 Октября н. ст. каждаго года. Симъ процентамъ присвоивается пятилѣтняя платежная давность. Погашеніе сего займа будетъ совершаться по нарицательной цѣнѣ путемъ тиражей, производимыхъ 1 Января и 1 Іюля н. ст. каждаго года, или путемъ выкупа по биржевой цѣнѣ. Первый тиражъ будетъ произведенъ 1 Января н. ст. 1903 г. Погашеннымъ облигаціямъ присвоивается тридцатилѣтняя платежная давность. До 1 Апрѣля н. ст. 1918 г. не можетъ быть приступлено ни къ досрочному выкупу, ни къ конверсіи сего займа.
Въ случаѣ, если бы расходы по уплатѣ процентовъ и погашенія по займу не могли быть покрыты изъ источниковъ, предусмотрѣнныхъ § 21 Королевскаго Декрета 22 Апрѣля (4 Мая) 1898 г., совмѣстная и солидарная гарантія Россіи, Франціи и Великобританіи вступитъ въ силу согласно условіямъ конвенціи, заключенной въ Парижѣ 17 (29) Марта 1898 г. между сими тремя Державами и Греціей. Гарантія сего займа установлена: въ Россіи Высочайшей ратификаціей вышеозначенной конвенціи, послѣдовавшей 28 Марта (9 Апрѣля) 1898 г., во Франціи закономъ 8 Апрѣля н. ст. 1898 г. и декретомъ 28 Мая н. ст. 1898 г. и въ Великобританіи закономъ 1 Апрѣля н. ст. 1898 г.
