### RIGHT COLUMN — Statute excerpts (Spanish)

**Disposiciones de los Estatutos,** aprobados por el Poder Ejecutivo de la República de Cuba, de acuerdo con las leyes de creación del Banco Territorial de Cuba, aplicables á las Obligaciones. (Gaceta Oficial de la República de Cuba de Marzo 1°, de 1911.)

**CAPÍTULO I.—DEL BANCO TERRITORIAL DE CUBA.**

ARTÍCULO I.—Se crea una Sociedad Anónima, institución de crédito, denominada "Banco Territorial de Cuba."

ARTÍCULO II.—El capital de este Banco totalmente liberado, será de cinco millones de pesos moneda oficial, representados por 50,000 acciones de á cien pesos moneda oficial cada una.

ARTÍCULO III.—Tendrá, con fuerza de ley, la facultad de emitir, por espacio de sesenta años, obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios sobre la base de préstamos á propietarios de inmuebles situados en la República, con garantía de hipoteca y amortizables á corto y largo plazo.

ARTÍCULO IV.—Ninguna Sociedad, Compañía ó Banco, podrá emitir, como institución de crédito intermediaria, obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios, durante los sesenta años en que gozará de esa facultad exclusiva el "Banco Territorial de Cuba," sin perjuicio de que puedan hacerlo los deudores directos, por sí ó por medio de fiduciarios.

**CAPÍTULO II.—REQUISITOS PREVIOS NECESARIOS PARA SU CREACIÓN.**

ARTÍCULO V.—Aprobados previamente por el Poder Ejecutivo de la República los presentes Estatutos y de acuerdo con las condiciones de su autorización, se constituirá definitivamente el "Banco Territorial de Cuba."

El "Banco Territorial de Cuba," tendrá su domicilio social en la ciudad de la Habana, establecerá Sucursales, por lo menos, en las Capitales de las otras Provincias y podrá además, establecer agencias, comités ó representaciones en el extranjero.

Un Interventor oficial nombrado por el Presidente de la República, tendrá el especial cometido de velar por el cumplimiento de los preceptos de la Ley, de los que se establezcan en el Decreto de autorización y de estos Estatutos.

**CAPÍTULO V.—CONDICIONES PARA LA EMISIÓN DE OBLIGACIONES, CÉDULAS Ó BONOS HIPOTECARIOS.**

ARTÍCULO VIII.—Las obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios que se emitan en representación de los préstamos dados, se ajustarán á las condiciones siguientes:

(a) Podrán ser al portador ó nominativas, transmisibles estas por simple endoso. La suma total de esos títulos hipotecarios puesta en circulación no excederá en ningún caso del importe de los préstamos hipotecarios que respondan. El de las obligaciones especiales no excederá tampoco en ningún caso del de aquellos préstamos por cuya razón se emita. Cada uno de estos títulos llevará su numeración correlativa dentro de la serie correspondiente, y no podrán crearse en ninguna emisión títulos de un valor inferior al de veinte ($20) pesos moneda oficial. Estarán firmados y rubricados por el gerente, el cajero y el interventor oficial nombrado por el Gobierno. Sus láminas puestas en circulación serán títulos cortados de un registro matriz y llevarán consignados el tipo, época y forma de pago de los intereses y de la amortización de su capital. Las firmas y rúbricas referidas, podrán ser grabadas; pero no podrán ponerse títulos en circulación, sin que el Interventor Oficial autorice para ello al "Banco Territorial de Cuba," pudiendo dicho Banco disponer que sean refrendados los Banqueros encargados de su venta.

(b) Serán reembolsables á la par . . . . . . . . . . . . á vencimiento fijo, sin que el capital sea exigible hasta que le responda su amortización. Cada reembolso comprenderá el número de títulos necesarios para que el importe de la amortización sea tal que las láminas que queden en circulación no excedan de los capitales adeudados con garantía hipotecaria.

(c) Las obligaciones ó bonos que emita el Banco no podrán ser utilizadas como medio para reembolsar al Banco Territorial de Cuba de los préstamos hechos. Los títulos, después de haber sido pagados al portador de ellos, serán anulados, no pudiendo ser puestos de nuevo en circulación.

(d) En caso de reembolso anticipado hecho por los deudores, el Consejo de Administración deberá emplear los fondos provenientes de los reembolsos y de la indemnización máxima del tres por ciento (3%), ya en la compra en Bolsa de obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios del Banco, ya en préstamos hipotecarios ó ya en bonos de los Gobiernos de los Estados Unidos de América, Francia, Alemania, Inglaterra y Cuba, ó bien en préstamos garantizados por la pignoración de dichos valores, ó bonos garantizados por los mismos Gobiernos, como valores ó cédulas, obligaciones y bonos hipotecarios del Banco. La compra de valores antes citados, no deberá efectuarse si estos títulos tienen una cotización superior á la par, y el precio de las obligaciones hipotecarias compradas en Bolsa no deberá exceder del promedio del precio neto á que fueron vendidas, excepto que la compra en ambos casos sea especialmente autorizada por el Consejo de Administración.

(e) Las obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios, ya sean nominales, ya al portador, tendrán fuerza de escritura pública sobre la cual haya recaído sentencia firme de remate, para reclamar del Banco por la vía de apremio el pago del capital é intereses después de su vencimiento. Estos títulos tendrán como garantía especial en cuanto se refiere á su capital é intereses, todos los bienes muebles é inmuebles que constituyan el activo del Banco y además, los inmuebles hipotecados en los préstamos contratados. Se reconocerá como propietario de las obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios al portador y de sus cupones, al que los presente. No podrá aceptarse ninguna oposición judicial ó extrajudicial por parte de un tercero al pago de esos valores, á no ser que el solicitante haya llenado las formalidades que prescriben las leyes para retener el pago de los títulos de la deuda pública al portador y de sus cupones. No se reconocerá como propietario de las obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios de carácter nominativo más que al titular de los mismos. En caso de transferencia del pago de los intereses ó del reembolso del capital, será necesario para legitimidad del pago que la cesión ó el recibo se hallen firmados por el titular.

**CAPÍTULO VII.—DISPOSICIONES GENERALES.**

ARTÍCULO XI. (g) I.—El modo de emisión y rescate y el modo de reembolso de obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios y de las obligaciones especiales serán: el de emisión, conforme á lo dispuesto en el Artículo VIII y párrafo anterior de estos Estatutos, según se vayan realizando los préstamos; el de rescate por medio de compra por acuerdo del Consejo de Administración, y el de reembolso con arreglo á dicho Artículo VIII, y en la proporción y fechas que fije el Consejo de Administración, ó en las fechas fijadas en las obligaciones para sus respectivos vencimientos.

II.—Los títulos de obligaciones, cédulas ó bonos hipotecarios y obligaciones especiales, llevarán sus correspondientes cupones; y los de series extranjeras podrán redactarse, además del idioma español, en inglés, francés ú otro extranjero; y los de la serie inglesa, llevar además del nombre del "Banco Territorial de Cuba" en español, el de "Cuban Mortgage Company," en inglés, los de la serie francesa el de "Crédit Foncier Cubain" en francés, y así para los demás países, [donde] podrá usarse también el nombre en el idioma correspondiente, con traducción adecuada.

III.—El importe del cupón y el tanto de amortización de las obligaciones, cédulas y bonos hipotecarios, que se emitan por razón de préstamos, no serán nunca mayor que el importe de la renta líquida anual que por término medio produzcan, en un quinquenio, los inmuebles ofrecidos y tomados en hipoteca con garantía del mismo préstamo. El cómputo se hará siempre relacionando el préstamo con el rendimiento del inmueble hipotecado y la anualidad de las obligaciones hipotecarias que, con ocasión de aquel se emitan. Esta anualidad podrá ser, en cualquier tiempo, inferior á la renta líquida anual de los respectivos inmuebles hipotecados como garantía del préstamo ó para la emisión de las obligaciones.

El Banco Territorial de Cuba garantiza al portador de esta obligación, que no tendrá que pagar los impuestos cubanos presentes y futuros, ni los impuestos franceses presentes; pero si se estableciera un nuevo impuesto francés, ya sea sobre los títulos en circulación ó sobre la renta, el importe de dicho impuesto deberá ser abonado por el portador.

### STATUTE EXCERPTS

**Provisions of the Statutes approved** by the Executive Power of the Republic of Cuba, in conformity with the laws creating the "BANCO TERRITORIAL DE CUBA," applicable to the obligations. (Official Gazette of the Republic of Cuba, 1 March 1911.)

**CHAPTER I.—"BANCO TERRITORIAL DE CUBA."**

ARTICLE I. There shall be organized, under the name "Banco Territorial de Cuba," a credit institution, a joint-stock company.

ARTICLE II. The capital of this Bank, fully paid in, shall be five million dollars (legal tender), represented by 50,000 shares of one hundred dollars each, legal tender.

ARTICLE III. The Bank shall, by force of law, have the right to issue, over a period of sixty years, obligations, mortgage certificates or bonds, on the basis of loans made to owners of real-estate property situated in the Republic, secured by mortgage and amortizable over the short or long term.

ARTICLE IV. No company, corporation or bank may issue, as an intermediary credit institution, obligations, mortgage certificates or bonds during the sixty years in which the "Banco Territorial de Cuba" shall enjoy this exclusive privilege — without, however, depriving of these rights the direct debtors, acting either themselves or through the intervention of trustees.

**CHAPTER II.—REQUIREMENTS TO BE MET BEFORE ITS ORGANIZATION.**

ARTICLE V. After approval of the present statutes by the Executive Power of the Republic and in conformity with the conditions of its authorization, the "Banco Territorial de Cuba" shall be definitively founded. The "Banco Territorial de Cuba" shall have its head office in the City of Havana; it shall establish branches, at the least, in the capitals of the other provinces, and may also establish agencies, committees or representations abroad. An official controller [interventor], appointed by the President of the Republic, shall have the special charge of seeing that the precepts of the law, those established in the decree of authorization, and the present statutes are fulfilled.

**CHAPTER V.—CONDITIONS FOR THE ISSUE OF OBLIGATIONS, CERTIFICATES AND MORTGAGE BONDS.**

ARTICLE VIII. The obligations, certificates and mortgage bonds issued to cover the loans made shall conform to the following conditions:

(a) They may be issued either to bearer or registered, the latter transferable by simple endorsement. The total sum of these mortgage securities placed in circulation shall in no case exceed the value of the mortgage loans on which they are based. Likewise, the total sum of the special obligations shall in no case exceed the value of the loans that gave rise to their issue. Each security shall bear one of the consecutive numbers of the corresponding series, and in no issue may securities of a nominal value below twenty dollars (legal tender) be issued. They shall bear the initials and signature of the manager, the cashier, and the official controller appointed by the Government. Securities placed in circulation shall be detached from a stub-book and shall state the rate, the time, and the form of payment of interest and of the amortization of the capital. The aforementioned signatures and initials may be engraved, but the securities may not be placed in circulation without the official controller authorizing the "Banco Territorial de Cuba" to do so; the said Bank may direct that they be countersigned by the banks charged with their sale.

(b) They shall be redeemable at par . . . . . . at a fixed maturity, and payment of the capital may not be demanded before the date of its amortization. Each redemption shall comprise a number of securities sufficient so that the value of the amortization is such that the value of the securities remaining in circulation does not exceed the capital represented by the sum of the loans made with mortgage security.

(c) The obligations or bonds issued by the Bank may not be used as a means of repaying the "Banco Territorial de Cuba" for loans it has made. The securities, after being paid to their holder, shall be cancelled and may not be placed in circulation again.

(d) In case of early payment by the debtors, the Board of Directors shall employ the funds arising from those repayments, plus a maximum premium of three per cent (3%), either in purchasing on the Exchange the obligations, certificates or mortgage bonds of the Bank; or in mortgage loans; or in the securities of the Governments of the United States of America, France, Germany, England and Cuba, or in securities guaranteed by the Governments of those same nations; as well as in securities guaranteed by deposits of the said securities, certificates, obligations or mortgage bonds of the Bank. The purchase of the aforesaid securities shall not be made if they stand at a price above par, and the price of mortgage bonds bought on the Exchange shall not exceed the average of the net price at which they were sold — except where the purchase, in either case, has been specially authorized by the Board of Directors.

(e) The obligations, certificates or mortgage bonds, whether registered or to bearer, shall have the force of public instruments [enforceable deeds] on the basis of which a final judgment of public sale may be obtained, to claim from the Bank, by judicial process, after maturity, the capital and interest they represent — all the movable and immovable property constituting the Bank's assets and, in addition, the mortgaged properties upon which those loans were made. The obligations, certificates and mortgage bonds shall be recognized as the property of the bearer, and the coupons as that of whoever presents them. No judicial or extrajudicial objection may be accepted on behalf of a third party to prevent payment of these securities, unless the applicant has completed the formalities required by law to stop payment of bearer public-debt securities of the said values or their coupons. Only the registered holder shall be recognized as owner of registered obligations, certificates or mortgage bonds. In the event of transfer, or of payment of interest, or of repayment of capital, it shall be necessary — to legitimize the payment — that the deed of assignment or the receipt be signed by the holder.

**CHAPTER VII.—GENERAL PROVISIONS.**

ARTICLE XI. (g) I. The manner of issue and of redemption, as well as the manner of repayment of the obligations, certificates or mortgage bonds and of the special obligations, shall be as follows: issue, in conformity with the provisions of Article VIII and the preceding paragraph of these statutes, as the loans are made; redemption, by means of purchases resolved upon by the Board of Directors; and repayment, in conformity with the said Article VIII and in the proportion and on the dates fixed by the Board of Directors, or on the maturity dates marked on the obligations.

II. The obligation, certificate or mortgage-bond securities and the special obligations shall carry their respective coupons attached; and the securities of the foreign series may be drawn up, besides the Spanish text, in English, French or other foreign languages; those of the English series may bear, in addition to the name "Banco Territorial de Cuba" in Spanish, the name "Cuban Mortgage Company" in English; those of the French series, the name "Crédit Foncier Cubain" in French; and likewise for the other countries the name of the bank may be printed suitably translated into the corresponding language.

III. The amount of the coupon and the rate of amortization of the obligations, certificates and mortgage bonds issued on account of the loans made shall in no case exceed the average net annual revenue produced over a five-year period by the properties offered and taken as security for those same loans. These values shall in every case be calculated so that the loan bears a relation to the revenue produced by the mortgaged property and to the annuity of the mortgage obligations issued on that occasion. This annuity may at any time be lower than the net annual revenue produced by the respective properties mortgaged as security for the loan, or for the issue of the obligations.

The Crédit Foncier Cubain [Banco Territorial de Cuba] guarantees to the bearer of this obligation that he shall not have to pay present or future Cuban taxes, nor present French taxes; but should a new French tax come to fall in any form upon the securities in circulation or upon their revenue, the amount of the said tax shall be borne by the bearer.

**AMORTIZATION TABLE** (see the year-by-year schedule above, running 1914–1963 and retiring the full 50,000 obligations).

title: Connecticut Comptroller's Certificate, Hartford
description: A Comptroller's certificate of the State of Connecticut, an interest warrant entitling its holder to receive Five Pounds in Lawful Money out of funds appropriated for the payment of interest on the state's liquidated debt. Issued from the Comptroller's-Office and dated July 7, 1809, it was printed by Hudson and Goodwin of Hartford and bears the Comptroller's signature, with the words "ISSUED" and "INTEREST" running up the side margins. A cancellation hole punched through the center marks its redemption. The instrument belongs to the long aftermath of the Revolutionary war debt: states that had funded the struggle in depreciated paper spent the following decades servicing consolidated obligations, and such warrants were the quiet machinery by which a state met its creditors. Engraved rules and a plain typographic frame favor clerical legibility over ornament, the look of public credit made routine.
creator: State of Connecticut, Comptroller's Office
notes: Connecticut state debt interest warrant; No. 79; £5; liquidated debt interest payment; printed Hudson and Goodwin, Hartford; punched cancellation hole

I will Pow.

title: Connecticut Comptroller's Interest Certificate, Hartford, 1789
description: An interest certificate issued by the Comptroller's Office of the State of Connecticut, certifying that Andrew Kingsbury is entitled to receive five shillings lawful money out of funds appropriated for the payment of interest on the liquidated debt of the state. Dated December 2, 1789, at Hartford, where it was printed by Hudson and Goodwin and signed by the Comptroller, Ralph Pomeroy. Engrossed at one of the smallest denominations, the slip dates from the first year under the new Federal Constitution, when Connecticut was still servicing its own Revolutionary debt ahead of Hamilton's assumption scheme. The restrained engraved border and copperplate script, with "INTEREST" and "ISSUED" set vertically along the margins, reflect the plain, ledger-minded printing of early republican public finance. A cancellation hole punched through the centre marks its redemption.

title: Denver & Rio Grande Railway Dutch Bearer Certificate, Amsterdam, 1886
description: A Dutch bearer certificate (Certificaat) for $1,000, representing ten preferred shares (bevoorrechte Aandeelen) of $100 each in the Denver & Rio Grande Spoorweg-Maatschappij — the Denver & Rio Grande Railway Company. Rather than holding the American shares directly, the bearer holds a Dutch instrument issued through an Amsterdam Administratie-Kantoor established by the firm Hubrecht, van Harencarspel & Vas Visser, which deposited the underlying preferred stock and paid out dividends locally. Dated Amsterdam, 8 December 1886. The certificate is a fixture of the era's transatlantic capital flows: Dutch savings funnelled into Western U.S. railroad expansion through a trustee structure that spared investors the cost and risk of direct foreign holding. The restrained engraved frame and company arms convey the sober, fiduciary respectability such a depositary instrument was meant to project.
creator: Internationale Administratie-Kantoor, Amsterdam
notes: Denver & Rio Grande Railway preferred stock; Dutch bearer certificaat; No. 669; $1,000 = 10 shares at $100; Internationale Administratie-Kantoor Amsterdam; December 6, 1890

Denver & Rio Grande Spoorweg-Maatschappij.
(PREFERRED STOCK.)

CERTIFICAAT van $ 1000,
No. 669

Zonder deze is gerechtigd tot Tien bevoorregte Aandelen à $ 100,— in de Denver Rio Grande Spoorwegen-Maatschappij (Preferred Stock), MET MEER ANDERE INGESCHREVEN OP NAAM VAN DE MAATSCHAPPIJ BEHEERD VAN HET ADMINISTRATIE-KANTOOR, OPGERICHT DOOR HUEBRECHT, VAN HARENCASPEL & VAS VISSER, Jon geduld onderworpen bij den ondergetekenden Notaris. De uitdeeligingen op de geleponeerde Aandelen zullen voor rekening van den houder in Amerika geïnassecerd, overgemaakt en aldier uitbetaald worden.

De invisseling van Certificaten met de onvervallen Dividend-Bewijzen tegen de oorspronkelijke Aandelen geschiedt overeenkomstig het Bericht door bovenvermeld Administratie-Kantoor, uitgegeven in December 1880.

Amsterdam, [date], 188[0]

Getekend door mij,
[Signature] A. Meysenbauer

De Maatschappij tot beheer van het Administratie-Kantoor Voorzitend:
[Signature - illegible]

title: German External Loan 1924 (Dawes Loan), Swiss Issue — Funding Bond Entitlement Certificate, Bad Homburg, 1953
description: A certificate of entitlement (Bezugschein) issued by the Federal Republic of Germany in 1953, redeemable for a funding bond (Fundierungsschuldverschreibung) with a face value of 200 Swiss Francs. It belongs to the Swiss issue (Schweizerische Ausgabe) of the German External Loan of 1924, the Dawes Loan floated to underwrite reparations after the First World War. Issued at Bad Homburg v.d.H. by the federal debt administration (Bundesschuldenverwaltung), it settles arrears under the London Debt Agreement of 1953: the embodied claim corresponds to eight semi-annual interest coupons of 25 Francs each, accrued between 16 October 1944 and 15 October 1952. Strikingly, delivery of the funding bond is deferred "not before the reunification of Germany." The austere ruled border and plain typography befit a sober instrument of postwar fiscal reconstruction, binding a divided state to debts inherited from Weimar and conditioning their settlement on a then-distant unification.
creator: Bundesrepublik Deutschland, Bundesschuldenverwaltung
notes: Dawes Loan 1924 Swiss issue; post-WWII arrears bond; No. 10080; 200 CHF; 8 semi-annual arrears 1944-1952; London Debt Agreement 1953; redemption deferred until German reunification
transcription:
# Original:

Deutsche Äußere Anleihe 1924
(Dawes-Anleihe)
Schweizerische Ausgabe

Nr. 10080

Die Bundesrepublik Deutschland verpflichtet sich, dem Inhaber im Umtausch gegen diesen Bezugsschein eine nach den Bestimmungen des Londoner Abkommens über Deutsche Auslandsschulden 1953 und seiner Anlage 1 ausgestellte Fundierungsschuldverschreibung im Nennwert von

200 Schweizer Franken
für Zinsrückstände der Deutschen Äußeren Anleihe 1924
(Schweizerische Ausgabe)

zu übergeben, jedoch nicht vor der Wiedervereinigung Deutschlands.

Der in dieser Fundierungsschuldverschreibung verkörperte Anspruch entspricht dem Fundierungswert von zwischen dem 16. Oktober 1944 und dem 15. Oktober 1952 aufgelaufenen 8 Halbjahrssinsen von je 25 Schweizer Franken.

Bad Homburg v.d.H., im Jahre 1953

Für die Bundesrepublik Deutschland
Bundesschuldenverwaltung

[Signatures: Präsident / Vizepräsident]

---

# English translation:

German Foreign Loan 1924
(Dawes Loan)
Swiss Edition

No. 10080

The Federal Republic of Germany undertakes to deliver to the holder, in exchange for this certificate, a funding debt certificate issued in accordance with the provisions of the London Agreement on German Foreign Debts 1953 and its Annex 1, with a face value of

200 Swiss Francs
for interest arrears of the German Foreign Loan 1924
(Swiss Edition)

to be handed over, however not before the reunification of Germany.

The claim embodied in this funding debt certificate corresponds to the funding value of 8 semi-annual interest accruals of 25 Swiss Francs each accumulated between October 16, 1944 and October 15, 1952.

Bad Homburg v.d.H., in the year 1953

For the Federal Republic of Germany

title: Hope & Co. French Three Percent Funds Certificate
description: This Dutch-language Certificaat van Drie Percents Fransche Fondsen entitles its bearer to an annual income of three hundred francs (Drie Honderd Franken Rente), corresponding to a nominal capital of Frs. 10,000 inscribed in the French national debt register (Grootboek der Publieke Schuld van Frankryk). It was issued in Amsterdam on 19 October 1868 by an administration office (Administratie-Kantoor) directed jointly by Hope & Co., Ketwich & Voombergh, and Wed. W. Borski, which collected the French interest and paid it on to the holder. The instrument exemplifies the Amsterdam practice of repackaging foreign sovereign rentes into bearer certificates: it let Dutch savers hold French government debt at one remove, with the leading houses interposing their credit and administration between the bondholder and a foreign treasury. The restrained engraved frame and dignified lettering convey institutional probity rather than ornament, signalling a conservative income security. A printed note records the twenty quarterly coupons issued alongside it.

title: Dutch Certificate for 1,000 Roubles of Russian Assignat Stock, Amsterdam, 1824
description: A Dutch transferable certificate (CERTIFICAAT) entitling its bearer to a capital of one thousand roubles in assignats ("DUIZEND ROEBELS in ASSIGNATEN") bearing six per cent interest, the sum inscribed in the Grootboek (Great Book) of the Russian Empire. Issued at Amsterdam, dated 7 October 1824, it was placed by the custodians Gerardus Blancke en Zoon, Chemet & Weetjen, and Van den Broeke & Comp., under the head direction (Hoofddirectie) of Van Vloten & de Gyselaar, Buys & Zoon, and H.W. van Driel Slam, with a long roster of Amsterdam houses subscribing beneath. Interest fell due against coupons per the notice of 5 July 1824, and the holder might exchange the certificate, with its unexpired coupons, at the Administratie Kantoor for a direct inscription in the Russian Great Book. The instrument shows how the Amsterdam capital market repackaged Russian sovereign debt into rouble-denominated paper for Dutch investors, the certificate standing as a custodial claim on stock held abroad. The sheet is austere, set in plain roman and small-capital type with no allegorical ornament, its authority resting on the engraved names and the counter-signatures rather than on imagery.

title: German External Loan 1924 (Dawes Loan) Rights Certificate, Bad Homburg, 1960
description: A trilingual rights certificate (Bezugschein / Rights Certificate / Bon), in German, English, and French, issued by the Bundesschuldenverwaltung of the Federal Republic of Germany at Bad Homburg vor der Höhe on 4 January 1960. For a face value of £40 sterling (Vierzig Pfund Sterling), it confers on the holder the right to receive consolidation bonds in accordance with the Agreement on German External Debts concluded in London on 27 February 1953, against surrender of arrears of interest on the British Issue of the German External Loan of 1924, the so-called Dawes Loan floated on 14/15 October 1924. The arrears, covering 15 October 1944 to 14 October 1952, were reckoned at three per cent simple interest. The plain typographic frame and the federal eagle at the centre announce a sober postwar settlement: the document marks Germany's reconstruction of its honour as a debtor, restoring access to foreign capital markets a generation after the original loan and reparations regime.
creator: Bundesrepublik Deutschland, Bundesschuldenverwaltung
notes: Dawes Loan 1924; trilingual Bezugschein/Rights Certificate/Bon; No. A 025220; £40; British and French tranches; London Debt Agreement 1953; Bad Homburg January 4, 1960

**£ 40 nominal**

**FORTY POUNDS.**

In the event of the reunification of Germany the funding bonds will be issued against surrender of this rights certificate.

title: German Government International Loan (Young Plan) Dollar Gold Bond
description: This is a bearer gold bond of the German Government International 5½ Per Cent Loan 1930, the credit instrument popularly known as the Young Loan. Denominated at $1,000 in United States gold dollars, it bears 5½ per cent interest payable half-yearly on June 1 and December 1, with principal and interest discharged at the office of J.P. Morgan & Co. in the Borough of Manhattan, New York, and matures June 1, 1965. The sheet is the "General Bond," printing the full conditions in English along its left flank: it recites the Hague Agreement of January 20, 1930, names the Bank for International Settlements as trustee, and pledges German customs, tobacco, beer and spirits revenues as security. Issued to commercialise Germany's reparations annuities under the Young Plan, the bond turned political war debt into marketable paper sold across the creditor powers. Engraved by the Reichsdruckerei in Berlin, its restrained maroon guilloche frame projects fiscal probity over the precarious arrangement it embodied.
creator: German Government (Deutsches Reich)
notes: Young Plan Loan 1930; No. 16917; $1,000; 5.5% gold bond; due June 1, 1965; J.P. Morgan & Co., New York; WWI reparations finance

title: German Government International 5½% Loan (Young Plan), French Tranche, Berlin, 1930
description: This is a trilingual bearer bond of 1,000 French Francs from the German Government International 5½% Loan of 1930, the financial instrument of the Young Plan. The text appears in parallel German, English, and French columns ("Internationale 5½%ige Anleihe des Deutschen Reichs 1930"), and the bond identifies itself as the French tranche (Französische Ausgabe). It is dated Berlin, 15 August 1930, sealed with the German eagle of the Reichsschuldenverwaltung (Reich Debt Administration) and printed by the Reichsdruckerei. Issued pursuant to the experts' plan of 7 June 1929 and the Hague agreements, the loan settled the reparations question by converting Germany's open-ended obligations into a fixed, marketable debt placed simultaneously across the principal money centres. A note states the bond is exempt from French stamp duty under the law of 7 April 1930. The sober, symmetrical engraving and the imperial eagle project sovereign creditworthiness rather than ornament.
creator: German Government (Deutsches Reich)
notes: Young Plan Loan 1930; No. A.2009599; 1,000 French Francs; French (Französische Ausgabe) tranche; trilingual German/English/French; WWI reparations finance

title: United States Three Per Cent Funded Debt Certificate, Philadelphia, 1792
description: This engrossed and partly handwritten certificate records a funded debt of the United States, issued from the Register's Office and dated Philadelphia, 14 June 1792. Under the heading "Treasury of the United States," it declares "BE IT KNOWN that there is due from the United States of America unto Donato & Burton of London, or their Assigns, the Sum of Fourteen thousand Dollars," bearing interest at three per centum per annum from the first of April 1792, payable quarter-yearly, and redeemable whenever provision shall be made by law. The debt is registered in the office and transferable only by appearance in person or by attorney. It is signed by Joseph Nourse, Register of the Treasury, and attested below by Clement Biddle, Notary Public for Pennsylvania. The instrument belongs to the three-per-cent stock created under Alexander Hamilton's 1790 funding and assumption acts, by which the new federal government consolidated Revolutionary debts into interest-bearing securities; that a London house holds it shows how quickly American funds drew foreign capital.
creator: United States. Treasury Department
notes: U.S. Treasury 3% Debt Certificate No. 4693. $14,000 issued to Donato A. Burton of London. Signed by Joseph Nourse, Register of the Treasury. Notarized by Clement Biddle, Notary Public, Philadelphia, June 14, 1792.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Treasury of the United States**

No. 4693, Register's Office 14 June 1792

**BE IT KNOWN** that there is due from the United States of America unto **Donato A. Burton** of London, or their Assigns the Sum of **Fourteen Thousand and Dollars** bearing interest at three per Centum per Annum from the **First of April 1792** inclusively; payable quarterly, at such place as Redemption, by the Payment of said Sum, when Provision shall be made therefor by Law; which Debt is recorded in this Office, and is transferable only by Appearance in Person, or by Attorney, at the proper Office, according to the Rules and Forms instituted for that Purpose.

**$14,000 Dollars**

**Joseph Nourse Reg.**

---

**I CLEMENT BIDDLE, Notary Public for the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, duly commissioned and qualified; dwelling in the City of Philadelphia, hereby and make known unto all Persons to whom these Presents shall come or may concern, that upon the **Fourteenth Day of June** in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Ninety Two I went to the Office of **The Register of the Treasury of the United States**

title: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, Virginia & Kentucky Land Share, Paris
description: This engraved registered share (action) of the Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine conveys title to 100 acres of land in the states of Virginia and Kentucky, free of mortgage or privilege, at a creation price of 1,300 francs. Issued in Paris to Charles Guillaume Juste Jérome of Série B, it bears 6 per cent annual interest — styled the "taux légal en Amérique et en France" — running from 15 July 1821 and disbursed through the dated dividend coupons that frame both margins. The text sets the company's 1,849,000-acre holding, of which 1,500,000 acres form 15,000 shares of 100 acres, while a Série A of 3,000 shares funds the colonization itself; the enterprise was constituted under the house of De Redern et Compagnie before the notary Froger Deschesnes. The grapevine borders, promising cultivated abundance, advertise a Restoration-era French speculation in American land. Interest above 10 per cent could be withheld to recover advances.
creator: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine
notes: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, Action Série B, No. A/234, 100 acres in Virginia and Kentucky, 1300 Francs. Issued to Charles Guillaume Juste Jerome. Guaranteed by De Beers et Compagnie, Paris. With dividend coupon strips.
transcription:
# American Colonization Company — Share of 100 Acres of Land in the States of Virginia and Kentucky, Series B, No. 4234 (Paris) — Transcription

## Original:

*(Center panel — main certificate face)*

**Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine.**

**Action**

**de 100 Acres de Terres dans les Etats de Virginie et de Kentucky.**

**Série B, N° 4234 — Francs 1300**

M.ᵣ CHARLES GUILLAUME JUSTE JÉROME est propriétaire de cent acres de Terres indivises dans les Etats de VIRGINIE ET DE KENTUCKY, libres de toutes hypothèques et priviléges, faisant partie de 1,849,000 acres de Terres appartenant à la Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, constituée sous la raison de DE REDERN ET COMPAGNIE, suivant acte passé devant M.ᵉ Froger Deschesnes, et son Confrère, Notaires à Paris; transcrit et affiché conformément à la loi.

La COMPAGNIE prend les mesures propres à tirer le plus grand parti possible de ses Propriétés par la Colonisation et les opérations industrielles et commerciales qui se rattachent naturellement à son Entreprise. Elle s'occupe des moyens de faciliter des Etablissements dans ses terres à ceux qui désirent se fixer dans les ÉTATS-UNIS D'AMÉRIQUE, et d'assurer leur bien-être; et elle fait les Expéditions nécessaires.

A cet effet, les Terres susdites sont réparties de la manière suivante:

349,000 acres sont destinés à être vendus de gré à gré aux Colons, qui préfèrent devenir Propriétaires. Le produit du prix de vente est laissé par les membres fondateurs de la Compagnie dans l'Entreprise de la Colonisation, sans intérêt pendant les dix premières années, après l'expiration desquelles, ladite Somme portera un intérêt de SIX pour cent à leur profit, et sera remboursable par cinquième d'année en année.

Les 1,500,000 acres restants ont été convertis en Quinze mille Actions, chacune de Cent acres de terres. La Compagnie mettra ces terres en valeur par des baux à ferme, des Moulins à Blé et à Scie, des Exploitations de Bois de Construction, et par tous les Etablissements de commerce et de manufactures que la culture et la richesse du sol amèneront naturellement.

Les actions seront nominatives ou au porteur; les nominatives sont transmissibles par simple endossement; l'action ne pourra jamais être sujette à aucun appel de fonds; le bénéfice net, résultant des opérations de la Compagnie, formera le dividende que l'actionnaire recevra annuellement sur la présentation des Coupons annexés.

Trois mille Actions, depuis le N° 1ᵉʳ jusqu'à 3,000, formant la série A, sont affectées à l'augmentation du Capital de la Compagnie, destiné à faire les opérations de la Colonisation.

Ces actions jouiront seules d'un intérêt annuel de SIX pour cent (Taux légal en Amérique et en France) du prix de création de 1,300 f., payable à bureau ouvert, à compter du 15 Juillet 1821.

Cet intérêt tient lieu de dividende jusqu'à ce que celui-ci excède SIX pour cent. Attendu que les deniers, provenant de la vente de ces actions, sont employés dans la Colonisation au profit de tous les actionnaires, la Compagnie aura le droit d'exercer une retenue sur le dividende des 15,000 Actions, lorsqu'il excédera Dix pour cent, mais seulement sur la portion excédante, pour se rembourser des avances qu'elle pourrait avoir été dans le cas de faire pour le paiement des Coupons de la série A.

*(The certificate face ends here at the bottom edge of the image; the signature/closing portion of the document is not present in this scan.)*

---

*(Left margin — vertical column of dividend coupons, each headed "**Coupon d'Action, Série B, de la Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine.**" followed by: "N° [x]. Coupon de dividende de l'Action n° 4234 à recevoir le 15 juillet [year], au Comptoir de la Compagnie, à Paris." Reading top to bottom:)*

- N° 29 — Coupon de dividende de l'Action n° 4234 à recevoir le 15 juillet 1849, au Comptoir de la Compagnie, à Paris.
- N° 27 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1847 …
- N° 25 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1845 …
- N° 23 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1843 …
- N° 21 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1841 …
- N° 19 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1839 …
- N° 17 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1837 …
- N° 15 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1835 …
- N° 13 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1833 …
- N° 11 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1831 … *(cut off at bottom edge)*

*(Right margin — corresponding column of coupons, top to bottom:)*

- N° 30 — Coupon de dividende de l'Action n° 4234 à recevoir le 15 juillet 1850, au Comptoir de la Compagnie, à Paris.
- N° 28 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1848 …
- N° 26 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1846 …
- N° 24 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1844 …
- N° 22 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1842 …
- N° 20 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1840 …
- N° 18 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1838 …
- N° 16 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1836 …
- N° 14 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1834 …
- N° 12 — … à recevoir le 15 juillet 1832 … *(cut off at bottom edge)*

---

## English Translation:

*(Center panel — main certificate face)*

**American Colonization Company.**

**Share**

**of 100 Acres of Land in the States of Virginia and Kentucky.**

**Series B, No. 4234 — 1,300 Francs**

Mr. CHARLES GUILLAUME JUSTE JÉROME is the owner of one hundred acres of undivided Land in the States of VIRGINIA AND KENTUCKY, free of all mortgages and liens, forming part of 1,849,000 acres of Land belonging to the American Colonization Company, constituted under the firm name of DE REDERN AND COMPANY, pursuant to a deed executed before Maître Froger Deschesnes and his Colleague, Notaries at Paris; transcribed and posted in conformity with the law.

The COMPANY takes the measures suitable to derive the greatest possible advantage from its Properties through Colonization and the industrial and commercial operations that naturally attach to its Enterprise. It occupies itself with the means of facilitating Settlements on its lands for those who wish to establish themselves in the UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, and of assuring their well-being; and it undertakes the necessary Expeditions.

To this end, the aforesaid Lands are apportioned in the following manner:

349,000 acres are intended to be sold by private agreement to the Colonists who prefer to become Proprietors. The proceeds of the sale price are left by the founding members of the Company in the Enterprise of Colonization, without interest during the first ten years, upon the expiration of which the said Sum shall bear interest of SIX percent for their benefit, and shall be repayable by one-fifth from year to year.

The remaining 1,500,000 acres have been converted into Fifteen thousand Shares, each of One hundred acres of land. The Company will develop these lands through farm leases, Flour and Saw Mills, Timber operations, and through all the establishments of commerce and manufacture that the cultivation and the richness of the soil will naturally bring about.

The shares may be registered (nominative) or to bearer; the registered ones are transferable by simple endorsement; the share can never be subject to any call for funds; the net profit resulting from the Company's operations shall constitute the dividend that the shareholder will receive annually upon presentation of the attached Coupons.

Three thousand Shares, from No. 1 up to 3,000, forming Series A, are allocated to the increase of the Company's Capital, intended to carry out the operations of Colonization.

These shares alone shall enjoy an annual interest of SIX percent (the legal rate in America and in France) on the creation price of 1,300 francs, payable at the open counter, beginning 15 July 1821.

This interest stands in place of a dividend until such time as the latter exceeds SIX percent. Whereas the monies arising from the sale of these shares are employed in the Colonization for the benefit of all the shareholders, the Company shall have the right to exercise a withholding on the dividend of the 15,000 Shares, when it exceeds Ten percent, but only on the excess portion, in order to reimburse itself for the advances it may have had occasion to make for the payment of the Series A coupons.

*(The certificate face ends here; the signature/closing portion is not present in this scan.)*

*(Left and right margins — dividend coupons.)* Each coupon reads: "**Share Coupon, Series B, of the American Colonization Company.** No. [x]. Dividend Coupon of Share no. 4234, to be received on 15 July [year], at the Company's Counter, in Paris." The left column carries the odd-numbered coupons Nos. 29, 27, 25, 23, 21, 19, 17, 15, 13, 11 (payable 15 July 1849, 1847, 1845, 1843, 1841, 1839, 1837, 1835, 1833, 1831 respectively); the right column carries the even-numbered coupons Nos. 30, 28, 26, 24, 22, 20, 18, 16, 14, 12 (payable 15 July 1850, 1848, 1846, 1844, 1842, 1840, 1838, 1836, 1834, 1832 respectively). The lowest coupon in each column (Nos. 11 and 12) is cut off at the bottom edge of the scan.

title: Forced Loan Receipt of the Year IV, Ghent
description: This printed récépissé is a receipt for the Emprunt forcé de l'an 4, the compulsory loan levied by the French Directory under the Law of 19 Frimaire, Year IV (10 December 1795). A local Receiver (Percepteur) of the Commune of Gand (Ghent), in the Département de l'Escaut, acknowledges payment of 350 francs in coin or representative value, and certifies that the quittance is divided into ten detachable coupons. The lower sheet bears those ten coupons, each printed "Emprunt forcé de l'an 4," each for thirty-five francs, individually numbered and signed, with the law's title ("Loi du dix-neuf Frimaire") running vertically between the columns. Issued at Gand on 28 Pluviôse An IV (17 February 1796), the document records how the Directory's emergency capital levy was extracted, in subdivided installments, from the recently annexed Belgian departments. Its plain administrative typography, devoid of ornament, suits a fiscal instrument meant for rapid, uniform collection.
creator: France. République française
notes: Emprunt Forcé de l'An 4 (Forced Loan of Year 4 = 1795-96). Récépissé No. 3869, Commune No. 9. Includes detachable coupon strip sheet with 9+ coupon bonds. Issued pursuant to the Law of 19 Frimaire, Year 4.
transcription:
# Receipt for the Forced Loan of Year IV, Commune of Ghent — Transcription

## Original:

(Printed form with handwritten insertions shown in italics-style *[handwritten]* markers below. Header spans full width.)

**DÉPARTEMENT** *de l'Escaut* — **EMPRUNT FORCÉ DE L'AN 4.** — **COMMUNE** *de Gand*

*N.° 1869*

**RÉCÉPISSÉ.**

Je soussigné *Pierre Mulle* Percepteur de la Commune *de Gand*, Canton *de Gand*, Département *de l'Escaut*, déclare avoir reçu du C.en *Enfans Kerlyn* [surname reading uncertain] taxé au rôle de l'Emprunt forcé établi par la Loi du 19 Frimaire, 4.e année de la République, à la somme de *trois cents cinquante francs* en numéraire ou valeur représentative aux termes de la Loi, dont je lui ai délivré la présente quittance, divisée en dix coupons, suivant la forme prescrite par l'article IX de la Loi du 19 Frimaire dernier.

FAIT à *Gand* le *28 Pluviose* an *4.e* de la République française, une et indivisible.

*[signature]* — Percepteur de la Commune *de Gand*

---

(Lower sheet: grid of detachable coupons. A large decorative title runs across the tops of the columns, one word per coupon-header — reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom: **EMPRUNT** | **FORCÉ** / **DE L'AN** | **QUATRIEME** / **DE** | **LA** … Between the two columns, printed vertically: **Loi du dix-neuf Frimaire**. Each coupon repeats the same printed body, differing only in the "an" cited, the coupon number, and handwritten inserts.)

**Coupon N.° 10** (top-left):
(left margin) DÉPARTEMENT *de l'Escaut* / Municipalité *de Gand* / COMMUNE *de Gand* / (diagonal) Bon pour *35* francs.
Emprunt forcé de l'an 4. Bon pour la somme de *trente cinq francs* faisant le dixième de la taxe d'Emprunt forcé de l'an 4, du C.en *Enfans Kerlyn* A valoir en paiement des Contributions directes de l'an treize, Ou admissible, dès-à-présent, en paiement du Droit d'enregistrement dû pour cause de succession. Conformément aux articles XI et XII de la Loi du 19 Frimaire an 4. (N.° 10.)
*[signature]* Percepteur de la Commune de Gand — *[signature]* Commissaire nommé par l'Administration Municipale.

**Coupon N.° 5** (top-right): identical body but "Contributions directes de l'an huit". Same margin, diagonal "Bon pour 35 francs.", same two signatures. (N.° 5.)

**Coupon N.° 9** (mid-left): identical body but "Contributions directes de l'an douze". (N.° 9.)

**Coupon N.° 4** (mid-right): identical body but "Contributions directes de l'an sept". (N.° 4.)

(A third row of coupons — headed **DE** and **LA** — is only partially visible at the bottom edge, cut off by the image; their bodies repeat "Emprunt forcé de l'an 4. Bon pour la somme de trente cinq francs…" but numbers and details are not legible.)

The percepteur's signature (a stylized "Mulle") recurs on each coupon; the commissaire's signature reads *[Van Lokeren]* [reading uncertain].

---

## English Translation:

**DEPARTMENT** of the Escaut — **FORCED LOAN OF YEAR 4.** — **COMMUNE** of Ghent

No. 1869

**RECEIPT.**

I, the undersigned, Pierre Mulle, Collector of the Commune of Ghent, Canton of Ghent, Department of the Escaut, declare that I have received from Citizen *Enfans Kerlyn* [surname uncertain], assessed on the roll of the Forced Loan established by the Law of 19 Frimaire, 4th year of the Republic, the sum of three hundred fifty francs in coin or representative value under the terms of the Law, for which I have issued him the present receipt, divided into ten coupons, according to the form prescribed by Article IX of the Law of 19 Frimaire last.

DONE at Ghent, the 28th of Pluviose, year 4 of the French Republic, one and indivisible.

*[signature]* — Collector of the Commune of Ghent

---

(Detachable coupons. Decorative title spelled across the column-heads: "EMPRUNT / FORCÉ / DE L'AN / QUATRIEME / DE / LA …" — "Forced Loan of the Fourth Year"; running vertically between the columns: "Law of the Nineteenth of Frimaire.")

**Coupon No. 10:**
(margin) Department of the Escaut / Municipality of Ghent / Commune of Ghent / (diagonal) Good for 35 francs.
Forced Loan of Year 4. Good for the sum of thirty-five francs, being the tenth part of the Forced Loan tax of Year 4, of Citizen Enfans Kerlyn. To be applied toward payment of the direct Contributions of Year thirteen, or admissible, from now on, in payment of the Registration Duty owed by reason of inheritance. In conformity with Articles XI and XII of the Law of 19 Frimaire Year 4. (No. 10.)
[signature] Collector of the Commune of Ghent — [signature] Commissioner appointed by the Municipal Administration.

**Coupon No. 4:** same text, but "direct Contributions of Year seven." (No. 4.)

title: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania Stock Transfer, Philadelphia, 1795
description: This is a manuscript stock-transfer endorsement, written on the verso of a share certificate of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. In a clerk's looping copperplate hand it records that John Oldden assigns and transfers to Henry Schlaesman four and a half shares in the capital or joint stock of the company, dated the thirtieth day of October 1795 at Philadelphia. It is witnessed by Jo. Palmer Jr. and executed by Alex. Elmslie as attorney. The document belongs to the formative years of American corporate finance: chartered in 1794, the company was among the first joint-stock marine and fire insurers in the United States, and its transferable shares show how risk-pooling and a market in fractional ownership took shape together in the early republic. The plain ruled card, free of vignette or seal, conveys authority through the dignified script and the legal formula 'In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand.'
creator: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
notes: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. Transfer endorsement: John Oldden to Henry Schlaesman, 2.5 shares, October 30, 1795. Signed by Alex. Elmslie, Attorney. Witnessed by Jo. Palmer.
transcription:
# Transcription

I John Oldden — Do hereby assign and transfer to Henry Schlaesman Two and a half — Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania.

In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand the Thirtieth day of October — 1795

Witness,
Jo. Palmer J.

[Signature] Alex. Elmslie Atty

---

## Notable markings:

- **Signatures present:** John Oldden (assignor), Jo. Palmer (witness), Alex. Elmslie (Attorney)
- **Date:** October 30, 1795
- **Key details:** This is an endorsement on the verso of a share certificate for the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
- **Share quantity:** 2½ shares being transferred
- **Handwriting:** Cursive script typical of late 18th-century formal documents

title: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania Stock Transfer, Philadelphia, 1797
description: This handwritten instrument is a stock transfer, or deed of assignment, recording the conveyance of shares in the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. The sheet pairs a ruled ledger on the left with the formal assignment text on the right. The ledger, headed Date, Folio/Ledger, Transferer and Transferee, No. of Shares, and Number of the Certificate, enters the date March 20, 1797, the folios 228 and 135, and the transfer of three shares from Henry Philips to Alexander Wilcocks; the certificate column is left blank. The text repeats this in legal form: 'I Henry Philips Do hereby assign and transfer to Alexander Wilcocks Three Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania,' dated the twentieth of March 1797, witnessed by John Lewis and signed by Philips. Such transfer books document how ownership in the young republic's early joint-stock insurers passed by manuscript entry rather than printed certificate, the company's register itself serving as proof of title. The plain ruled form, unadorned but for the engraved Insurance Company heading in blackletter, mirrors the sober, ledger-bound character of late-eighteenth-century American corporate practice.
creator: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
notes: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. Transfer: Henry Philips to Alexander Wiltocks, 3 shares, March 20, 1797. Witnessed by John Lewis. Includes ledger record (Folio 228→135).
transcription:
# Transcription

**Left side (Ledger entry):**

Date: March 20, 1797

Folio Ledger: 228 | 135

Transferer and Transferee: Henry Philips to Alexander Wiltocks

No. of Shares: 3

Number of the Certificate: [blank]

---

**Right side (Formal assignment text):**

I Henry Philips Do hereby assign and transfer to Alexander Wiltocks Three — Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania.

In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand the Twentieth day of March 1797.

Witness:
John Lewis

[Signature]: Henry Philips

- Ink markings and cross-outs in the left ledger column, indicating document handling and corrections
- Aged parchment/paper with visible foxing and discoloration consistent with late 18th-century document preservation

title: State of Massachusetts Bay Treasury Note, 1780
description: This printed-and-manuscript instrument is an interest-bearing treasury note of the State of Massachusetts Bay, dated the First Day of January, A.D. 1780, promising to pay Richard Fry the sum of two hundred eighty-five pounds, with interest at six per cent. per annum, on or before the First Day of March 1783. Issued amid the Revolutionary War to help meet the State's quota toward the Continental Army, the note answers the era's ruinous depreciation by indexing repayment to commodities rather than to nominal money: both principal and interest are made payable in the current money of the State adjusted to the then-current prices of five bushels of corn, sixty-eight and four-sevenths pounds of beef, ten pounds of sheep's wool, and sixteen pounds of sole leather. The text states that the promised sum stands at thirty-two and a half times what those same quantities would have cost at the prices fixed by the 1777 act "to prevent Monopoly and Oppression," making explicit the scale of wartime inflation. The engraved scrollwork heading and decorative border lend the obligation an air of public credit, while the indexing clause itself testifies to the collapse of confidence in paper currency. Signed by Thomas Dawes and Richard Cranch as committee and by the State Treasurer, whose name is partly obscured by a cancellation mark.
creator: Massachusetts Bay. Treasury
notes: Massachusetts Bay Consolidated Note No. 287, £285, January 1, 1780. Issued to Richard Fry. 6% interest, due March 1, 1783. Commodity-indexed principal. Signed by Wm. Dawes, R. Cranch (Committee) and Hy. Jones, Treasurer.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No.** [struck through]

287

**The FIRST Day of JANUARY, A. D. 1780.**

IN Behalf of the State of Massachusetts-Bay, I the Subscriber do hereby promise and oblige Myself and Successors in the Office of TREASURER of said STATE; to pay unto Richard Fry or to his Order, the SUM of Two hundred and eighty five Pounds on or before the FIRST Day of MARCH, in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-three, with Interest at Six per Cent. per Annum: Both Principal and Interest to be paid in the then current Money of said STATE, in a manner as Five Bushels of CORN, Sixty-eight Pounds and four-seventh Parts of a Pound of BEEF, Ten Pounds of SHEEPS WOOL, and Sixteen Pounds of SOLE LEATHER, shall then current Money, at the then current Prices of said ARTICLES.—This SUM being THIRTY TWO and a HALF what the same Quantities of the same Articles would sell at the Prices fixed to them in a Law of this STATE made in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-seven, intituled, "An Act to prevent Monopoly and Oppression" The current Prices of said Articles, and the consequent Value of every Pound of the Sum herein promised, to be determined agreeably to a LAW of this STATE, intituled, "AN ACT to provide for the Security and Payment of the Balances that may appear to be due by Virtue of a Resolution of the General Assembly of the Sixth of February One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-nine, to THIS STATE'S Quota of the CONTINENTAL ARMY, agreeably to the Recommendation of CONGRESS, and for Supplying the TREASURY with a Sum of Money for that Purpose."

**Signatures:**
- Wm. Dawes } Committee
- H. Cranch }
- Hy. Jones Treasurer [signature]

**Notable markings:**
- Ornamental decorative border surrounding entire document
- Serial number "287" handwritten at top
- "1780" handwritten at top left
- Document number "[struck through]" at top left
- Multiple handwritten signatures in cursive script
- Aged, cream-colored paper with visible wear and foxing

title: United States Loan Office Three Per Cent Debt Certificate, New York, 1790
description: This engrossed manuscript certificate of the United States Loan Office, issued at the State of New York office on 17 October 1790, acknowledges a debt of 533 dollars and 33 cents owed by the United States to Jean François Paul Grand of Switzerland or his assigns, bearing interest at three per centum per annum from the first day of October 1790, payable quarter-yearly and redeemable whenever provision is made by law. The principal is transferable only by appearance in person or by attorney at the proper office. It is signed by M. Clarkson, Commissioner of Loans, and attested by John Wilkes, Notary Public of New York, beneath a wax seal; an engraved roundel of the United States arms anchors the upper margin. The certificate embodies Hamilton's funding scheme of 1790, which consolidated Revolutionary debts into interest-bearing federal obligations. That a member of the Grand banking house holds it attests to the early appetite of European financiers for the young republic's funded debt.
creator: United States. Treasury Department
notes: U.S. Loan Office Certificate No. 4513, New York, $533.33 at 3%. Issued to Jean Francois Paul Grand of Switzerland, October 17, 1790. Signed by M. Clarkson, Commissioner of Loans. Notarized by John Wilkes, Notary Public, New York.
transcription:

(No. 4513)     State of New York 17th Octr. 1790.

BE it known that there is due from the United States of America unto Jean Francois Paul Grand of Switzerland or his Assigns, the Sum of Five hundred & thirty Three Dollars & thirty three Cents a Debt bearing Interest at Three per Centum per Annum, from the first day of Octr. 1790 inclusively, payable quarter-yearly, and subject to Redemption by Payment of said Sum whenever Provision shall be made therefor by Law; which Debt is recorded in this Office and is transferable only by Appearance in Person, or by Attorney at the proper Office, according to the Rules and Forms instituted for that purpose.

£533 DOLLARS. 33                                    (Signed)
    100                                            M. Clarkson

State of New-York,
U.S. of America.

F. title: French Royal Treasury Receipt
description: A handwritten quittance on a printed form, this receipt records a transaction in French royal credit. Jean de Turmenyes, Conseiller du Roi and Garde du Trésor Royal, acknowledges receiving 8,000 livres in gold louis, silver, and other coin as the principal for 400 livres of annual and perpetual rente, computed at the denier vingt (one-twentieth, or five percent). The rente is to be constituted by the Prévôt des Marchands et Échevins of Paris upon the one million livres of perpetual rente newly alienated by the King under the Édit of March 1698, secured on the revenues of the Aides et Gabelles. Dated at Paris, 10 April 1698. The instrument shows how the crown, pressed by the costs of the Nine Years' War, borrowed through the municipality of Paris, whose civic credit was lent to the throne. The austere engraved form, all text and almost no ornament, befits a fiscal mechanism that ran on signatures and ledgers rather than display.
creator: France. Trésor Royal
notes: Quittance du Garde du Trésor Royal, No. 391, April 10, 1698. 8,000 livres principal for 400 livres of annual rente on the Hôtel de Ville de Paris (Aides et Gabelles). Issued to Jean Eloy Udtin. Signed by Jean de Turmenyes, Garde du Trésor Royal. Pursuant to Edit of March 1698.
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (French):**

N° 391                                    DENIER 20. Mars 1698

JE JEAN DE TURMENYES, Conseiller du Roy en fes Confeils, Garde de fon Trefor Royal, Confeile avoir receu comptant en cette Ville de Paris, de Jean Eloy Udtin [argentier de Monsieur l'Archevêque de Rouen]

[several lines with handwritten corrections and annotations]

la Somme de huil mille livres en Louis d'or, d'argent, & autre Monnoye pour le principal de quatre cens livres de Rente qu'il ly feront vendus & constituez par Messieurs; les Prevost des Marchands & Efchevins de ladite Ville de Paris fur le Million de livres auquels & effectifs de rente annuelle & perpetuelle à eux nouvellemnt alienez par Sa Majefté en confequence de fon Edit du mois de Mars 1698. regiftré où befoin a été, à prendre fur les droits d'Aydes & Gabelles de France, pour jouïr par ledit Udtin à raifon de Denier Vingt, ainfi qu'il fera plus au long expliqué par le Contrat de confti tution qui luy fera expedié de ladite Rente par lesdits Sieurs Prevost des Marchands & Efchevins, en execution dudit Edit. De laquelle Somme de huit mille livres

à moy ordonnée pour employer au fait de ma Charge, je quitte lesdits Sieurs & tous autres. A Paris le [date]

jour d'[illegible] mil fix cent quatre-vingt-dix-huit.

Quittance du Garde du Trésor Royal, Année mil fix cent quatre-vingt-dix-huit.

[Signature: de Turmenyes]

M. Sougis                N.e

---

**English Translation:**

I, JEAN DE TURMENYES, Counselor to the King in his Councils, Keeper of his Royal Treasury, certify that I have received in cash in this City of Paris, from Jean Eloy Udtin [financial agent of the Archbishop of Rouen]

the Sum of eight thousand livres in gold louis, silver, and other coin for the principal of four hundred livres of annual perpetual rente which will be sold and constituted to him by the Messieurs Provost of Merchants and Aldermen of the said City of Paris on the Million livres of annual and perpetual rente newly alienated to them by His Majesty in consequence of his Edit of the month of March 1698, registered where necessary has been, to be drawn from the rights of Aides and Gabelles of France, for the enjoyment by the said Udtin at the rate of Denier Vingt, as will be more fully explained by the Contract of constitution which will be issued to him for said Rente by the said Messieurs Provost of Merchants and Aldermen, in execution of said Edit. From which Sum of eight thousand livres

ordered to me for employment in the discharge of my office, I acquit the said Messieurs and all others. At Paris on [date]

day of [illegible] thousand six hundred ninety-eight.

Receipt of the Keeper of the Royal Treasury, Year thousand six hundred ninety-eight.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Document number: N° 391 (upper left)
- Date notation: "DENIER 20. Mars 1698" (upper right)
- Signature: "de Turmenyes" (cursive, center-right)
- Multiple handwritten corrections and annotations in the margins and between lines
- Names at bottom: "M. Sougis" and "N.e"
- Several passages marked with strikethrough or revision marks

title: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation Share Certificate
description: This part-printed, part-manuscript certificate records a single transferable share in the Piscataqua Bridge, issued by its proprietary corporation and held here by the Town of Portsmouth in the County of Rockingham, New Hampshire. Dated the seventh day of December 1793, it is authenticated under the corporation's seal and signed by James Sheafe as President, a Treasurer, and Nathaniel Adams as Proprietors' Clerk, with the share declared transferable by assignment recorded on the Proprietors' Records. The document belongs to the first wave of American chartered toll-bridge companies, which spanned the Piscataqua River between Portsmouth and Kittery and raised capital from towns and private subscribers through joint-stock shares. Plain and text-dominated, without ornament, the certificate's authority rests on the affixed seal and officers' hands rather than engraved imagery, reflecting the early republic's reliance on transferable proprietary shares to fund public works.
creator: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation
notes: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation, Share No. 262, Town of Portsmouth, Rockingham County, New Hampshire, December 7, 1793. President: James Sheafe. Proprietors' Clerk: Nathaniel Adams. Early American toll bridge infrastructure share.

IN testimony whereof, the Seal of the Corporation is hereto affixed, the seventh day of December One thousand seven hundred and ninety three

James Sheafe President.

[illegible] Treasurer.

Attest,

Nathl. Adams Proprietors' Clerk.

title: Caisse d'Épargnes Lafarge Tontine Share, Paris, 1793
description: This is an action (share) of ninety livres issued by the Caisse d'Épargnes et de Bienfaisance Lafarge, Seconde Société, a Parisian savings-and-benevolence institution operating tontine-style life annuities. For the sum of ninety livres the subscribers, Paul Beautier and his wife Catherine Ferraudin, acquire a share whose rente and accroissements (the survivors' accruing gains) are established on the life of Catherine Ferraudin, up to a ceiling of three thousand livres of rente per annum, payable on production of the certificat de vie. It is dated at Paris, the thirtieth of September 1793, and signed by the Directeur Général and the Vérificateur Général (and visaed by an Administrateur). Issued amid the Revolution's fiscal turmoil and assignat inflation, the share illustrates how private mutual schemes courted small savers with longevity-linked income. The engraved seal — a coffer with attendant figures beneath the legend "L'Économie est la Seconde Providence du Genre Humain" — moralises thrift as a providential virtue.
creator: Caisse d'Épargnes et de Bienfaisance Lafarge
notes: Caisse d'Épargnes et de Bienfaisance Lafarge, Seconde Société. Action No. 124824, 90 livres, Paris, September 30, 1798. Issued to Paul Beauttier and Catherine Ferraudin (on the life of Catherine Ferraudin). Tontine-style life annuity savings instrument. Registration No. 10317.
transcription:
# Share of the Caisse d'Épargnes et de Bienfaisance Lafarge (Second Society), Paris, 1793 — Transcription

## Original:

(Top header, above the ruled frame — printed labels with manuscript entries)

**Indication.** **Fol.** *419* (manuscript)
**Département d**[e] *Paris* (manuscript)

**Reconnoissance, numérotée.**
*16817* (manuscript; first digits uncertain, read approx. 16817)

(Inside the ruled frame:)

**SECONDE SOCIÉTÉ.**

**ACTION DE LA CAISSE**
**D'ÉPARGNES ET DE BIENFAISANCE**
**LAFARGE.**

(Left ornamental box:) ACTION de quatre-vingt-dix liv.

**N°** *124824* (manuscript)      **Action de 90 l.**

IL a été payé à la Caisse, par *Paul Beautier et Catherine Ferraudin* *Son épouse,* (manuscript) la somme de quatre-vingt-dix livres, prix de la présente Action, délivrée sous le N° *Cent Vingt quatre Mille huit Cent Vingt quatre* (manuscript = 124824) pour en jouir sur la tête de *l'Antoinette Cécile Cramer née à Genève le vingt un Mars mil sept cent soi[xante-cinquième?]* (manuscript, struck through — final word illegible) de la Rente qui écherra à ladite Action, et participer aux Accroissemens qui auront lieu à mesure des extinctions qui surviendront, jusqu'au *maximum* de trois mille livres de Rente, en rapportant par le[s] dit *[susnommée]* le certificat de vie de *[la]d[ite] Cramer.* (manuscript, partly struck through)

— A Paris, ce *Trente Septembre* 1793 — (day and month manuscript; year printed "179" with final "3" completed in manuscript)

(Signature:) *Lafarge*
**Directeur général.**

Vu par nous Administrateur.
(Signature:) *[Cadot?]* (manuscript, illegible)

Enregistrée sous ledit N° *124824.* (manuscript)      au folio *139.* du *31.ᵉ* (manuscript) Registre des classes.

(Engraved circular seal, center: a figure kneeling at a coffer with two attendant figures; encircling legend:) **L'ÉCONOMIE EST LA SECONDE PROVIDENCE DU GENRE HUMAIN**

(Signature at right of seal:) *[Robelot?]* (manuscript, illegible)
**Vérificateur général.**

**CLAUSE DE RIGUEUR.** L'Actionnaire est tenu, aux termes de l'art 26 du chap. Ier. des Statuts, de faire viser son Action chaque année, dans le courant du mois de Janvier, soit au Bureau général de la Caisse d'Épargnes, soit chez les Receveurs établis par elle dans les Départemens ; s'il ne remplit pas cette formalité, qui est de rigueur, il ne participera pas au tirage de l'année.
Ce *visa* n'est pas celui exigé par les décrets qui a lieu pour les *effets au porteur* ; la Caisse n'a délivré aucune Action de cette nature dans cette seconde Société. — Les Actions prises sur les *têtes de Genève* ne sont pas soumises à cette formalité de *visa*.

**Department of** Paris

**AND BENEVOLENCE FUND**

It has been paid to the Fund, by Paul Beautier and Catherine Ferraudin his wife, the sum of ninety livres, being the price of the present Share, issued under No. One Hundred Twenty-four Thousand Eight Hundred Twenty-four (= 124824), to enjoy it upon the life of Antoinette Cécile Cramer, born at Geneva on the twenty-first of March seventeen hundred and sixty-[fifth?] (this life-name struck through, final word illegible), namely the annuity that shall fall due to the said Share, and to share in the Accruals that shall occur as the successive extinctions arise, up to a maximum of three thousand livres of annuity, upon production of the certificate of life of the aforesaid person — the certificate of life of the said Cramer (partly struck through).

At Paris, this Thirtieth of September 1793.

(Signature:) Lafarge
**Director General.**

Seen by us, Administrator.
(Signature illegible)

Registered under the said No. 124824, at folio 139, of the 31st Register of classes.

(Engraved circular seal; legend:) **THRIFT IS THE SECOND PROVIDENCE OF THE HUMAN RACE**

(Signature:) [illegible]
**Verifier General.**

**BINDING CLAUSE.** The Shareholder is bound, under the terms of art. 26 of chap. I of the Statutes, to have his Share endorsed (visaed) each year, during the month of January, either at the General Office of the Savings Fund or with the Receivers established by it in the Departments; if he does not fulfil this formality, which is obligatory, he shall not take part in the year's drawing.
This visa is not the one required by the decrees that applies to bearer instruments; the Fund has issued no Share of that kind in this Second Society. — Shares taken out upon the lives of Geneva heads are not subject to this formality of visa.

title: Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan Coupon Sheet (Rothschild, London)
description: A sheet of attached interest coupons from the Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan of 1896, a sterling external loan of the Republic of Chile. Each coupon promises £2.10.0 — six months' interest on a £100 bond at five per cent — payable at the office of Messrs. N.M. Rothschild & Sons, London, or at the exchange of the day in Santiago, Paris, Berlin, Hamburg, Amsterdam and Brussels, the network of houses through which Chile placed its debt in the European market. The surviving coupons run semi-annually from January 1938 to January 1946, the loan's later maturities. That a South American republic could borrow in London at five per cent, serviced through the Rothschilds and quoted across the continent's principal bourses, attests to Chile's standing as a reliable sovereign creditor in the age before the First World War. The reverse bears finely engraved guilloche rosettes and interlaced medallions, the security ornament of the period.
creator: Republic of Chile
notes: Coupon strip for Bulgaria 6% State Mortgage Loan 1892; horizontal format with rectangular coupons in row; semi-annual interest coupons
transcription:
# Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan 1896 — Interest Coupon Sheet (N. M. Rothschild & Sons, London) — Transcription

## Original:

*(Layout: A horizontal sheet of detachable semi-annual interest coupons for bond serial No. 10,269 of the "Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan 1896." All coupons carry the same engraved wording, differing only in the due-date and the sequential coupon number printed as a large bold numeral. A finely engraved pale-green guilloche security ground repeats the microtext "CHILIAN FIVE PER CENT LOAN 1896" throughout each coupon, and the outer decorative borders repeat "CHILIAN 5 PER CENT LOAN 1896." Every coupon bears the serial number "10,269" twice and the value "£2.10.0." twice. The currency mark is an ornate pound sign; the shillings/pence form "2.10.0" = £2 10s 0d, i.e. one half-year of 5% interest on £100. The top row runs left-to-right; two ornamental engraved vignette panels close the right end of the sheet; one further coupon (No. 80) is detached at the lower right.)*

For £2.10.0. being Six Months Interest on £100 payable at the Office of Mess.rs N. M. Rothschild & Sons London & at the exchange of the day in Santiago, Paris, Berlin, Hamburg, Amsterdam & Brussels.
10,269
£2.10.0.
10,269
[coupon number]

title: Russian Provisional Government Liberty Loan Bond, Petrograd, 1917
description: This is a 5% bearer bond (облигація) of fifty rubles nominal, issued under the "Заемъ Свободы" — the Liberty Loan — of the Russian Provisional Government, marked IV series and dated Petrograd, 27 March 1917. Floated weeks after the February Revolution toppled the monarchy, the loan sought to fund a state in fiscal crisis and to bind the citizenry to the new order through patriotic subscription rather than coercion. The engraved text makes the appeal explicit: it addresses the "citizens of great free Russia," warns that a strong enemy threatens to break and return the country to its dead past, and frames lending as the fulfillment of duty owed to a homeland rebuilt on equality and truth. The central vignette of the domed State Duma (Государственная Дума) supplies the visual argument, casting the parliamentary chamber as the emblem of the freedom the loan was meant to defend. The minister-president and assembled ministers sign below.
creator: Russian Provisional Government
notes: Russian Freedom Loan (Заем Свободы), 5% bond, 50 rubles nominal, IV Series, No. 059857. Petrograd, March 27, 1917. Provisional Government. Same issue as goetzmann0677; lower denomination. Handwritten "½ of 50R" annotation at top.

**5% ОБЛИГАЦІЯ**

**въ ПЯТЬДЕСЯТ РУБЛЕЙ нарицательныхъ.**

Къ Вамъ, граждане великой свободной Россіи, къ тѣмъ изъ Васъ, кому дорого будущее нашей Родины, обращаемъ мы нашъ горячій призывъ.

Сильный врагъ вторгся въ наши предѣлы, грозитъ сломить насъ и вернуть страну къ старому, нынѣ мертвому строю.

Только напряженіе всѣхъ нашихъ силъ можетъ дать намъ желанную побѣду.

Нужна затрата многихъ милліардовъ, чтобы спасти страну и завершить строеніе свободной Россіи на началахъ равенства и правды.

Не жертвы требуетъ отъ насъ Родина, а исполненія долга.

Одолжимъ деньги Государству, помѣстимъ ихъ въ новый заемъ, и спасемъ этимъ отъ гибели нашу свободу и достоинство.

title: Republic of Texas Consolidated Fund Stock Certificate
description: This is a $500 certificate of stock in the Ten per Cent Consolidated Fund of the Republic of Texas, created by Act of February 5th, 1840, and issued to Edwards Hall. The principal bears interest at ten per cent per annum, payable semi-annually upon presentation of the certificate and delivery of the several interest warrants at the Treasury Department in gold or silver; the principal is redeemable at the pleasure of the government after the 30th day of June 1845, and the stock is transferable by simple endorsement. It was issued at Austin on 15 June 1840 over the signature of the Comptroller, with ten interest coupons attached below. The instrument records the young Republic's effort to fund its chronic deficits by consolidating debt into an interest-bearing fund, a recurring expedient of a treasury starved of specie. Neoclassical engraved vignettes carry the message: a maritime scene of a youth and a winged-capped figure of commerce gazing toward ships, and at left Hebe offering a cup to an eagle, allegories of trade and aspiration befitting a fledgling state seeking credit.
creator: Republic of Texas / Treasury Department
notes: Republic of Texas, Certificate of Stock in the Ten Per Cent Consolidated Fund, $500, issued to Edward Hall. Created by Act of Feb. 5, 1840. Austin, June 18, 1840. Signed by Comptroller. Ten interest coupons attached below.
transcription:

**REPUBLIC OF TEXAS**

**Certificate of Stock in the ten per cent consolidated fund**

**CREATED BY ACT**

**OF FEB. 5TH 1840**

---

**Willing the Republic of Texas to Edward Hall**

**Five Hundred Dollars** Payable in order with interest at the rate of ten per cent per annum, payable semi-annually on the 1st days of June and Dece., at the Treasury Department, in Gold or Silver, on presentation of this Certificate and delivery of the several interest coupons on the margin hereof, the first payment of interest [shall] be made on the 15 days of Dece. 1840, in [further] interest at the pleasure of the Republic after the 1st day of June 1841. Payable interest to be transferred by such Government payment Department. Austin 15 June 1840.

[Signature] James E. Ewan, Comptroller    [Signature] Charles Sewall [unclear] Comm.

*Sanderson & Peck, N.Y. Engs*             *Endicott & Co. New Orleans*

Each coupon reads:

**= Republic of Texas =**

[Signature marks] Charles Sewall

*No[.] [number]*
