title: Strand Bridge Company Share
description: An engraved share certificate of the Strand Bridge Company, the London undertaking incorporated by Act of Parliament to build the Thames crossing later renamed Waterloo Bridge. Flourished copperplate lettering spells out the company name across the head, and the text certifies the named holder as a proprietor of one share, entered in the company's Registry Book and held under the Common Seal, here a gilt oval wafer affixed at the left. Issued at London on 30 December 1809, the document represents a private subscription company raising capital for public infrastructure; a footnote requires every sale or transfer of the share to be entered by the company's clerk, tying ownership to a controlled register.

pages.description: Engraved certificate with an ornamental "STRAND BRIDGE" header and number at upper left. The body certifies that the holder is a proprietor entitled to one share, entered in the Registry Book and incorporated by Act of Parliament, given under the Common Seal at London, 30 December 1809. A gilt oval wafer seal sits at left; a footnote requires transfers to be entered by the clerk. Page 1 of 2.

Verso, headed "1810," bearing a handwritten ledger of calls received on the share. Successive entries note dates (including January, March, May and July) against money columns, recording the instalment payments made by the proprietor toward the subscribed capital. The lower sheet is blank. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Strand Bridge Company
notes: Strand Bridge Company later became Waterloo Bridge; reverse (goetzmann0913) shows call payment records 1810-1811

title: Derby Canal Company Share, Derby, 1793
description: This printed share certificate of the Derby Canal Company certifies the holder's title to one share in the undertaking, entered by name in the company's book and authenticated under the common seal. It records the enabling Act passed in the last session of Parliament for making and maintaining a navigable canal from the River Trent near Swarkston Bridge through the borough of Derby to Little Eaton, with a cut joining the Erewash Canal near Sandiacre and railways serving collieries at Denby, Horsley, and Smalley. Issued at the company's second General Assembly, held the seventh day of October 1793 at Derby, it belongs to the canal-mania of the early 1790s, when joint-stock subscriptions financed inland navigation to move coal and goods. A note warns that all transfers require duplicate deeds lodged with the clerk before any purchaser may claim profit or vote as a proprietor. An engraved red seal anchors the lower margin.
creator: Derby Canal Company
notes: Derby Canal Company share certificate, Share No. 320; certified to Matthew [illegible] of Derby; established under Act of Parliament for canal from River Trent through Borough of Derby to Little Eaton; issued at second General Assembly, October 7, 1793
transcription:
# DERBY CANAL

THIS Ticket, or Instrument, is to certify, that Matthew How of Derby in the County of Derby (Hatter)

is entitled to the share No. 320 in this undertaking; and that her name and proper addition, with the number of the said share, are fairly and distinctly entered in the book kept by "The Derby Canal Company" for that purpose, agreeable to the directions of the act passed in the last session of Parliament, intituled, "An Act for making and maintaining a Navigable Canal from the River Trent at or near Swarklton Bridge, to and through the Borough of Derby, to Little Eaton, with a Cut out of the said Canal in or near the said Borough, to join the Erewash Canal near Sandiacre; and for making Railways from such Canal to several Collieries in the parishes or liberties of Denby, Horfley, and Smalley, all in the county of Derby." Given under the Common Seal of the said Derby Canal Company, at their second General Assembly, held the seventh day of October, in the year One Thousand Seven Hundred and Ninety Three.

N.B. There must be Duplicates of all Transfers of Shares, each of them executed both by the Seller and the Purchaser, one part whereof must be delivered to the Clerk to the *Derby Canal Company*, and entered by him before the purchaser can have any share of the profits of this undertaking, or be entitled to vote as a proprietor.

**Notable markings:**
- Circular wax seal (red/ochre colored) affixed to lower right of document
- Handwritten annotation at top left corner (partially visible)
- Serial number: 320
- Date: 7 October 1793

title: Société Toulousaine du Bazacle Share
description: This is a bearer founder's share (part de fondateur au porteur) of the Société Toulousaine du Bazacle, a société anonyme with its registered seat at 10, Quai Saint-Pierre in Toulouse. The statutes, deposited before Maître Moyne, notary at Paris, were modified by the extraordinary assemblies of 18 March 1927 and 26 April 1928; the company's capital then stood at 13,150,000 francs, divided into 131,500 shares of 100 francs each. Created by the extraordinary general assembly of 26 April 1928, this instrument stands apart from that capital, conferring instead a 1/20,000th claim on the founders' advantages reserved by the statutes. Such parts de fondateur rewarded promoters without diluting subscribed capital, monetising goodwill rather than equity. The Bazacle company, raised upon Toulouse's ancient Garonne mill works, had by this date become an electricity concern. A restrained border of stylised foliage frames a document whose worth lay not in machinery but in a contractual share of surplus.
creator: Societe Toulousaine du Bazacle
notes: Societe Toulousaine du Bazacle traces its origins to a medieval water mill cooperative; one of the oldest joint-stock companies in history

title: Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation Specimen Share
description: A specimen share certificate of the Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation, incorporated under the laws of the State of New York. Printed in green with an ornate engraved border, it certifies ownership of full-paid, non-assessable shares of common stock without par value, drawn from an authorized capital of 300,000 shares. At its head an allegorical vignette shows reclining female figures attended by a winged genius, an emblem of prosperity and hospitality befitting a grand hotel enterprise. The certificate is transferable in New York or Boston, with The First National Bank of Boston as transfer agent. Marked SPECIMEN in red and numbered 000, it is an unissued printer's proof rather than a circulated share, documenting the financing of one of America's most celebrated hotels.

pages.description: The certificate face: an engraved green border frames an allegorical vignette of reclining women and a winged genius above the firm's name in ornate script. Text certifies non-assessable, no-par common stock, transferable in New York or Boston. Numbered 000, twice stamped SPECIMEN in red, with blank date and facsimile officers' signatures. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse, printed in black, quotes clauses of the certificate of incorporation governing share issuance, director self-dealing, and the absence of preference among common shares. Below, a blank assignment and power-of-attorney form for transferring the stock, with punched binding holes at the margin. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation
notes: SPECIMEN certificate; reverse (goetzmann0917) is blank transfer form
transcription:
# Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation Stock Certificate - Transcription

## Main Text:

**INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK**

**Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation**

THIS CERTIFICATE IS TRANSFERABLE IN NEW YORK, N.Y. OR IN BOSTON, MASS.
AUTHORIZED CAPITAL STOCK–300,000 SHARES WITHOUT PAR VALUE

**This is to Certify that** [blank] **is the owner of** [blank] **fully paid and non assessable shares of Limited Stock without par value of Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation transferable only on the books of the Corporation by the holder hereof in person or by duly authorized attorney upon surrender of this Certificate properly endorsed. The shares hereof shall be held subject to all the terms and conditions of the Certificate of Incorporation on file in the office of the Secretary of State and to the By-laws of the Corporation heretofore adopted and now in effect, a copy of which is on file herewith, and the holder is entitled to all the rights and privileges incident to the ownership of such shares.

**Witness the facsimile seals of the Corporation and the facsimile signatures of duly authorized officers.**

**Dated** [blank]

**Treasurer** [signature - marked SPECIMEN]

**President** [signature - marked SPECIMEN]

## Notable Markings:

- Serial number at top: **110537**
- Red text stamps: **SPECIMEN** (appears twice - below Treasurer and President signature lines)
- Large corporate seal in center bottom with text around perimeter (partially legible): **[SEAL OF THE ASTORIA CORPORATION]**
- "THE FIRST NATIONAL BANK OF BOSTON" printed vertically on left margin
- "THE COMMONWEALTH BANK OF BOSTON" printed vertically on right margin
- Ornate green border with decorative floral and geometric patterns throughout
- Empty green cartouches/boxes on either side of central engraving (for denomination or identifying numbers)
- Central allegorical engraving showing two figures seated on barrel or vessel

---

# Transcription

The Certificate of Incorporation, as amended, provides, among other things, as follows:

The Corporation may issue and sell its authorized shares without par value for such consideration as from time to time may be fixed by the Board of Directors.

"No contract or other transaction between the corporation and any other corporation shall be affected or invalidated by the fact that any one or more of the directors of this corporation is or are interested in, or is a director or officer or are directors or officers of, such other corporation, and any director or directors, individually or jointly, may be a party or parties to, or may be interested in, any contract or transaction of this corporation or in which this corporation is interested; and no contract, act or transaction of this corporation with any person or persons, firm or corporation, shall be affected or invalidated by the fact that any director or directors of this corporation is a party, or are parties to or interested in such contract, act or transaction, or in

any way connected with such person or persons, firm or association, and each and every person who may become a director of this corporation is hereby relieved from any liability that might otherwise exist from contracting with the corporation for the benefit of itself or of any firm, association or corporation in which it may be in anywise interested."

"No holder of the capital stock of this corporation shall be entitled as such as a matter of right, to subscribe for or purchase any part of any new or additional issue of stock of any class whatsoever whether now or hereafter authorized, or of securities convertible into stock of any class whatsoever, whether now or hereafter authorized, or whether issued for cash, property or services or by way of dividend, and all such rights are waived by each holder of the capital stock of this corporation."

---

Forwardly received, hereby sell, assign, and transfer unto

_____________________________________________________________________________

Shares of the Stock represented by the within Certificate, and do hereby irrevocably constitute and appoint _____________ Attorney, to transfer the said Stock, on the books of the within-named Corporation, with full power of Substitution in the premises.

Dated _________________ 19 _____

In Presence of:

_____________________________________________________________________________

_____________________________________________________________________________

_____________________________________________________________________________

THIS SPACE MUST NOT BE COVERED IN ANY WAY

---

**Notable markings:**
- Three black circular punch holes on the right side of the document
- Stamps/text visible on the right margin (partially illegible due to document condition)
- Signature lines in cursive script
- Date field marked "19____" indicating vintage document

title: United States Radium Corporation Common Stock
description: A common stock certificate of the United States Radium Corporation, a firm incorporated under Delaware law in 1917 and known for producing luminous radium paint. Engraved in brown with an ornate floral border, the certificate represents forty shares at a par value of one dollar each. It was issued in 1968 to two holders as joint tenants in Kenilworth, Illinois, and authenticated by the facsimile signatures of the treasurer and president together with the embossed corporate seal. The reverse carries the standard assignment form, here completed and guaranteed by a brokerage. The document records the company late in its corporate life, decades after the radium-dial industry that gave it notoriety.

pages.description: Engraved face of the certificate: brown ornamental border framing the title "United States Radium Corporation," Common Stock, par value $1.00 each, forty shares. Names two joint-tenant holders in Kenilworth, Illinois, dated May 6, 1968, with the embossed Delaware corporate seal and facsimile signatures of treasurer and president. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse of the certificate: the printed legend of ownership abbreviations (TEN COM, JT TEN, UNIF GIFT MIN ACT) above the assignment-and-transfer form. The form is completed and stamped with attorney instructions and a Dean Witter & Co. signature guarantee, dated August 1968. Page 2 of 2.

title: Silk-Reeling Partnership Share Certificate
description: A share certificate of a Japanese silk-reeling partnership (seishi gōmei kaisha), issued in Meiji 33 (April 1900). Printed in classical vertical script on tinted paper within an elaborate floral border, it certifies one share of 50 Yen held in the firm. The face declares the denomination, the share number, and the holder's name, then names the company's officers — president, vice-president, and directors — each authenticating the document with a red seal impression. An orange government revenue stamp is affixed at the upper right. The reverse carries the printed rules governing the share and a ruled ledger for recording future transfers, reflecting the registered-name regime by which ownership in such partnerships changed hands.

pages.description: Front of the certificate within a dense floral border with corner medallions. Large central text gives the denomination, "one sum, 50 Yen," beside the company name and share number (No. 44). An orange revenue stamp sits at upper right; the holder's name and the date, Meiji 33, fourth month, run down the side. President, vice-president, and directors are listed below, each over a red seal. A transfer-registration grid occupies the right. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse of the certificate. The right half prints the regulations governing the share in vertical columns — dividend entitlement, name registration, and the transfer procedure requiring the company seal. The left half is a blank ruled ledger headed by date columns (year/month/day) for recording successive ownership changes, with printed officer seal-lines beneath. Page 2 of 2.

title: Shirai Dry Goods Store Bond Certificate
description: A bond certificate (saiken shō) issued by the Shirai Dry Goods Store (Shirai Gofukuten) of Motomachi, Kobe, in January of Meiji 35 (1902). It records a loan of one hundred yen, one of two hundred such bonds raising twenty thousand yen for the expansion of the store's capital. The face is richly lithographed with peonies in ink-grey and violet, the encircled character 白 (Shirai) serving as the house mark, and the denomination set in bold stylised characters reading 金壹百圓. A two-sen revenue stamp is affixed, alongside a vermilion dated proprietor's seal and the signature of the store owner. The reverse sets out the conditions of repayment and interest.

pages.description: The certificate face. A peony spray at upper left and a large violet peony cluster at lower right frame the encircled house mark 白 and store name. A green two-sen revenue stamp sits top right beneath the number; the character 證 (certificate) stands at right. Centre reads 金壹百圓 (one hundred yen), 壹通 (one document), citing the issue at Kobe in Meiji 35 and the twenty-thousand-yen, two-hundred-bond series. A rectangular dated seal and round vermilion seal flank the proprietor's signature, 白井源兵衛, with address in Motomachi. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse, carrying the bond's terms in ruled vertical columns of brush-written clauses: the purpose of the twenty-thousand-yen capital expansion, schedules of repayment and interest, and dated provisions in Meiji years. At right, 所有者 (holder) names the bondholder, 端吉三郎殿. Vermilion seals appear at upper left and right. Page 2 of 2.

title: Kokueki Petroleum Association Share Certificate
description: A share certificate of the Kokueki Sekiyu Kumiai (国益石油組合, "National-Profit Petroleum Association"), a Meiji-era Japanese oil cooperative. The certificate represents a paid-up holding of fifty yen (一金五拾圓也) and entitles the holder to the association's rights and to a share of profits, as the central clause affirms. Issued to a named member, it carries the cooperative's title in a decorative arched banner, a cartouche of large vermilion company seals, a green government revenue stamp at upper right, and a row of directors' names each authenticated with a red seal impression. The reverse provides a transfer ledger and a dividend-payment table. The document reflects the cooperative form by which small Japanese petroleum producers pooled capital around the turn of the twentieth century.

pages.description: The certificate's face. An arched banner reads 国益石油組合證券; below, the sum 一金五拾圓也 (fifty yen) sits between a block of large vermilion association seals at left and a green government revenue stamp at upper right. A central clause grants the holder membership rights and a profit share. The recipient's name appears at right above a row of directors' names, each stamped with a red seal. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse. At left, a ruled transfer ledger with columns for date, transferor's and transferee's names and seals, and a witness line, bearing two red seal impressions and brushed entries. At right, a dividend-payment table listing successive distributions (第壱回 onward) in five- and ten-yen sums against Meiji-era dates, with a row of four vermilion seals beneath. Page 2 of 2.

title: Connecticut Land Company Certificate
description: A trust certificate of the Connecticut Land Company, issued at Hartford on 5 September 1795, recording one investor's fractional share in the Connecticut Western Reserve, the tract in present-day Ohio that the company had purchased from the State of Connecticut. The instrument vests a holder with an undivided proportion of the Reserve held by trustees under a Deed of Trust, transferable by assignment under the company's Articles of Agreement, and recordable before a magistrate. The accompanying leaves carry the registration docket and a later deed assigning the share, with consideration stated in dollars, illustrating how interests in unsurveyed frontier land circulated as negotiable property well before settlement.

pages.description: Engraved-headed certificate, No. 151, Hartford, Sept. 5th 1795. Certifies that Calvin Austin of Sheffield, County of Hartford, is entitled to Three Thousand twelve-hundred-thousandths of the Connecticut Western Reserve held by trustees John Caldwell, Jonathan Brace, and John Morgan under a Deed of Trust, transferable by assignment. Signed by all three trustees, marked "Trustees." Page 1 of 4.

Verso docket: "No. 151. Certificate from Trustees to Calvin Austin. Received Novr. 15. 1796 & entered in the Book of Records of the Connecticut Land Company for registering Certificates. Teste Eph. Root, Clerk of the Directors." Faint mirror-offset script bleeds through at left. Page 2 of 4.

Manuscript assignment: Calvin Austin, for consideration of Eight Thousand Dollars, conveys the annexed certificate for Three Thousand twelve-hundred-thousandths of the Western Reserve to James W. Galpin of Hartford. Dated 20 December 1796, signed and sealed by Austin, witnessed by two persons, and acknowledged before a justice. Page 3 of 4.

Verso of the assignment: largely blank, bearing a brief registration docket repeating that the deed was entered in the company's record book by the Clerk of the Directors. Heavy mirror-offset text from the facing page shows through. Page 4 of 4.

notes: Multi-document set: certificate (0926-0927) and transfer assignment (0928-0929); Western Reserve land in present-day Ohio

in certain Articles of Agreement entered into by the Persons com-

transferrable by Assignment, under Hand and Seal, witnessed by
two Witnesses, and acknowledged before any Justice of the Peace
in the State of Connecticut, or before a Notary Public, or a
Judge of the Common Pleas in any of the United States, and to
be recorded by the Clerk of the Board of Directors.

title: Rhode Island Land-Bank Mortgage Indenture, Newport and Providence, 1738
description: A printed-and-manuscript mortgage indenture securing a loan of colonial bills of credit under Rhode Island's public land-bank scheme. The engraved form, made in the Twelfth Year of His Majesty's Reign, Annoque Domini 1738, names the trustees John Wanton, George Goulding, Jahleel Brenton, Daniel Updike, John Gardner and James Sheffield, appointed by Act of Assembly to emit paper upon loan. Against bills advanced, the borrower mortgages a sixty-acre parcel in Providence township, bounded northerly by the land of James Williams and westerly by the highway, covenanting repayment with interest at five per centum in passable bills of public credit. Recorded at Providence on 20 April 1739 in the book for the sixth bank's mortgages by town clerk Richard Waterman, the deed exposes a chronic specie shortage that drove the colony to mint land itself into money, pledging real estate to underwrite the currency in circulation.
creator: (private parties)
notes: Colonial American land deed; partially illegible due to age and water damage

title: Share Receipt (Recepis) for the Garphytte Iron and Alum Works and Beata Christina Alum Work
description: A Dutch-language share receipt (Recepis No. 27) certifying a 1/1063 interest in the iron manufactory and alum works at Garphytte and in the alum work Beata Christina, both in Sweden, financed through a negotiation floated on the Amsterdam capital market. Issued at Amsterdam, 1 April 1776, it is given in exchange for a share in the outstanding loan, together with the arrears of unpaid interest coupons, and records that part of the underlying obligations had already been redeemed by lottery drawing ("by loting afgelost"). Printed under the directors' authority and registered before a notary, the document exemplifies how Dutch investors held and traded fractional stakes in foreign mining enterprises through Amsterdam's secondary market for negotiation paper.

pages.description: The engraved receipt itself: header "RECEPIS, wegens 1/1063 Aandeel" in the Garphytte iron manufactory and alum works and the Beata Christina alum work in Sweden. Letterpress body explains the exchange of share and coupon arrears and the lottery redemption. Dated Amsterdam, 1 April 1776; signed by two directors (G.L. Claeywanger and a second, [illegible]) and registered by notary Paulus [Huntum]. Page 1 of 3.

A sheet of detachable dividend receipts (quittances) tied to Recepis No. 27. A header notes that on surrender of this number new receipts will be issued in 1786, signed M.H. Coster, April 1776. Below run repeated printed acknowledgements of one year's distribution on the Garphytte and Beata Christina works, dated by descending year (1785, 1784, 1783…), each signed M.H. Coster. Page 2 of 3.

An unrelated French marine insurance policy bound with the item, headed "AU NOM DE DIEU AMEN." The underwriter assures Monsieur James Dormer 2,800 florins exchange money on his interest in the French corvette "la Byzantine," against the perils of the sea, friend or enemy. Made at Brussels, 13 April 1757, with subscription "Bon pour deux mille huit cens florins de change," signed F. Depestre. Page 3 of 3.

creator: Garphytte Iron and Alum Works / Beata Christina Alum Works
notes: Recepis No. 27; reverse (goetzmann0932) contains annual dividend/interest receipts

**GARPHYTTE,**
And in the ALUM-WORK
**BEATA CHRISTINA,**
IN SWEDEN.

Received in exchange for
one of the shares that were still outstanding, charged against one of the Negotiations
on the Iron-Manufactory and Alum-Works Garphytte, and on the Alum-
Work Beata Christina, and this together with its unpaid Interest-Coupons;
and the Holder of this instrument is, on that account, hereby recognized by the undersigned, by this
Receipt, as Owner of a 1/1063 Share in the Iron-Manufactory and
Alum-Works Garphytte, and in the Alum-Work Beata Christina, both together,
with everything that now belongs and pertains thereto — which Works have, by the Royal
Majesty's and the Realm's Swedish Court of Justice, been awarded to the Interested Parties in the said
Negotiations, for and toward satisfaction of the sum still owing thereon of ƒ 10,63000:- and the
unpaid Interest thereof, which ƒ 10,63000:- , together with a further ƒ 12000:- that, on account of one
of the said Negotiations, has meanwhile been redeemed by lottery drawing, had here been negotiated.

Done at Amsterdam, this *(handwritten:)* First of April 1776

*(handwritten director signatures, lower right:)*
[signature] G. B. Craeyvanger
[signature] A. van Heshuysen & Co. *(reading uncertain)*

**As Directors.**

*(lower left block:)*
This Receipt No. 27 is
duly recorded and
registered by me

*(handwritten signature:)* [signature] Paulus Huntum
Notary.

title: Unified Debt of Egypt Bearer Bond
description: A bearer bond of the Unified Debt of Egypt (Dette d'Égypte unifiée), issued under decrees of the Khedive dated 7 May and 18 November 1876 that consolidated Egypt's borrowings as European creditors pressed for control of its finances. Printed in parallel French and English columns, it bears 7 per cent interest on a denomination of 2,500 Francs / 100 Pounds Sterling, drawn from a total capital of 1,475,000,000 Francs, and is redeemable at par over sixty-five years through semi-annual drawings. A pale Egyptian-revival border of lotus columns, sphinxes and winged sun-discs frames the text, signed at Cairo for the Khedive's government and countersigned by Commissioner-Directors of the Caisse de la Dette Publique, among them E. Baring and E. de Blignières.

pages.description: The bond face, in facing French and English columns under a "7%" panel and an Egyptian-revival border of lotus columns and sphinxes. It recites the 1876 decrees, the 1,475,000,000-Franc capital, redemption at par in 65 years, and a quoted Commissioners' resolution "Fait au Caire, le 19 avril 1877." Denomination panels read Fr. 2,500 and £100; manuscript signatures and an attached coupon block sit below. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse, showing two large engraved counterfoils repeating the bond's terms for the £100 (English) and Fr. 2,500 (French) sides, alongside the surviving talon of coupons worth Fr. 87.50 / £3:10:0 each. A boxed "Amortissement / Amortization" note explains that no redemption table could be drawn, the sinking fund depending on the fifty-four remaining years and budget surpluses under the decree of 18 November 1876. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Egyptian Government (Caisse de la Dette Publique)
notes: Unified Debt of Egypt established 1876; coupon sheet attached; companion page (goetzmann0935) shows smaller denomination bonds and amortization table

title: Antwerp Marine Insurance Policy (1761) with an Amsterdam Survivorship Contract (1774)
description: A grouping of two unrelated risk-pooling instruments from the eighteenth-century Low Countries. The first leaf is a printed marine insurance policy of the Chambre Impériale et Royale d'Assurance d'Anvers, headed "AU NOM DE DIEU" beneath an engraved vignette of a storm-tossed ship; its blanks are completed in manuscript to cover a bale of merchandise aboard the vessel La Paix, dated and signed at Antwerp on 25 April 1761 with a wax seal. The remaining four leaves form a separate printed survivorship contract (Contract van Overleeving) under the device "De Tyd baard Roozen," issued at Amsterdam in 1774, setting out its conditions in numbered articles and ending in a manuscript registration certificate. Together they illustrate two strands of early speculative finance: maritime underwriting and life-contingent tontine subscription.

pages.description: Printed marine insurance policy of the Chambre Impériale et Royale d'Assurance d'Anvers, with an engraved roundel of a ship in heavy seas and the words "AU NOM DE DIEU." Manuscript entries insure a bale of goods on the vessel La Paix; signed by Thomas Joseph De Bie and Charles Crol, dated Antwerp 25 April 1761, with an embossed wax seal. Page 1 of 5.

Letterpress title and opening articles of a survivorship contract, headed "CONTRACT VAN OVERLEEVING, Letter G" under the motto "DE TYD BAARD ROOZEN." Dense two-column "Conditien en Voorwaarden" begin the numbered terms, followed by tables of subscription sums by age class. Page 2 of 5.

Continuation of the printed articles of the survivorship contract, two columns of closely set numbered clauses governing nominees, classes, payments, and administration. Wholly typographic, with no manuscript entries. Page 3 of 5.
Final printed columns of the contract's articles, closing the regulations; the lower text records its enactment at Amsterdam in 1774. Two columns of small type, no illustration. Page 4 of 5.

Manuscript registration certificate: the directors certify that Diderikus Tijk is entered on the small list, no. nine, in the age class 15 to 35, under letter G, for a subscription of f10 furnished by himself; dated Amsterdam 31 October 1774. Below are folded ledger leaves of annual handwritten entries. Page 5 of 5.

creator: Chambre Imperiale et Royale d'Assurance d'Anvers
notes: Chambre Imperiale et Royale d'Assurance d'Anvers, Marine Insurance Policy, 1811
transcription:
# Marine Insurance Policy of the Imperial and Royal Insurance Chamber of Antwerp (1761) — Transcription

## Original:

(Top left, printed with manuscript number:)
**N° _2766_**

(Engraved circular vignette, center top: a ship tossed on stormy seas, a castaway on the shore in the foreground, a flag lettered **S.A.**; legend around the rim:)
**SECURUS PELLAGI**
(engraver's signature beneath the medallion: *L. Fruytiers f.*)

(Printed heading, split to the left and right of the vignette:)
**CHAMBRE IMPERIALE ET ROYALE D'ASSURANCE D'ANVERS.**

**AU NOM DE DIEU.**

(Body — printed text with manuscript insertions shown in *italics*:)

**Nous soussignés Directeurs de la Chambre d'Assurance d'Anvers, reconnoissons & confessons avoir pris aux Risques, Perils & Fortunes de la dite Chambre de Vous** *Madame Ladoüa J: Dormer & C°* **pour le compte neutre de qui il appartiendra la Somme ci-après mentionnée sur** *Un Balot marq[ué] ADI N°.1 pour Compt[e] Espagnole Chargée à bord du Navire Hollandois La Paix Capitaine Izaac Geynstroom à Ostende Destiné pour Cadix*

(several blank ruled lines follow)

**lesquelles Marchandises Vous estimez ensemble à la Somme de** *Dix Sept Cens Cinquante Florins Argent de change* **de la quelle Estimation nous nous contentons, & en cas de Pillage ou Avaries, ils ne seront à notre charge que lorsqu'ils excéderont Trois pour Cent.**

**Lesquels Risques nous prennons à la charge de la Compagnie, depuis le Jour & Heure, que les dites Marchandises ont été embarquées à bord du dit Navire, jusqu'à tant que les dites Marchandises soient arrivées à** *Cadix* **à bon sauvement, & accordons que le dit Navire, faisant le dit Voyage, pourra naviguer avant & arriere, à dextre & à senextre côte: nous soumettans de courir Risque de tous Perils de Mer, de Feu, de Vents, d'Amis, d'Ennemis, de Lettres de Marque & Contre-marque, d'Arrets & Détentions des Rois, Princes & Seigneurs quelconques, & de Baraterie de Patron, Maître, Mariniers, & généralement de tous autres Perils, Fortunes ou Cas fortuits qui pourroient avenir en quelque maniere que l'on puisse s'imaginer, se mettant de tout en la place de l'Assuré pour le garantir & indemniser de toutes Pertes & Dommages qui pourroient arriver: & en cas de Perte ou Dommages aux dites Marchandises (ce que Dieu ne veuille) promettons & nous obligeons de payer ou rembourser au Porteur de cette Police, la perte ou dommage, à proportion de la Somme que nous avons assurée, à raison de Quatre-vingt Dix-huit pour Cent, & ce comptant après que nous serons dûement informés de la Perte ou Dommage arrivé aux dites Marchandises, & en tel cas donnons Pouvoir special au dit Assuré ou à son Commis pour tant à notre Dommage que Profit, travailler ou faire travailler à la Salvation des dites Marchandises, promettant en tout évenement de payer les Fraix & Dépens faits pour leur Salvation, soit qu'elles soient récouvrées ou non, ajoûtant foi & entier credit au Compte, & à la Personne ou Personnes qui auront fait les dits Fraix & Dépens. Confessons être payés du Prix de la présente Assurence par le dit Assuré à raison de** *1 ¾* **pour Cent.** *La Somme de Trente florins Douze sous & demi Argt de change*

**[Fait] de bonne Foi, sans fraude ni malengin: & en cas de Contestation entre nous pour le fait de la présente [Assurance] & dépendance d'icelle, nous conviendrons d'Arbitres pour juger de nos différens. [Fait au] Bureau de la Chambre d'Assurance à Anvers le** *25 avril 1761*

(Manuscript signatures, lower right:)
*Thomas Joseph De Bie*
*Charles Crol[e]*

(Embossed paper wax seal, lower left.)

(Left margin, printed vertically:)
**NB. La Compagnie n'entre point dans le Coulage des liquides, ni dans les Avaries sur toute sorte de Grains, Graine ou Semence, Poisson, Cuirs, Tabac, Sel, Fruis verts ou secs, à moins que l'Avarie ne soit grosse, ni sur Chanvre, Lin, Sucre, que lorsqu'elle excéde Dix pour Cent.**

(Note: the source image contains only this single leaf — the printed Antwerp marine policy. The Amsterdam survivorship contract (Contract van Overleeving) mentioned in the catalogue description is on other leaves not present in this image.)

---

## English Translation:

**No. 2766**

(Engraved medallion, legend:) **SECURE FROM THE SEA** (flag: **S.A.**; engraver: *L. Fruytiers*)

**IMPERIAL AND ROYAL INSURANCE CHAMBER OF ANTWERP.**

**IN THE NAME OF GOD.**

**We the undersigned Directors of the Insurance Chamber of Antwerp acknowledge and confess that we have taken upon the Risks, Perils & Fortunes of the said Chamber from You** *Madame Ladoüa J. Dormer & Co.* **for the neutral account of whomsoever it may concern, the Sum hereafter mentioned upon** *One Bale marked ADI No. 1 for Spanish account, loaded aboard the Dutch Ship La Paix, Captain Isaac Geynstroom, at Ostend, destined for Cadiz*

**which Merchandise You value altogether at the Sum of** *Seventeen Hundred and Fifty Florins in exchange money*, **with which Valuation we are content, and in case of Pillage or Average, they shall not be to our charge unless they exceed Three per Cent.**

**Which Risks we take upon the charge of the Company, from the Day & Hour that the said Merchandise was loaded aboard the said Ship, until such time as the said Merchandise shall have arrived at** *Cadiz* **in safety; and we agree that the said Ship, making the said Voyage, may sail forwards and backwards, to the right and to the left side: submitting ourselves to run the Risk of all Perils of the Sea, of Fire, of Winds, of Friends, of Enemies, of Letters of Marque & Counter-marque, of Arrests & Detentions of Kings, Princes & Lords whatsoever, and of Barratry of the Owner, Master, Mariners, and generally of all other Perils, Fortunes or fortuitous Cases that might occur in any manner one may imagine, placing ourselves entirely in the stead of the Insured to guarantee and indemnify him against all Losses & Damages that might occur: and in case of Loss or Damage to the said Merchandise (which God forbid) we promise and bind ourselves to pay or reimburse to the Bearer of this Policy the loss or damage, in proportion to the Sum we have insured, at the rate of Ninety-eight per Cent, and this in cash after we shall have been duly informed of the Loss or Damage befallen the said Merchandise; and in such case we give special Power to the said Insured or to his Clerk, as much to our Loss as Profit, to work or cause to be worked at the Salvage of the said Merchandise, promising in every event to pay the Costs & Expenses incurred for their Salvage, whether they be recovered or not, giving full faith and entire credit to the Account and to the Person or Persons who shall have made the said Costs & Expenses. We confess to be paid the Price of the present Insurance by the said Insured at the rate of** *1 ¾* **per Cent.** *The Sum of Thirty florins Twelve sous and a half in exchange money.*

**[Made] in good Faith, without fraud or deceit: and in case of Dispute between us concerning the matter of the present [Insurance] and its dependencies, we shall agree upon Arbitrators to judge our differences. [Done at the] Office of the Insurance Chamber at Antwerp, the** *25 April 1761*

*Thomas Joseph De Bie*
*Charles Crol[e]*

(Left margin:) **NB. The Company does not enter into the Leakage of liquids, nor into the Average on any kind of Grain, Seed or Seed-corn, Fish, Hides, Tobacco, Salt, Fruit fresh or dried, unless the Average be great; nor on Hemp, Flax, Sugar, except when it exceeds Ten per Cent.**

title: Egyptian Treasury Payment Order to Oppenheim & Cie, Legalized at Alexandria
description: A payment order of the Egyptian Treasury (“bon sur le ministère des finances”), drawn up under the Khedive Ismaʼil’s administration and addressed to the banking house J.H. Oppenheim Neveux & Cie. The order promises payment of £E 195,000, equivalent to 390 bourses, on 8 November 1866, signed by the Minister of Finance Mohamed Hafiz Pacha and dated 23 Zilhicce 1282 of the Muslim calendar. The item survives as a pair: the Arabic original, bearing the bankers’ registration stamp and ministerial seal, and a certified French translation. Both sheets carry the legalizations of the French Consulate General at Alexandria, dated 12 May 1866, authenticating the seal of the Finance Minister and the signature of the acting Foreign Affairs Minister, Raghib Pacha. The instrument records the short-term advances through which European bankers financed the Egyptian state on the eve of its debt crisis.

pages.description: The certified French translation of the order, opening “Bon sur le ministère des finances” and promising £E 195,000 (390 bourses) to MM. Oppenheim Neveux & Cie, signed Mohamed Hafiz Pacha, 23 Zilhicce 1282. Below, the First Dragoman B. Garnier certifies the translation and the Consul General legalizes it, with two oval consular seals, Alexandria, 12 May 1866. Page 2 of 2.

The Arabic original: a Treasury order (سند طلب على ديوان المالية المصرية) for £E 195,000 payable 8 November 1866 to Oppenheim & Cie. Bears the blue bankers’ stamp “H.O.N. & Co. No 1694,” a red exchange notation, Arabic ministerial seals, and French consular legalizations of the Finance and Foreign Affairs ministers’ marks, Alexandria, 12 May 1866. Page 1 of 2.

creator: French Consulate General, Alexandria
notes: French Consular Certificate of Egyptian Financial Document, Alexandria, 1866
transcription:
# Egyptian Treasury Payment Order to J.H. Oppenheim Nephews & Co., Certified French Translation Legalized at Alexandria (1866) — Transcription

## Original:

**Bon sur le ministère des finances.**

Du Trente Rabi 1er 83 correspondant au 8 novembre 1866, il sera payé par le trésor du ministère des finances à Messieurs J.H. Oppenheim neveux et Cie, ou à leur ordre la somme de £E 195,000 équivalent à Bourses 390 en espèce à l'exclusion de monnaie en cuivre et cela à valoir de leur crédit du ministère des finances.

Prix seulement la somme de cent quatre vingt quinze mille piastres tarif soit trois cent quatre vingt dix Bourses.

Le 23 Zilheggé 1282.

Le ministre des finances

(P.P.) mohamet Haffiz Pacha.

(certification of translation)

Pour Traduction Certifié conforme

Alexandrie le 12 Mai 1866.

Le premier Drogman.

*B. Garnier* (signature)

(consular legalization)

Vu pour légalisation de la signature ci-dessus de M<sup>r</sup> B. Garnier Premier Drogman du Consulat Général de France à Alexandrie d'Égypte.

Alexandrie le 12 Mai 1866.

P. l'Agent et Consul Général

Le Consul

[signature — illegible]

(left margin, written vertically) N° 8956 — Art. 71 — pour V[ou]s — [illegible]

(two blue circular consular seals, lower left and mid-left: **CONSULAT GÉNÉRAL DE FRANCE / A ALEXANDRIE**, with imperial eagle at center)

(structural notes: the amount abbreviation is written "£.E." before the figure "195,000"; the opening date "Trente Rabi 1er 83" is the Muslim-calendar date the writer equates to 8 November 1866 — best read as 30 Rabi' [al-awwal] (12)83; the internal ministerial date "23 Zilheggé 1282" = 23 Dhū al-Ḥijja 1282. A few words are given as best readings: the summation word "Prix", and the marginal registration note, are uncertain.)

---

## English Translation:

**Bond drawn on the Ministry of Finance.**

On the Thirtieth of Rabi' I [12]83, corresponding to 8 November 1866, there shall be paid by the treasury of the Ministry of Finance to Messrs J.H. Oppenheim Nephews & Co., or to their order, the sum of £E 195,000, equivalent to 390 Bourses, in specie, to the exclusion of copper coin, and this to be charged against their credit with the Ministry of Finance.

Say only the sum of one hundred ninety-five thousand piastres tariff, that is, three hundred ninety Bourses.

The 23rd of Dhū al-Ḥijja 1282.

The Minister of Finance

(By proxy) Mohamet Haffiz Pacha.

(certification of the translation)

Certified a true translation.

Alexandria, 12 May 1866.

The First Dragoman.

*B. Garnier* (signature)

(consular legalization)

Seen, for legalization of the above signature of Mr B. Garnier, First Dragoman of the Consulate General of France at Alexandria, Egypt.

Alexandria, 12 May 1866.

For the Agent and Consul General

The Consul

[signature — illegible]

(left margin, written vertically) No. 8956 — Art. 71 — for you — [illegible]

(two blue circular consular seals: CONSULATE GENERAL OF FRANCE / AT ALEXANDRIA, with imperial eagle)

title: Dutch Life-Annuity Negotiation Deed and Nominee List
description: A printed copy ("Copia") of a notarial deed establishing a Dutch life-annuity negotiation at Amsterdam, opening "Heden den Eerste May des Jaars Seventien Honderd Seven-en-Tachtig" (1 May 1787). The loan is directed by Pieter Stadnitski and Hendrik Vollenhoven and passed before notary Abraham van Beem; each subscribed share carries one thousand guilders, with interest paid against coupons due 1 November and 1 May. The income runs upon the lives of a long roster of named young women, whose survival governs each share's duration—a financing device that pooled capital while spreading mortality risk. Printed articles fix the terms; subscribers acquire numbered shares against receipt.

pages.description: Headed "Copia." Opening deed dated 1 May 1787 names directors Pieter Stadnyski and Hendrik Vollenhoven and notary Abraham van Berk, followed by a dense block of printed conditions. Below, a numbered roster of female nominees (from No. 30) begins, each a named life upon which a share rests. Page 1 of 3.

Continuation of the numbered nominee roster (about Nos. 60–86), listing further women's names in italic. The lower half resumes printed articles (Art. I, Art. II) governing the negotiation, quittances, interest, and the directors' obligations. Page 2 of 3.

Printed articles continue (Art. III–VII) on payment of coupons and reckoning, closing with the directors' and notaries' signatures, Amsterdam 5 May 1787. A printed receipt at foot acknowledges one thousand guilders for a share, coupons due 1 November and 1 May, dated 8 May 1787, with handwritten ledger entries. Page 3 of 3.

creator: Abraham van Berk, Hendrik Vollemioven
notes: 3-page document; see goetzmann0944.jpg (p.2/3), goetzmann0945.jpg (p.3/3)
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Copia

HEDEN DEN EERSTE MAY DES JAARS SEVENTEEN HONDERD SEVEN-EN TACHTIG.

Compareerden voor mij ABRAHAM VAN BEEMS, openbaar Notaris, bij den Hove van Holland geplaatst, en Getuigen onderleede,
De Heeren PIETER STADNITSKI en Mr. HENDRIK VOLLENHOVEN, als Directeuren,
En de Weduwe, Heeren ABRAHAM FOCK en ADRIAAN SCHARPF, als Commissarissen
In het Levens-Verzekeringe-Maatschappy.

Woonende de Heeren Comparanten binnen deze Stad, my Notaris bekend.

De Komeni overwegende dat door de twee Heeren eerfte Comparanten, ingevolge hun uitgegevene Bericjts, van diverfe Participanten, volgens gereeckende Receptlift is geneociteert, een Comptoir van Negocie maal Honderd Vyftig Duizend Guldens Hollandsch Courant, tengevolge zoodanige Jaarlyks Interest, als hier na zal worden gezegd, ten einde d-zelve ongenomen Hoeninzgen & empolementen, te don aanköop van Een Millison en Vyfmaal Honderd Duizend Livres tournoos, gevestigd op 50 Contracten, ieder van Livres 50,000: of 60 Contracten, ieder van Livres 50,000:, en de Capitaal van de Koninglyke Francke Lyrente Negotiatie van Miert 1781, rentende in pCt. op een Hould, dog tastet met de korting van de tiendte Penning, en dezelve in bescheiden hier geno'd der Interest zedert Primo July 1786, tot dan Stuyvers de Livres, daar en tegen schuldig en Zegels, vooral allerlei Interest, Copijen en Zegels, voorte alle onkosten van Provelie, Premie, en Courtage Cc, hier ook gonsanten, negens't ven gereckene hier Negotiatie, en voorts dat dezolve Contracten zouden onder warborg van 50 of 20 Jonge Jufvrouwen, alliestuschen de 3 en 8 Jaren, en gefield op de manen en in houvaing van de Heeren laaste Comparanten en van my Notaris, alles on soldage voelen—

en met alzalke precaution en voorzorgen, als by zeekere Acte op heeden ten overlllan van de Notaris GERRIT RODAN en Getuigen, door gemelde twee Heeren laaste Comparanten gepssfocrt, thaat vermeld, en welcke Acte, under de Dispensie van dezer Negotiatie, ter Ville van de Eigenaars en Geinteresseerdm, een Comptoir van my Notaris, als Notaris van dit Fonds, berustiende blylft.

Insgevolgs, en ter volleindiging van 't invoege voormeld uitgegevens Bericjts, zijn door de twee Heeren laaste Comparanten, binne deze Hand gefield; Zo is'k Notaris, ook bekenne ten Fine megenfeld, overige

Negen-en-Vyftig Contract, van Vyf-en-Twintig Duizend Livres Capitaal, ieder op een Hould, rentende op pCt., onder korting van de tolle Penning, van de Koninglyke Francke Lyrente Negotiatie, insgelijker bij Edict van Maart 1781. Stände alles op de Naamen van ABRAHAM SCHARPF en ABRAHAM VAN BEEM, genoemde en beleijd op de Lyven hier in aangeleijd. Zijnde reeds na de Contritie, een Contract van 2-50,000 - belegd een Lyve ELISABERTI VRIES, N°. 74, door haar overileven (voorgevoijdt bij het 14 February 1782, Waar van aan my Notaris de dood Cedul is geëxhibeert) Vervallen, zijnde de overige Contracten gemummert & geconstitueert als volgt:

N°. 50. Jurry Gertrude Elisabetn Vooet.
51. —— Constantia Gerardina Nobiet.
52. —— Cornelia Elizabeth Spilker.
53. —— Johanna Jofina van de Velde.

# Dutch Negotiation Contract Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Art. III.
Dat vermits volgens de gevoonde kans Reekening in ieder Actien Maanden, een hoofd, van de renten, kan en moet Sterven, als dan het verlies door ieder liefdeburen zou worden gedeeld, en naar dezen hoofd, in evenredigheijd met ½ pCo., ½ jaars vermrindering van Interest zou kunnen vergoed worden, zo als ook ieders, becom bij zijne keuring hoofd ook het vermeêrderen der Interest; met ½ pCo., ½ jaars goude toeslachen, waar der beide gevallen, het opgeteggen Capitaal, geneogzaam zoude blijven Accresfeefering op by overgalving van dit lastfte hoofd weder galyck aan de Inlang te zyn. De Heeren Eerste Compagnen hebben vermeinerd dit het eevenaudige middel tot herttel van 't goene tegen de Intrestenfoorders mogte, zoude zyn, dog vermits de veranderering van Interest egter juist niet om middelyk, op het Eerthe omregulering in den loop der Vereffeningen nodig is, zo sullen de Heeren Directeuren zhooren tot het eerste over te gaan de Gemeentesfoerdens Convocestenyen en als dan overgelegd worden of men de ½ pCo. zal blijven onderseeren, en dus de Negotiatie eene hoewel twijsom, oger en einde boornen, dan of men 't Interest jets verminnderen zal, om daardoor betatig te doen zyn, zullende Usthun in dit geval genoomen worden bij meerdeheid van Stemmen.
Gehandhaafd en de hoe grootheid van ieders Interest.

Art. IV.
Dat indien deze Negotiatie niet den uiteinde haar reguliere loop houd en de Interesfenste tot ½ pCo., differentia even bij Harta de doorgetting benoemd, bestaald zyn: de gezanmuddyke Gemeintesfoerdens de aangekocke Effecten verifleeten zullen of zodanige andere Schikingen beraa men zij op die Tijd door de Meerderheld zal grodgevonden worden.

Art. V.
De Directeuren zullen niet dan de onvermijdbare Kosten en 1 pCo., provisie van 't totanst der uitvangene Renten (en geyne van de uitsloopene Effectsten) in Reekening brengen.

Art. VI.
De Directeuren zullen bij de betaling der Coupons een exacte Reekening der Negotiatie ter Visfie en Examen van de Deelnemers Suppletieteren op dat een ieder initaat zal kannen zyn, hun beffier te bonoordelen.

Art. VII.
En eindelijk dat bij overlijden of affland van een der Heeren Directeuren, Commissarissen of Notaris van dit Fonds, zal worden gehanddeld conform de bepalingen bij 't uitgeegeven Bericht behoeeten, zooal van een Exemplaar aan de Groste dezer Geamneceerte is, om zig daar na in allen gevallen te regulieeren.

En verzoeken de Heeren Commissaristen hier van op te maaken Acte, de Groste dezes te bewaa hebben en van de Negotiatie volgends der rato van hun Aandeel in de Negotiatie Copien Authenclie dezer Acte wit te leeveren.

Gesloteken nimmen Aantekendain in Presentie van ABRAHAM JACOB BROES en JAN BORG-HORST als Getuigen. (Was Geteekend)

PIETER STADNITZSKI, HEINZ. VOLLEMIOVEN, JOB ADAM POOCH, A. SCHAHTZ, A. J. BROES, P. Borgh horst, en A. van BEEM Notaris.

In Amsteldam 5 Mei A°. 1787.

Accordeerd met de Minutte
[signature]

---

Deze Sub. N°. 597 door
mij Ondergetekende No-
taris en Protocolis aange-
tekend.
[signature]

**Ontvangen van**
een Somma van EEN DUYZEND GULDENS, voor een
Aandeel in deez' Negotiatie, waar van de Interesfien, conform de bijgevoegde Coupons, op primo November en primo Mei zullen betaald worden.

Amsteldam, 8 Mei A°. 1787.

[signature] als Directeuren.

---

**English Translation:**

Art. III.
That because according to the customary chance calculation in every stock month, one principal share of the annuity may and must die, as then the loss shall be shared by each beneficiary, and after this principal, in proportion with ½ pCo., ½ yearly reduction of Interest shall be able to be compensated, just as also each [person] by his choice principal also the increase of Interest; with ½ pCo., ½ yearly good addition, where in both cases, the stated Capital would remain sufficiently Accretion upon passing of this last principal again equal to the Input. The Gentlemen First Companies have determined that the equal means to remedy what against the Interest creditors might be, would be, because the change of Interest just not immediately, on the Earth of regulation in the course of the Settlements is necessary, so the Gentlemen Directors shall hear to take the first step to the Community creditors Convocation and then when delivered whether one the ½ pCo. shall remain agreed, and thus the Negotiation some doubt, more and end thereof, or whether one the Interest somewhat reduce shall, to thereby make payment be possible, providing you in this case understood be by majority of Votes.
Maintained and the proportionality of each one's Interest.

Art. IV.
That if this Negotiation not the utmost its regular course holds and the Interest to ½ pCo. difference even by Heart the accomplishment named, be paid: the arranged Community creditors the purchased Effects shall verify or such other arrangements about men by which on that Time by the Majority shall be found good.

Art. V.
The Directors shall except the unavoidable Costs and 1 pCo. provision of the total of the collected Rents (and none of the terminated Effects) in the accounting.

Art. VI.
The Directors shall by the payment of the Coupons an exact accounting of the Negotiation for Verification and Examination of the Participants deliver so that each individual shall be able to be, their better to understand.

Art. VII.
And finally that upon death or departure of one of the Gentlemen Directors, Commissioners or Notary of this Fund, shall be handled according to the determinations in the issued Notice stated, just as from an Example of the Community this, to regulate oneself after in all cases.

And request the Gentlemen Commissioners here of to make Act, the Community this to preserve and of the Negotiation according to their ratio of their share in the Negotiation Copies Authenticate this Act to deliver.

Signed with witness Signed in Presence of ABRAHAM JACOB BROES and JAN BORG-HORST as Witnesses. (Was Signed)

PIETER STADNITZSKI, HEINZ. VOLLEMIOVEN, JOB ADAM POOCH, A. SCHAHTZ, A. J. BROES, P. Borg horst, and A. van BEEM Notary.

In Amsterdam 5 May A°. 1787.

Agreed with the Minute
[signature]

title: Rancocus Toll-Bridge Company Share Certificate, New Jersey, 1793
description: This printed certificate, completed in manuscript, attests one share in the stock of the President, Managers, & Company of Rancocus Toll-Bridge, issued to Abel James and dated the eighteenth day of February 1793. It is witnessed by the signatures of the president, Joshua M. Wallace, and countersigned by the treasurer, William Coxe junior, beneath an impressed corporate seal at the left margin. The instrument belongs to the wave of toll-bridge and turnpike incorporations that spread through the early American republic, when state legislatures chartered private joint-stock companies to finance internal improvements that public treasuries could not. Spanning Rancocas Creek in Burlington County, New Jersey, the bridge promised to monetise the crossing through tolls, the shareholder's return resting on traffic rather than on any fixed rate of interest. Spare and typographic, the document forgoes vignettes, its authority vested wholly in printed text, signatures, and seal.
creator: President, Managers, & Company of Rancocus Toll-Bridge
notes: Rancocus Toll-Bridge, one share in the stock of the President, Managers & Company of Rancocus Toll-Bridge; holder: Abel James; February 18, 1793; signed by Joshua M. Wallace (President) and William Cooper Jun. (Treasurer); No. 29

title: Chinese Imperial Government Tientsin-Pukow Railway Loan Bond
description: A £100 bearer bond from the Imperial Chinese Government's 5% Tientsin-Pukow Railway Loan, a £5,000,000 sterling issue raised in London on 1st September 1908 to build the trunk line linking Tientsin (Tianjin) with Pukow (Pukou) on the Yangtze. Printed in orange and black, the certificate frames its English text within an ornate border crowned by an imperial dragon cartouche, while a parallel column of Chinese characters carries the corresponding undertaking. Authority is vouched by two vermilion calligraphic seals, a purple seal, and a manuscript signature; the issue is countersigned for the Chinese Central Railway, London. The document records foreign capital and engineering financing China's rail expansion under the late Qing.

pages.description: Front of the certificate. A dragon-topped cartouche heads the titles "Imperial Chinese Government" and "5% Tientsin-Pukow Railway Loan of £5,000,000 Sterling," with "Bond for £100" repeated in side roundels. English terms run beside a tall column of Chinese characters; below sit two red seals, a purple seal, and a signature, countersigned for the Chinese Central Railway, London, 1st September 1908. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse, printed in orange. At left a locomotive vignette surmounts a repeat of the loan title and "£100." A central redemption table lists the years and drawn amounts for the loan's amortisation, and a framed panel at right sets out the loan agreement and conditions in dense English text. Page 2 of 2.

title: Kousanie Tea Company Share Certificate
description: A share certificate of the Kousanie Tea Company, Limited, issued at Kousanie in northern India and dated 22nd April 1874. The company was registered under Act XIX of 1857 of the Legislative Council of India, with a capital of Rupees 3,45,000 divided into 3,450 shares of Rs 100 each. The certificate registers the holder as a proprietor entitled to the benefits, powers, and privileges attaching to the share, transferable according to the company's Deed of Settlement. An ornate display banner names the firm across the head, repeated down the left margin, while a purple one-anna Indian revenue stamp bearing the profile of Queen Victoria fixes the document within the fiscal apparatus of British India. Manager and Managing Director authenticate it by hand. The certificate attests to the flow of British capital into Himalayan tea cultivation during the industry's rapid mid-Victorian expansion.

pages.description: Recto of the certificate. An ornate banner reads "KOUSANIE TEA COMPANY," LIMITED across the top, with the name repeated vertically down the left border. Printed lines cite Act XIX of 1857 and the capital of Rs 3,45,000 in 3,450 shares of Rs 100. A purple one-anna revenue stamp with Queen Victoria's profile sits at upper right. Manuscript entries name the holder and date it Kousanie, 22nd April 1874; signed by Manager and Managing Director. Page 1 of 2.

Verso, the transfer record. A printed note states all transfers are effected only by a separate Deed, with Transfer Register No. and date fields, here filled 23rd April 1875 and signed by the Manager. A lengthy manuscript entry records the transfer to the Revd. Henry Francis Martin and the Venerable William Capeck, Archdeacon of Kilmore. Remaining ruled transfer lines are blank. Page 2 of 2.

title: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania Share Transfer, Philadelphia, 1795
description: A handwritten deed of transfer by which Matthew Lawler assigns ten shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania to Peter Stephen du Ponceau, executed at Philadelphia and dated the twenty-ninth day of August 1795, signed "Mat. Lawler" and attested by the witness John Lewis. The brief, formulaic text is a stock-assignment instrument rather than an engraved share certificate, recording the conveyance of an existing holding. It speaks to the rapid maturing of corporate finance in the early American republic: chartered in 1794, the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania was among the first joint-stock marine and fire insurers, and its freely transferable shares circulated as investable property in Philadelphia, then the young nation's financial capital. The plain ledger hand, unadorned by any printed device, reflects an era when such transfers were still personal, scribal acts.

title: Liverpool Colquitt Street Tontine Share
description: An engraved share certificate in the Liverpool Colquitt Street Tontine, certifying that Frances Harmand Hanly and Richard Stewart Hanly are proprietors of Share No. 29, duly entered in the Register Book of the Proprietors. The nominee on whose life the share depends is named as Frances Harmand Hanly, daughter of Richard Hanly of Liverpool, banker; the share is held by transfer from Richard Hanly. Dated Liverpool, 7 October 1807, and entered by Charles Bird. A tontine pooled subscribers' capital and distributed income among surviving nominees, the last survivor taking the whole, so the choice of a young nominee's life was a calculated wager on longevity. The cautionary note on transfer, warning purchasers to verify the register before paying, registers the instrument's speculative secondary market. The restrained typographic frame is wholly utilitarian, the document's authority resting on text and signature rather than ornament.

title: Compagnie des Associés aux travaux du midi de Lyon, Share for the Pont de la Mulatière (Lyon)
description: An engraved share certificate (action) of 500 livres in the Compagnie des Associés aux travaux du midi de Lyon, the venture charged with building the bridge of La Mulatière at Lyon. The share yields thirty livres of annual interest and a hundred-livres premium at redemption, returning 600 livres if repurchased. Its security is layered: charged specifically on the bridge's toll receipts (the péage) and generally on company assets, with express subrogation to a 1,500,000-livre loan raised at Genoa and a 350,000-livre Crown advance. A sinking fund, opening 1 January 1790, retires twenty shares yearly by lot until the issue—capped at 880 shares—is extinguished. Signed at Lyon by the company's director, treasurer, and commissioners, the instrument shows a provincial public-works company financing a toll bridge by binding subscribers' returns directly to its future receipts. The plain typographic frame, free of allegory, befits a sober administrative paper of the late Ancien Régime, its authority resting on terms and signatures rather than ornament.
creator: Compagnie des Associes aux travaux du Pont de la Mulatiere
notes: Compagnie des Associés aux travaux du Pont de la Mulatière (Lyon bridge company), Action de 500 livres tournois; produces 30 livres annual interest with 100 livres premium on redemption; authorized by Merchants of Lyon; No. 106

SUR LE PONT DE LA MULATIERE.

*[Header ornament]*

Action de cinq cents livres sur le pont de la Mulatiere, produisant trente livres d'intérêt, avec une prime de cent livres lors du remboursement.

Nous, souffignés, Directeur, Tréforier, & Commiffaires de la Compagnie des Affociés aux travaux du midi de Lyon, duement autorisés à cet effet, reconnoiffons avoir reçu de M. la fomme de cinq cents livres, pour être employée aux travaux du pont de la Mulatière, fous les clauses & conditions.

1°. Que la préfente action eft fpécialement affectée fur les produits du péage dudit Pont.

2°. Que, fans deroger à cette hypotheque particuliere, le porteur jouira d'une hypotheque générale fur les biens de la Société, avec fubrogation expresse fur lefdits biens aux droits, privileges & hypotheques résultant, soit de l'emprunt de Gênes, montant à 1,500,000 livres, soit de l'avance de 350,000 livres, faite par Sa Majesté à la Compagnie, qui, ne pouvant être affranchie de ces deux créances qu'à la charge du Pont, déclare que sa libération fera l'ouvrage de ceux qui fourniront des fonds pour le construire, & qui, par conséquent, auront les mêmes droits sur l'entreprise que les anciens créanciers avoient acquis.

3°. Que ladite action produira trente livres d'intérêt, dont le premier paiement fera fait par M. le préfident de Fleurieu, tréforier de la Compagnie, dans un an, à dater de ce jour; le second, à la même époque de l'année fuivante, & ainsi de fuite.

4°. Qu'au cas que la Compagnie rachete ladire action, comme elle s'en réserve la faculté, le porteur en fera remboursé fur le pied de 600 l., c'est-à-dire, avec une prime de 100 l. en capital.

5°. Que, dans dix années au plus tard, à compter du 1.er Janvier 1790, il fera procédé chaque année, & par la voie du fort, au remboursement de vingt actions, jusqu'à l'extinction totale des actions qui auront été vendues, & dont le nombre n'excèdera pas 880.

A LYON;

*[Signatures below]*

---

## English Translation:

**Action for five hundred livres on the bridge of Mulatiere, producing thirty livres of interest, with a premium of one hundred livres upon redemption.**

We, the undersigned, Director, Treasurer, & Commissioners of the Company of Associates for the works of the south of Lyon, duly authorized for this purpose, acknowledge having received from M. the sum of five hundred livres, to be employed in the works of the Mulatière bridge, under the following clauses and conditions.

1°. That this present action is specially charged upon the revenue from tolls of the said Bridge.

2°. That, without derogating from this particular mortgage, the holder shall enjoy a general mortgage on the assets of the Company, with express subrogation on said assets to the rights, privileges & mortgages resulting, either from the Genoese loan, amounting to 1,500,000 livres, or from the advance of 350,000 livres, made by His Majesty to the Company, which, being unable to be freed from these two debts except at the charge of the Bridge, declares that its liberation shall be the work of those who shall provide the funds to construct it, & who, consequently, shall have the same rights on the enterprise as the former creditors had acquired.

3°. That the said action shall produce thirty livres of interest, the first payment of which shall be made by M. the president of Fleurieu, treasurer of the Company, within one year from this date; the second, at the same time in the following year, & so on.

4°. That in the case that the Company redeems the said action, as it reserves the faculty to do so, the holder shall be reimbursed at the rate of 600 l., that is to say, with a premium of 100 l. in capital.

5°. That, within ten years at the latest, commencing from the 1st of January 1790, there shall be proceeded each year, & by means of the lottery, to the redemption of twenty actions, until the total extinction of the actions which shall have been sold, & of which the number shall not exceed 880.

AT LYON;

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** No. 106 (handwritten in upper left margin)
- **Reference Numbers:** £506 and £506 (marked in left margin)
- **Signatures:** Four handwritten signatures at bottom:
  - Chard de [Fleurieu] (first signature)
  - George Sartore (second signature - partially legible)
  - Sartore (third signature)
  - Laurencin (fourth signature)
- **Date:** ca. 1780s (document context indicates dating)
- **Decorative ornaments:** Horizontal rule at top and bottom of title section

title: Mississippi Union Bank Bond
description: A $2,000 bond of the State of Mississippi, styled a "Five Per Cent Loan," issued for the benefit of the Mississippi Union Bank. The State promises to pay the sum in current money of the United States to the order of the bank's President, Directors and Company on the fifth day of February 1850, with interest at five per cent per annum payable half-yearly on the first of May and November. Signed and sealed at Jackson in June 1838, with a sheet of interest coupons still attached. The instrument records Mississippi's pledge of public credit to capitalize a state-chartered planters' bank during the speculative cotton boom that preceded the Panic of 1837's aftermath; the State would repudiate such Union Bank bonds in the early 1840s. The engraved vignette pairs a seated classical figure of Commerce with a feathered Native American amid corn and cotton bales above a river landscape, an allegory of the agrarian wealth offered as security.
creator: State of Mississippi / Mississippi Union Bank

title: Mississippi Union Bank Bond (Reverse)
description: This is the reverse of a sterling bond of the Mississippi Union Bank, bearing the engraved undertaking by which the President and Directors designate the Agency of the Bank of the United States in the city of London as the place of payment of the within bond and interest. The instrument is transferred to bearer for value received, declared equal to four hundred and fifty pounds sterling, and the payment guaranteed; it is subscribed by S. Gwin as cashier and H. G. Runnels as president. A red overprint identifies it as a "Mississippi State Bond," No. 562, £450, redeemable February 5th, 1850, and below runs a sheet of attached interest coupons, each for eleven pounds five shillings sterling payable at the agency of the Bank of the United States, London. Issued to fund a state-chartered bank, the bond shows Mississippi raising capital in the London market on the eve of the panic and repudiation that would soon make such state debts notorious. The face is plain and clerical, its authority resting on signatures and a guarantee rather than on ornament.
creator: State of Mississippi / Mississippi Union Bank

title: Forty Wall Street Corporation First Mortgage Sinking Fund Gold Bond
description: Specimen $500 First Mortgage, Fee and Leasehold, 6% Sinking Fund Gold Bond, Series of 1958, issued by the Forty Wall Street Corporation, builder and owner of the Manhattan Company Building in New York. Dated 1929 and maturing November 1, 1958, the bond carries semi-annual interest each May and November, secured against both the freehold and the leasehold of the skyscraper rising at 40 Wall Street. Engraved in green with an allegorical vignette and ornate borders, it embodies the speculative tower-building of the late 1920s, when Lower Manhattan's race for height was financed through long-dated mortgage bonds. Defaced and cancelled, stamped SPECIMEN, with attached coupon sheet and registration provisions.

pages.description: The bond's face: a central allegorical vignette of classical figures and cherubs between two "$500" panels beneath a "United States of America" arch. Engraved title, terms, signature blocks and seal follow. Stamped SPECIMEN, overprinted in red "DEFACED & CANCELLED." Page 1 of 3.

Reverse and registration side. A left panel ruled for date of registration, registered owner and registrar's signature; a central ornamental panel restating the bond's terms and the Guaranty Trust Company trusteeship; a right panel of engraved assignment and registration provisions. SPECIMEN. Page 2 of 3.

A full sheet of semi-annual interest coupons in a grid, each bearing a small vignette, value and payment date running May and November from 1931 through May 1958. Numbered 00000 and overprinted SPECIMEN. Page 3 of 3.

title: Chanin Building First Mortgage Leasehold Bond
description: A $100 First Mortgage Leasehold 5% Bond of the Lexington Ave. & 42nd St. Corporation, secured on the Chanin Building on the west side of Lexington Avenue between 41st and 42nd Streets in Manhattan. Engraved in blue, the certificate centers a vignette of the Art Deco skyscraper flanked by ornamental medallions bearing "$100," with the corporate seal of 1934 below. Issued as of August 1, 1945 and due September 1, 1965, the bond reflects the financial reorganization of one of New York's landmark office towers, the obligation tied not to fee ownership but to a leasehold interest. Interest is payable semiannually through The Continental Bank & Trust Company of New York as trustee. The specimen is stamped "CANCELLED."

pages.description: The bond's face. A central engraving of the Chanin Building rises between two columns and decorative medallions reading "$100." Below, the granting and promise-to-pay clauses set out the $100 principal, 5% rate, and 1965 maturity, with reorganization terms. Attested by signature and the 1934 corporate seal; stamped "CANCELLED." Page 1 of 2.

The reverse, in three panels. At left a Voting Trust Certificate for Class A Common Stock under a five-year voting trust agreement, signed by voting trustees Howard S. Cullman and Alfred J. Callahan. The center stub repeats the bond's terms and carries the trustee's certificate of authentication. At right, a blank assignment form with signature-guarantee note. Page 2 of 2.

title: Times Square 46th Street Building First Mortgage Gold Bond
description: A $1,000 First Mortgage Leasehold Six Per Cent Sinking Fund Gold Bond issued by the 1556 Broadway Corporation, dated as of the first day of April, 1928, and falling due April 1, 1953. The obligation is secured by a leasehold estate in the land and the Times Square 46th Street Building at Nos. 1556-1560 Broadway and 157-165 West 46th Street in Manhattan. Principal and the semi-annual interest, payable April 1 and October 1, are promised in United States gold coin at the office of the trustee, the Interstate Trust Company. Framed by an engraved foliate border in green, the certificate registers the speculative optimism of late-1920s Times Square real estate, financed against a ground lease rather than outright ownership.

pages.description: The bond's face, in green within an engraved border. A central vignette heads the title "Times Square 46th Street Building, 1556 Broadway Corporation," with $1,000 panels at each side. Dense text sets the 6% rate, gold-payment and tax covenants, dated April 1, 1928; blank Secretary and President signature lines below. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse and stub: at left a blank registration panel with registrar's signature lines; at center a coupon-style summary repeating the title, $1,000 value, due date April 1, 1953, interest dates, and the Interstate Trust Company trustee certificate; at right an ornate engraved guilloche column. Page 2 of 2.

## BOND TEXT (Main Body)

1556 BROADWAY CORPORATION, a corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of New York (hereinafter called "the Corporation"), for value received, acknowledges itself indebted and hereby promises to pay to the bearer hereof, or if this bond be registered, to the registered holder hereof, on the first day of April, 1963 (unless this bond shall have been called for previous redemption as provided herein, and in the indenture hereinafter mentioned),

and to pay interest thereon at the rate of six per cent. (6%) per annum, payable semi-annually on the first day of April and the first day of October in each year, and such interest hereon until maturity hereof (unless this bond shall have been called for previous redemption) being payable only upon surrender of the interest coupons hereto attached as they severally mature.

This bond is one of a series of bonds issued under and pursuant to an indenture dated the principal office of Interstate Trust Company, in the Borough of Manhattan, City and State of New York, or at the option of the bearer or registered holder hereof, in the manner and upon the conditions and subject to the limitations provided in the indenture herein, any taxes which, under any present or future law of any State, Territory or possession of the United States of America, or of the District of Columbia, the bearer or holder hereof, as a resident of any said state, territory or possession, is, or shall be, obligated to pay by reason of the ownership hereof, upon or on account of the principal or interest of this bond, shall be paid by the Corporation, as an additional sum to the dollar of the principal amount hereof in any year, provided that application for such reimbursement shall be made to the Corporation as provided in the provisions above referred to by [illegible].

For the payment of any such tax, or in case default be made in the payment of any interest hereon as it First Mortgage Leasehold Sinking Fund Gold Bonds, all of like tenor (except as to designated number and denomination), issued and to be issued in an aggregate principal amount equal to Seven Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($7,400,000) at any time outstanding, under the indenture dated the 1st day of April, 1928 (hereinafter referred to as the "Indenture"), and duly executed and delivered, mortgaging, transferring and conveying unto Irtus [Trustees?] of Columbia University in the City of New York, as lessor, to Bethlehem Engineering Corporation, as lessee, dated February [date], [illegible], mortgaging, transferring and conveying unto [Trustees?] of Columbia University in the City of New York as lessor to Bethlehem Engineering Corporation, as lessee, by assignment dated May 20, 1924, recorded in the Office of the Register of New York County on July 11, 1924 as Liber 3456 [illegible]; and also by leases from the Trustees of Columbia University, in the City of New York, as lessor, to Bethlehem [Engineering Corporation?], as lessee, all the real property situate, lying and being in the Borough of Manhattan, City and State of New York, and more particularly described in the mortgages aforesaid, together with all the improvements thereon, and all the appurtenances thereto, all of which real property is particularly described in the mortgage, and the rights and interests thereon is payable at the office of agency of the Corporation, in the Borough of Manhattan, City and State of New York, or at the option of the bearer or registered holder hereof, at the principal office of Interstate Trust Company, in the Borough of Manhattan, City and State of New York.

[The text continues with extensive legal terms regarding the sinking fund, redemption conditions, and bond holder rights. Much of this middle section is overprinted with red text that is largely illegible in the scan]

[Continuing from legible portions:]

...This bond shall pass by delivery and registration by the Trustee hereto until such registration by being in like manner transferred to bearer and thereupon transferability by delivery shall be restored, but this bond may again and from time to time be registered and transferred to bearer as before. The registered holder hereof shall be entitled to the benefit of the lien created by the mortgage hereinabove mentioned, which shall continue to be transferable by delivery, and payment to the bearer thereof shall discharge the Corporation with respect to the principal and interest thereon.

1556 BROADWAY CORPORATION.
